topics.title: Digital Image Fundamentals - Image Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Digital Image Fundamentals - Image Representation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Digital Image** is a two-dimensional discrete function $f(x, y)$ where $x$ and $y$ are spatial (plane) coordinates, and the amplitude of $f$ at any pair of coordinates $(x, y)$ is called the **intensity** or **gray level** of the image at that point. Mathematically, the image is defined as: $$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$ where: - $i(x, y)$ = illumination component (amount of source light incident on the scene) — ranges from $0$ to $\infty$ - $r(x, y)$ = reflectance component (amount of light reflected by the object) — ranges from $0$ to $1$ Therefore, the intensity $f(x, y)$ lies in the range: $$0 < f(x, y) < \infty$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Definition**: When both the spatial coordinates $(x, y)$ and the intensity values $f$ are finite, discrete quantities, the image is called a **Digital Image**. Each element of a digital image — having a unique location $(x, y)$ and a value $f$ — is called a **pixel** (short for *picture element*). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation) Think of a digital image as a **mosaic wall** or a **chessboard made of tiny light bulbs**: | Real-World Object | Digital Image Equivalent | |---|---| | Wall made of small tiles | Image made of pixels | | Each tile's color & brightness | Each pixel's gray level | | Total number of tiles in width × height | Image dimensions $M \times N$ | | Number of distinct paint shades available per tile | Gray-level resolution $L = 2^k$ | > [!NOTE] > **Concept Clarification**: When you take a photo with your smartphone, the camera sensor (CCD/CMOS) breaks the scene into millions of tiny squares (pixels). Each pixel captures one number representing brightness (and three numbers for color: R, G, B). The collection of all these numbers, arranged in a 2D grid, **is** the digital image. ## 1.3 Classification of Images by Type > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight** — Three Image Categories: 1. **Binary Image** — Contains only two gray levels (0 and 1, i.e., black & white). Storage: $1$ bit/pixel. 2. **Gray-Scale Image** — Contains $L$ intensity levels, typically $L = 2^k$ where $k$ is the number of bits per pixel. Common choice: $k = 8$, giving $L = 256$ levels. 3. **Color Image** — Composed of three 2D planes (R, G, B), each a gray-scale image. Storage: $24$ bits/pixel (8 bits per channel). ## 1.4 Image as a Matrix A digital image of size $M \times N$ is represented as a 2D matrix: $$ f(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} f(0,0) & f(0,1) & \cdots & f(0,N-1) \\ f(1,0) & f(1,1) & \cdots & f(1,N-1) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ f(M-1,0) & f(M-1,1) & \cdots & f(M-1,N-1) \end{bmatrix} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Convention**: The coordinate origin $f(0,0)$ is conventionally placed at the **top-left** corner of the image. The $x$-axis points **downward** and the $y$-axis points to the **right** (this differs from standard Cartesian math, but is the universal digital image convention). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Image Acquisition Pipeline (Sampling & Quantization) To convert a continuous real-world scene into a digital image, two fundamental operations are performed: ### Step 1: Sampling (Digitization of Coordinates) - The continuous spatial domain is discretized into a finite grid of $M$ rows and $N$ columns. - Sampling rate determines the **spatial resolution**. ### Step 2: Quantization (Digitization of Intensity) - Each continuous intensity value is mapped to one of $L$ discrete gray levels. - Quantization step size determines the **gray-level resolution**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KTU Sampling-Quantization Theorem**: The total number of bits $b$ required to store a digital image of size $M \times N$ with $k$ bits per pixel is: > $$b = M \times N \times k$$ ## 2.2 Spatial Resolution **Spatial Resolution** = A measure of the **smallest discernible detail** in an image, expressed as: - **Pixels per unit distance** (e.g., dots per inch, DPI), or - **Total number of pixels** $M \times N$ (line pairs per unit distance) Higher spatial resolution $\Rightarrow$ smaller pixel size $\Rightarrow$ finer detail. ## 2.3 Gray-Level (Intensity) Resolution Defined as the **smallest discernible change in gray level**, denoted by: $$\Delta = \frac{L - 1}{2^k - 1} = 1 \text{ (in the standard integer scale)}$$ The number of gray levels is $L = 2^k$ where $k$ is the number of bits/pixel. ## 2.4 The Nyquist Sampling Criterion > [!IMPORTANT] > To faithfully reconstruct a periodic pattern, the sampling rate must be **at least twice** the highest frequency present in the image. $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ If sampling is below the Nyquist rate, **aliasing** (e.g., moiré patterns, jagged edges) occurs. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Image Function | $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ | Illumination $\times$ Reflectance | | 2 | Total Bits | $b = M \times N \times k$ | bits | | 3 | Gray Levels | $L = 2^k$ | dimensionless | | 4 | File Size (Bytes) | $\text{Size} = \dfrac{M \times N \times k}{8}$ | bytes | | 5 | File Size (KB) | $\text{Size (KB)} = \dfrac{M \times N \times k}{8 \times 1024}$ | kilobytes | | 6 | Compression Ratio | $C_R = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Nyquist Criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | samples/unit | | 8 | Aspect Ratio | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{M}{N}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Pixel Density (DPI) | $\text{DPI} = \dfrac{\text{pixels}}{\text{inch}}$ | dpi | | 10 | SNR (Image Quality) | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\text{Peak Signal}}{\text{Noise RMS}}\right)$ | dB | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility**: These formulas are the foundation of medical imaging (MRI/CT), satellite remote sensing, camera sensor design, and video compression standards (JPEG, MPEG). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Medical Imaging** — MRI/CT scans use $512 \times 512$ or $1024 \times 1024$ images with $k = 12$ bits (4096 gray levels) to detect subtle tissue variations. 2. **Satellite Imaging** — Landsat-8 captures $185$ km $\times$ 185 km scenes at $30$ m resolution using $11$-bit quantization. 3. **Mobile Cameras** — A 12 MP smartphone camera ($M \times N = 4000 \times 3000$) with $k = 10$ bits stores a single raw frame in $b = 4000 \times 3000 \times 10 = 120 \times 10^6$ bits $\approx 14.3$ MB. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solved Problems ## 3.1 Solved Problem 1: Image Storage Calculation **Problem**: A gray-scale image of size $1024 \times 1024$ pixels uses $8$ bits per pixel. Calculate: (a) The total number of bits in the image. (b) The total number of bytes. (c) The number of distinct gray levels. ### Solution: **Part (a): Total bits** Using the master storage formula $b = M \times N \times k$: $$ b = 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 $$ $$ b = (2^{10}) \times (2^{10}) \times 8 = 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^3 = 2^{23} $$ $$ \boxed{b = 8{,}388{,}608 \text{ bits} = 8 \text{ Mbits}} $$ **Part (b): Total bytes** $$ \text{Bytes} = \frac{b}{8} = \frac{8{,}388{,}608}{8} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Bytes} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB (exactly)}} $$ **Part (c): Number of gray levels** $$ L = 2^k = 2^8 $$ $$ \boxed{L = 256 \text{ gray levels}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Solved Problem 2: Multi-Channel (Color) Image Storage **Problem**: A color image of size $640 \times 480$ uses 8 bits per color channel (RGB). Compute the total file size in KB. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Bits per pixel (color)**: A color image has 3 channels, each $k = 8$ bits. $$ k_{\text{total}} = 3 \times 8 = 24 \text{ bits/pixel} $$ **Step 2 — Total bits**: $$ b = M \times N \times k_{\text{total}} = 640 \times 480 \times 24 $$ $$ b = 307{,}200 \times 24 = 7{,}372{,}800 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to KB**: $$ \text{Size (KB)} = \frac{7{,}372{,}800}{8 \times 1024} = \frac{921{,}600}{1024} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Size} = 900 \text{ KB}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Solved Problem 3: Finding Image Dimensions from File Size **Problem**: An uncompressed gray-scale image occupies 256 KB of memory and uses 8 bits per pixel. Determine the image dimensions if it is square. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Convert KB to bits**: $$ b = 256 \text{ KB} \times 8 \times 1024 \text{ bits/KB} $$ $$ b = 256 \times 8192 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2 — Use master formula** (square image: $M = N$): $$ b = M^2 \times k \Rightarrow 2{,}097{,}152 = M^2 \times 8 $$ $$ M^2 = \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{8} = 262{,}144 $$ **Step 3 — Take the square root**: $$ M = \sqrt{262{,}144} = \sqrt{2^{18}} = 2^9 = 512 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Image dimensions} = 512 \times 512 \text{ pixels}} $$ --- ## 3.4 Solved Problem 4: Nyquist Sampling Verification **Problem**: A checkerboard pattern has a fundamental frequency of $400$ line pairs/mm. Calculate the minimum sampling rate (in samples/mm) required to reconstruct it faithfully. ### Solution: Applying the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem: $$ f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max} $$ $$ f_s \geq 2 \times 400 $$ $$ \boxed{f_s \geq 800 \text{ samples/mm}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation**: Any sampling rate below 800 samples/mm will produce aliasing — the checkerboard will appear with false patterns, wavy edges, or Moiré effects. --- ## 3.5 Solved Problem 5: Bit-Depth Reduction and File Size **Problem**: A $256 \times 256$ image is stored with $k = 16$ bits/pixel. The university wants to convert it to $k = 8$ bits/pixel. What is the percentage reduction in file size? ### Solution: **Step 1 — Original size**: $$ b_1 = 256 \times 256 \times 16 = 65{,}536 \times 16 = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2 — Reduced size**: $$ b_2 = 256 \times 256 \times 8 = 65{,}536 \times 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3 — Percentage reduction**: $$ \%\text{Reduction} = \frac{b_1 - b_2}{b_1} \times 100 $$ $$ \%\text{Reduction} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576 - 524{,}288}{1{,}048{,}576} \times 100 = \frac{524{,}288}{1{,}048{,}576} \times 100 $$ $$ \boxed{\%\text{Reduction} = 50\%} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight**: Halving the bit-depth exactly halves the file size. This is a key memory-saving technique in display pipelines and compression pre-processing. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Image Acquisition & Digitization Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Continuous Real World Scene] --> B[Optical Lens System] B --> C[Sensor CCD or CMOS Array] C --> D[Sampling: Spatial Discretization] D --> E[Quantization: Intensity Discretization] E --> F[Digital Image: f x y Matrix MxN] F --> G[Storage in Memory or Disk] F --> H[Display on Monitor] F --> I[Further Processing] style A fill:#e1f5ff style F fill:#ffe1e1 style G fill:#e1ffe1 style H fill:#fff5e1 style I fill:#f0e1ff ``` ## 4.2 Sampling and Quantization Conceptual Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SAMP["Sampling Stage: Continuous to Discrete Coordinates"] S1[Continuous 2D Plane x,y] --> S2[Apply Grid of M rows and N columns] S2 --> S3[Sample intensity at each grid intersection] end SAMP --> QUANT subgraph QUANT["Quantization Stage: Continuous to Discrete Intensity"] Q1[Continuous intensity value] --> Q2[Map to nearest of L = 2 to the k gray levels] Q2 --> Q3[Assign integer code 0 to L-1] end QUANT --> OUT OUT[Final Digital Image: MxN matrix with L gray levels] style SAMP fill:#e3f2fd style QUANT fill:#fff3e0 style OUT fill:#c8e6c9 ``` ## 4.3 Pixel Coordinate System (KTU Convention) ```mermaid graph TB O["Origin f 0,0 Top-Left Corner"] X["X axis Points DOWNWARD Rows"] Y["Y axis Points RIGHTWARD Columns"] P["Pixel at x i, y j"] DIM["Image Size: M rows by N columns"] R["Row index i varies from 0 to M-1"] C["Column index j varies from 0 to N-1"] O --> X O --> Y X --> P Y --> P DIM --> R DIM --> C P --> R P --> C style O fill:#ffcccb style P fill:#c8e6c9 ``` ## 4.4 Image Type Classification Tree ```mermaid graph TB IMG[Digital Image] --> B[Binary Image] IMG --> G[Gray Scale Image] IMG --> COL[Color Image] B --> B1["Two levels: 0 and 1"] B --> B2["Storage: 1 bit per pixel"] G --> G1["L = 2 to the k gray levels"] G --> G2["Typical k equals 8: 256 levels"] COL --> COL1["Three channels: R, G, B"] COL --> COL2["Storage: 24 bits per pixel"] COL --> COL3["Each channel is a gray scale image"] style IMG fill:#bbdefb style B fill:#ffe0b2 style G fill:#c8e6c9 style COL fill:#f8bbd0 ``` ## 4.5 Resolution Trade-off Visualization (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Low Spatial Resolution
Small MxN] -->|Blocky| OUT1[Pixelated Image] B[High Spatial Resolution
Large MxN] -->|Sharp| OUT2[Smooth Image] C[Low Gray Level Resolution
k = 2, L = 4] -->|Bands| OUT3[False Contours] D[High Gray Level Resolution
k = 8, L = 256] -->|Smooth| OUT4[Natural Gradients] OUT1 --> T[Quality Improvement via Increased Resolution] OUT2 --> T OUT3 --> T OUT4 --> T style OUT1 fill:#ffcdd2 style OUT2 fill:#c8e6c9 style OUT3 fill:#ffcdd2 style OUT4 fill:#c8e6c9 style T fill:#bbdefb ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define a digital image. Explain the terms: pixel, gray level, and resolution.** **Model Answer**: A **digital image** is a two-dimensional function $f(x, y)$ in which both the spatial coordinates $(x, y)$ and the intensity $f$ are finite, discrete quantities. - **Pixel**: The smallest addressable element of a digital image, located at coordinates $(x, y)$, storing a single intensity value. It is short for "picture element." - **Gray Level**: The intensity value $f(x, y)$ assigned to a pixel, ranging from $0$ (black) to $L-1$ (white). The number of distinct gray levels is $L = 2^k$, where $k$ is the number of bits per pixel. - **Resolution**: Two types — (i) **Spatial resolution** = the density of pixels (pixels per unit distance, or $M \times N$ total), and (ii) **Gray-level resolution** = the number of bits $k$ used to represent intensity. Higher values yield better image quality but larger storage. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **With a neat diagram, explain the image acquisition pipeline from the continuous scene to the digital image.** **Model Answer**: The image acquisition pipeline consists of three main stages: 1. **Optical Stage**: Light from the scene is focused by a lens onto the image sensor. 2. **Sampling Stage**: The continuous scene is sampled spatially at regular grid intervals defined by the sensor's pixel array (e.g., $M \times N$ grid). 3. **Quantization Stage**: The continuous intensity at each sample is rounded to the nearest of $L = 2^k$ discrete gray levels. The output is a 2D matrix of integers $f(x, y)$ of size $M \times N$, which can be stored, displayed, or processed. > *[Examiner's Note: A labelled block diagram earns 1 mark; explanation of each stage earns the remaining 2 marks.]* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### 📘 Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** Explain in detail the process of **sampling and quantization** in forming a digital image. Illustrate with an example showing the effect of varying $M \times N$ (spatial resolution) and $k$ (gray-level resolution) on image quality. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An image of size $4096 \times 4096$ pixels is stored as an uncompressed color image using 8 bits per channel. Calculate: (i) the number of bits required, (ii) the file size in MB, and (iii) the maximum number of distinct colors representable. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Sampling** is the discretization of spatial coordinates. The continuous image is sampled at a uniform grid of $M$ rows and $N$ columns: $$ (x, y) \in \{(x_i, y_j) \mid i = 0, 1, \dots, M-1; \; j = 0, 1, \dots, N-1\} $$ **Quantization** is the discretization of intensity values. The continuous amplitude $f \in [0, f_{max}]$ is mapped to one of $L = 2^k$ discrete levels: $$ f_Q = \text{round}\left( \frac{f - f_{min}}{f_{max} - f_{min}} \times (L - 1) \right) $$ **Effect of varying $M \times N$**: - $M \times N = 64 \times 64$: blocky, checkerboard appearance (visible pixelation). - $M \times N = 256 \times 256$: recognizable shapes, some blockiness. - $M \times N = 1024 \times 1024$: smooth, photorealistic appearance. **Effect of varying $k$**: - $k = 1$ ($L = 2$): stark black/white only. - $k = 4$ ($L = 16$): visible bands of false contours. - $k = 8$ ($L = 256$): smooth gradient rendering. > **[Mark Distribution]: Sampling definition (1.5) + Quantization definition (1.5) + $M \times N$ effect (2) + $k$ effect (2) = 7 marks** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Number of bits**: Color image has 3 channels, each $k = 8$ bits. $$ b = M \times N \times 3k = 4096 \times 4096 \times 3 \times 8 $$ $$ b = 16{,}777{,}216 \times 24 = 402{,}653{,}184 \text{ bits} $$ **(ii) File size in MB**: $$ \text{Size (MB)} = \frac{402{,}653{,}184}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} = \frac{50{,}331{,}648}{1{,}048{,}576} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Size} = 48 \text{ MB}} $$ **(iii) Maximum number of distinct colors**: Each channel: $L = 2^8 = 256$ levels. For RGB combination: $$ L_{\text{color}} = 256^3 = (2^8)^3 = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 $$ $$ \boxed{L_{\text{color}} = 16.77 \text{ million colors (True Color)}} $$ > **[Mark Distribution]: Part (i) formula + calc (2.5), Part (ii) conversion to MB (2), Part (iii) $256^3$ logic (2.5) = 7 marks** --- ### 📗 Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Define **spatial resolution** and **gray-level resolution**. Discuss, with examples, how reducing each affects the perceived quality of a digital image. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A radiologist needs to store $500$ medical X-ray images, each of size $2048 \times 2048$ pixels, with $k = 12$ bits/pixel. Calculate: (i) the total storage in bits, (ii) total storage in GB, and (iii) the compression ratio required if the storage budget is $2$ GB. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Spatial Resolution** is the density of pixels per unit area in an image, defined either as: - The number of rows and columns $M \times N$, or - Pixels per inch (PPI) / dots per inch (DPI), or - Line pairs per mm (lp/mm) in imaging systems. **Gray-Level Resolution** is the smallest discernible change in intensity, defined as the number of bits $k$ used to represent pixel intensity, giving $L = 2^k$ levels. **Effects on Image Quality**: - Reducing spatial resolution (e.g., from $1024 \times 1024$ to $128 \times 128$): produces **blocky, pixelated** images with loss of fine detail — edges appear as staircases ("jaggies" or "aliasing"). - Reducing gray-level resolution (e.g., from $k = 8$ to $k = 2$): produces **false contouring** — smooth gradients appear as flat bands of constant intensity, mimicking terrain map contours. - Both reductions may be tolerable in some applications (thumbnails) but unacceptable in diagnostic or scientific imaging. > **[Mark Distribution]: Definitions (2) + Spatial effect (2.5) + Gray-level effect (2.5) = 7 marks** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Total bits for 500 images**: Bits per image: $$ b_{\text{image}} = 2048 \times 2048 \times 12 = 4{,}194{,}304 \times 12 = 50{,}331{,}648 \text{ bits} $$ For $500$ images: $$ b_{\text{total}} = 500 \times 50{,}331{,}648 = 25{,}165{,}824{,}000 \text{ bits} $$ **(ii) Convert to GB**: $$ \text{Size (GB)} = \frac{25{,}165{,}824{,}000}{8 \times 1024^3} = \frac{3{,}145{,}728{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Size} \approx 2.93 \text{ GB}} $$ **(iii) Required compression ratio**: $$ C_R = \frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Budget}} = \frac{2.93 \text{ GB}}{2 \text{ GB}} $$ $$ \boxed{C_R = 1.465 : 1 \;\text{(or about 32\% compression)}} $$ > **[Mark Distribution]: Part (i) per-image + total (2.5), Part (ii) GB conversion (2.5), Part (iii) $C_R$ definition + calculation (2) = 7 marks** --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Coordinate Origin Confusion** (–1 mark): Students often write the origin as bottom-left (Cartesian). In image processing, **origin = top-left**, $x$ = downward, $y$ = rightward. > > 2. **Bits vs. Bytes Mistake** (–1 mark): Final answers must clearly state the unit. 1 byte = 8 bits. Don't write "MB" when the value is actually Mbits. > > 3. **Using $|x|$ inside a table** (–1 mark): Use `$\vert x \vert$` not `|x|` in LaTeX tables to avoid breaking the markdown parser. > > 4. **Confusing $L$ with $k$** (–1 mark): $L$ = number of gray levels, $k$ = number of bits. $L = 2^k$, NOT $k = 2^L$. > > 5. **Forgetting the factor of 3 for color images** (–2 marks): When a problem says "color image with 8 bits per channel," you must multiply by **3** for the total bit count. > > 6. **Wrong MB definition** (–1 mark): $1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \times 1024$ bytes $= 2^{20}$ bytes. Some students use $10^6$ — this is SI (MB) but not the standard for file sizes in image processing problems. **Always use $2^{20}$ unless the problem explicitly says "mega-bytes (SI)"**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝 > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam** - ✅ **Master Formula**: $b = M \times N \times k$ (total bits of an image). - ✅ **Gray Levels**: $L = 2^k$ where $k$ is the bits/pixel. - ✅ **Coordinate Convention**: Origin = top-left; $x$ = down, $y$ = right. - ✅ **Image Function**: $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ where $0 \leq i < \infty$ and $0 \leq r \leq 1$. - ✅ **Nyquist Rate**: Sampling frequency $\geq 2 \times$ highest frequency. - ✅ **Binary Image** = 1 bit/pixel, **Gray-Scale (8-bit)** = 8 bits/pixel, **Color (24-bit)** = 24 bits/pixel. - ✅ **File Size Conversions**: - bits $\to$ bytes: divide by $8$ - bytes $\to$ KB: divide by $1024$ - bytes $\to$ MB: divide by $1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576$ - bytes $\to$ GB: divide by $1024^3 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$ - ✅ **Image as a 2D Matrix** of size $M \times N$ with integer entries in $[0, L-1]$. - ✅ **Reducing spatial resolution** $\Rightarrow$ pixelation / blockiness. - ✅ **Reducing gray-level resolution** $\Rightarrow$ false contouring. - ✅ **Aliasing** occurs when sampling rate is below Nyquist rate. - ✅ **Compression Ratio** $C_R = \dfrac{\text{Original}}{\text{Compressed}}$. - ✅ **For color images**, total bits = $3 \times M \times N \times k_{\text{channel}}$. - ✅ **Aspect Ratio** = $\dfrac{M}{N}$ (used in display scaling). - ✅ A square image of $N \times N$ has $N^2$ pixels.

topics.title: Basic Relationship Between Pixels
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Relationship Between Pixels

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Digital Image Processing (DIP), a digital image is a discrete two-dimensional function $f(x, y)$ where $x$ and $y$ are spatial (integer) coordinates and $f$ is the intensity (gray level) at that coordinate. The smallest element of this discrete grid is called a **pixel** (short for *Picture Element*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Pixels do **not** exist in isolation. Almost every image processing operation — edge detection, segmentation, filtering, morphological operations, and connected component analysis — depends on understanding how a given pixel **relates** to the pixels surrounding it. This neighborhood-based reasoning is collectively called the **Basic Relationship Between Pixels**.

The fundamental relationships studied in this topic are:

1. **Neighbors of a Pixel** (4-neighbors $N_4$, Diagonal neighbors $N_D$, 8-neighbors $N_8$)
2. **Adjacency** (4-adjacency, 8-adjacency, m-adjacency)
3. **Connectivity** (4-, 8-, m-connected paths)
4. **Regions & Boundaries**
5. **Distance Measures** ($D_4$, $D_8$, $D_e$)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Neighborhood Map:** Imagine a 2D image as a **city grid** where every pixel is a **house** with its own address $(x, y)$.
> - The **4-neighbors** of a house are the houses directly **North, South, East, and West** — i.e., the houses that share a common *wall* with it.
> - The **diagonal neighbors** are the houses at the **NE, NW, SE, SW** corners — they share only a *point* (a corner).
> - The **8-neighbors** are the combination of both — all houses that touch it either by wall or by corner.
> - **Adjacency** is like asking: *"Are two houses connected by a road that is currently open (i.e., both have the same intensity value)?"*
> - **Connectivity** is the concept of being able to **walk** from one house to another using only such open roads.
> - **Distance** is the shortest number of steps (or shortest road length) you must take to walk from one house to another.

This neighborhood logic is the **backbone** of nearly every algorithm you will study in Module 1, 2, and 3 of DIP — from **point detection** and **line detection** to **region growing** and **boundary tracing**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - A pixel at coordinate $(x, y)$ has exactly **4** cardinal neighbors, **4** diagonal neighbors, and **8** total neighbors (excluding itself).
> - The image is assumed to be a 2D array of size $M \times N$ (rows $\times$ columns).
> - Coordinate origin is the **top-left corner** $(0, 0)$ in standard KTU digital image notation.
> - For boundary pixels, neighbors outside the image are considered **non-existent** (some textbooks pad them with $0$).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Neighbors of a Pixel

Let $p$ be a pixel with coordinates $(x, y)$.

### 2.1.1 4-Neighbors of $p$ — $N_4(p)$
The four cardinal (horizontal + vertical) neighbors:
$$N_4(p) = \{(x+1, y),\ (x-1, y),\ (x, y+1),\ (x, y-1)\}$$

- **Why it matters:** Defines the basic cross-shaped local window — used in masks like the Laplacian, Sobel, and Prewitt filters.
- **Count:** Exactly 4 pixels (provided they lie inside the image).

### 2.1.2 Diagonal Neighbors of $p$ — $N_D(p)$
The four corner (diagonal) neighbors:
$$N_D(p) = \{(x+1, y+1),\ (x+1, y-1),\ (x-1, y+1),\ (x-1, y-1)\}$$

- **Why it matters:** Combined with $N_4$, they form the full 8-connected neighborhood — important in **chessboard-distance** calculations and **diagonal connectivity** analysis.

### 2.1.3 8-Neighbors of $p$ — $N_8(p)$
Union of the two sets:
$$N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$$

- **Count:** Up to 8 pixels (fewer for boundary pixels).
- **Why it matters:** Forms the $3 \times 3$ window around $p$ — the **fundamental processing window** of spatial filtering in DIP.

## 2.2 Adjacency

Let $V$ be the **set of intensity values** used to define adjacency. For a binary image, $V = \{1\}$; for a grayscale image, $V$ is a subset of $\{0, 1, \dots, L-1\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** A pixel $q$ is **adjacent** to $p$ if and only if $q \in N(p)$ **AND** $f(q) \in V$. Both conditions are mandatory.

### 2.2.1 4-Adjacency
Two pixels $p$ and $q$, both with values in $V$, are **4-adjacent** if $q \in N_4(p)$.

### 2.2.2 8-Adjacency
Two pixels $p$ and $q$, both with values in $V$, are **8-adjacent** if $q \in N_8(p)$.

### 2.2.3 m-Adjacency (Mixed Adjacency)
Two pixels $p$ and $q$ are **m-adjacent** if:
- $q \in N_4(p)$ **AND** both $p$ and $q$ have values in $V$, **OR**
- $q \in N_D(p)$ **AND** both are in $V$ **AND** $N_4(p) \cap N_4(q)$ **has no pixel with value in $V$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for m-adjacency:** It eliminates the multiple-path ambiguity that arises in 8-adjacency when diagonal pixels share 4-neighbors of the same value. m-adjacency ensures **unique** paths — crucial for **region labeling** and **boundary tracing** algorithms.

## 2.3 Connectivity

A **path** from pixel $p$ at $(x_0, y_0)$ to pixel $q$ at $(x_n, y_n)$ is a sequence of distinct pixels:
$$(x_0, y_0),\ (x_1, y_1),\ \dots,\ (x_n, y_n)$$

where each $(x_i, y_i)$ is **adjacent** to $(x_{i-1}, y_{i-1})$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$, and all pixels have values in $V$.

- $n$ is the **length of the path**.
- $p$ and $q$ are **connected** in a given adjacency (4, 8, or m) if at least one such path exists.

## 2.4 Regions and Boundaries

- **Region $R$:** A set of pixels that are **connected** (under a specified adjacency) and share the same intensity value (in $V$).
- **Boundary of $R$:** The set of pixels in $R$ that have **at least one neighbor** (in the corresponding adjacency sense) that is **not in $R$**.
- **Edge:** A local concept — pixels where intensity changes sharply. The boundary is a **global** concept, while the edge is **local**.

## 2.5 Distance Measures

For pixels $p = (x, y)$, $q = (s, t)$, and $z = (v, w)$:

### 2.5.1 Euclidean Distance
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(x - s)^2 + (y - t)^2}$$

### 2.5.2 $D_4$ Distance (City-Block / Manhattan Distance)
$$D_4(p, q) = \vert x - s \vert + \vert y - t \vert$$

- Pixels with $D_4(p, q) \leq k$ form a **diamond** centered at $p$.

### 2.5.3 $D_8$ Distance (Chessboard Distance)
$$D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert x - s \vert,\ \vert y - t \vert)$$

- Pixels with $D_8(p, q) \leq k$ form a **square** centered at $p$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both $D_4$ and $D_8$ are **metric distances** — they satisfy:
> 1. $D(p, q) \geq 0$ (non-negativity)
> 2. $D(p, q) = 0 \iff p = q$ (identity)
> 3. $D(p, q) = D(q, p)$ (symmetry)
> 4. $D(p, z) \leq D(p, q) + D(q, z)$ (triangle inequality)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Set Definition | Geometric Shape | Use in DIP |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N_4(p)$ | $\{(x\pm 1, y),\ (x, y\pm 1)\}$ | Cross (+) | Cross-shaped masks |
| $N_D(p)$ | $\{(x\pm 1, y\pm 1)\}$ | Diagonal (X) | Diagonal filters |
| $N_8(p)$ | $N_4 \cup N_D$ | $3\times 3$ box | Spatial filtering window |
| 4-adjacency | $q \in N_4(p),\ f(q) \in V$ | — | Conservative connectivity |
| 8-adjacency | $q \in N_8(p),\ f(q) \in V$ | — | Looser connectivity |
| m-adjacency | $q \in N_4$ OR ($q \in N_D$ AND $N_4(p) \cap N_4(q) \cap V = \emptyset$) | — | Unique-path connectivity |
| $D_e$ | $\sqrt{(x-s)^2 + (y-t)^2}$ | Circle | True geometric distance |
| $D_4$ | $\vert x-s \vert + \vert y-t \vert$ | Diamond | City-block metric |
| $D_8$ | $\max(\vert x-s \vert,\ \vert y-t \vert)$ | Square | Chessboard metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These relationships power **medical imaging segmentation** (tumor isolation), **autonomous vehicle perception** (lane and obstacle detection), **satellite image analysis** (road network extraction), and **biometric systems** (fingerprint minutiae extraction).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Identifying the Neighborhood of a Pixel

**Problem:** Let $p$ be the pixel at coordinate $(3, 4)$ in an $8 \times 8$ image. List all of $p$'s:
1. 4-neighbors
2. Diagonal neighbors
3. 8-neighbors

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: List the 4-neighbors $N_4(p)$.**

Substitute $(x, y) = (3, 4)$ into the formula:
$$N_4(p) = \{(3+1, 4),\ (3-1, 4),\ (3, 4+1),\ (3, 4-1)\}$$
$$\therefore N_4(p) = \{(4, 4),\ (2, 4),\ (3, 5),\ (3, 3)\}$$

**Step 2: List the diagonal neighbors $N_D(p)$.**

$$N_D(p) = \{(3+1, 4+1),\ (3+1, 4-1),\ (3-1, 4+1),\ (3-1, 4-1)\}$$
$$\therefore N_D(p) = \{(4, 5),\ (4, 3),\ (2, 5),\ (2, 3)\}$$

**Step 3: Form the 8-neighbors $N_8(p)$ by taking the union.**

$$N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$$
$$\therefore N_8(p) = \{(4, 4),\ (2, 4),\ (3, 5),\ (3, 3),\ (4, 5),\ (4, 3),\ (2, 5),\ (2, 3)\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This forms a complete $3 \times 3$ window centered at $p$, except for $p$ itself. This is the standard $3 \times 3$ spatial mask used in **convolution-based filtering**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Adjacency Type Identification

**Problem:** Consider a $3 \times 3$ sub-image with intensity values (center pixel $p$ is at position $(1, 1)$):
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

With $V = \{1\}$, determine whether the top-left pixel $(0, 0)$ and the top-right pixel $(0, 2)$ are 4-adjacent, 8-adjacent, and m-adjacent to $p$ (at $(1, 1)$).

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Let $A = (0, 0)$, $B = (0, 2)$, $C = (1, 1)$.

**Step 1: Check 4-adjacency of $A$ and $p$.**

- $A = (0, 0)$, $p = (1, 1)$. Is $A \in N_4(p)$?
- $N_4(p) = \{(0, 1),\ (2, 1),\ (1, 0),\ (1, 2)\}$.
- $A = (0, 0) \notin N_4(p)$. So $A$ is **NOT 4-adjacent** to $p$.

**Step 2: Check 8-adjacency of $A$ and $p$.**

- $A = (0, 0) \in N_D(p)$? Yes, because $N_D(p) = \{(0, 0),\ (0, 2),\ (2, 0),\ (2, 2)\}$.
- Value of $A$ is $1 \in V$. So $A$ **IS 8-adjacent** to $p$.

**Step 3: Check m-adjacency of $A$ and $p$.**

- $A \in N_D(p)$ (diagonal neighbor) — first condition satisfied.
- Both $A$ and $p$ have value $1 \in V$ — second condition satisfied.
- Now check the third condition: $N_4(A) \cap N_4(p) \cap V = \emptyset$?
  - $N_4(A) = N_4(0, 0) = \{(1, 0),\ (0, 1)\}$
  - $N_4(p) = N_4(1, 1) = \{(0, 1),\ (2, 1),\ (1, 0),\ (1, 2)\}$
  - $N_4(A) \cap N_4(p) = \{(1, 0),\ (0, 1)\}$
  - Values at $(1, 0)$ and $(0, 1)$ are both $1 \in V$.
  - Intersection with $V$ is $\{(1, 0),\ (0, 1)\} \neq \emptyset$.
- **Third condition fails** → $A$ is **NOT m-adjacent** to $p$.

**Result:** $A$ is 8-adjacent but NOT 4-adjacent and NOT m-adjacent to $p$.

This is the classic m-adjacency example: it **breaks** the diagonal link because a horizontal/vertical 4-connected path through $(1, 0)$ or $(0, 1)$ already exists, avoiding the **8-connectivity ambiguity**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Computing Distance Metrics

**Problem:** Compute $D_e$, $D_4$, and $D_8$ between pixels $p = (2, 3)$ and $q = (7, 8)$.

**Step 1: Identify the absolute differences.**

- $\vert x - s \vert = \vert 2 - 7 \vert = 5$
- $\vert y - t \vert = \vert 3 - 8 \vert = 5$

**Step 2: Compute Euclidean distance $D_e$.**

$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(x - s)^2 + (y - t)^2}$$
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{5^2 + 5^2}$$
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{25 + 25}$$
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{50}$$
$$D_e(p, q) = 5\sqrt{2} \approx 7.071\ \text{pixels}$$

**Step 3: Compute $D_4$ distance.**

$$D_4(p, q) = \vert x - s \vert + \vert y - t \vert$$
$$D_4(p, q) = 5 + 5 = 10\ \text{pixels}$$

**Step 4: Compute $D_8$ distance.**

$$D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert x - s \vert,\ \vert y - t \vert)$$
$$D_8(p, q) = \max(5,\ 5) = 5\ \text{pixels}$$

**Verification of Triangle Inequality** (using a third pixel $z = (4, 5)$):
- $D_4(p, q) = 10$, $D_4(p, z) = \vert 2 - 4 \vert + \vert 3 - 5 \vert = 2 + 2 = 4$, $D_4(z, q) = \vert 4 - 7 \vert + \vert 5 - 8 \vert = 3 + 3 = 6$
- Check: $D_4(p, z) + D_4(z, q) = 4 + 6 = 10 = D_4(p, q)$ ✓ (saturated triangle inequality — $z$ lies on the shortest path).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $D_4$ and $D_8$ are preferred over $D_e$ in hardware implementations (FPGA, GPU) because they involve only integer arithmetic — no floating-point square root computation, which is expensive in real-time image processing pipelines.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Connectivity

| Property | 4-Connectivity | 8-Connectivity | m-Connectivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adjacency Basis | $N_4$ | $N_8$ | $N_4$ + $N_D$ (with $V$-intersection rule) |
| Path Direction | Cardinal only | All 8 directions | Cardinal + selective diagonal |
| Multiple Paths Issue | None | Can occur | Eliminated |
| Path Length (diagonal step) | N/A | 1 step | 1 step (if rule allows) |
| Best For | Conservative region growing | Wide region merging | Unique-path region labeling |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Define the following for a pixel $p$ at $(x, y)$:**
**(a)** $N_4(p)$
**(b)** $N_D(p)$
**(c)** $N_8(p)$

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) $N_4(p)$:** The set of four cardinal (horizontal and vertical) neighbors of $p$:
$$N_4(p) = \{(x+1, y),\ (x-1, y),\ (x, y+1),\ (x, y-1)\}$$

- **(b) $N_D(p)$:** The set of four diagonal neighbors of $p$:
$$N_D(p) = \{(x+1, y+1),\ (x+1, y-1),\ (x-1, y+1),\ (x-1, y-1)\}$$

- **(c) $N_8(p)$:** The union of $N_4$ and $N_D$, representing all 8 surrounding pixels (a $3 \times 3$ window minus the center):
$$N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is m-adjacency? Why is it preferred over 8-adjacency in region labeling?**

**Model Answer:**

**m-Adjacency Definition:** Two pixels $p$ and $q$ with values in set $V$ are **m-adjacent** if:
- $q \in N_4(p)$, OR
- $q \in N_D(p)$ **AND** $N_4(p) \cap N_4(q)$ contains **no pixel whose value is in $V$**.

**Why preferred over 8-adjacency:**
m-adjacency **eliminates the ambiguity of multiple paths** that exists in 8-adjacency. For example, in an 8-connected framework, two diagonal pixels may be linked via **two distinct paths**, leading to inconsistent region labels. m-adjacency enforces a **unique path** by disallowing diagonal connections when a 4-connected alternative exists — making it ideal for **connected component labeling** and **boundary tracing** algorithms.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With respect to a pixel $p$ at coordinates $(x, y)$, explain in detail the three types of neighbors — $N_4(p)$, $N_D(p)$, and $N_8(p)$ — with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the $3 \times 3$ sub-image shown below, with $V = \{1\}$, determine all pairs of pixels that are 4-adjacent, 8-adjacent, and m-adjacent to the center pixel $p$. **[7 Marks]**
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Solution:**

A pixel $p$ at coordinate $(x, y)$ has three categories of neighbors:

**1. 4-Neighbors $N_4(p)$ — Cardinal Neighbors:**
$$N_4(p) = \{(x+1, y),\ (x-1, y),\ (x, y+1),\ (x, y-1)\}$$

These are the four pixels sharing an **edge** (not a corner) with $p$. For $p = (2, 3)$ in a $5 \times 5$ image:
$$N_4(p) = \{(3, 3),\ (1, 3),\ (2, 4),\ (2, 2)\}$$

**2. Diagonal Neighbors $N_D(p)$:**
$$N_D(p) = \{(x+1, y+1),\ (x+1, y-1),\ (x-1, y+1),\ (x-1, y-1)\}$$

These are the four pixels sharing only a **corner point** with $p$. For $p = (2, 3)$:
$$N_D(p) = \{(3, 4),\ (3, 2),\ (1, 4),\ (1, 2)\}$$

**3. 8-Neighbors $N_8(p)$ — Full Neighborhood:**
$$N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p) = \{(3, 3),\ (1, 3),\ (2, 4),\ (2, 2),\ (3, 4),\ (3, 2),\ (1, 4),\ (1, 2)\}$$

This is the complete $3 \times 3$ window around $p$, excluding $p$ itself. **Engineering use:** $N_8$ defines the spatial support for **all $3 \times 3$ convolution masks** (Sobel, Prewitt, Laplacian, etc.).

**(b) Solution:**

Let the image positions be $(r, c)$ where $r$ is row and $c$ is column (0-indexed). The center pixel $p$ is at $(1, 1)$ with value $1$.

The 8 surrounding pixels are:
| Position | Coordinate | Value | In $V = \{1\}$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top-Left | $(0, 0)$ | 0 | No |
| Top-Mid | $(0, 1)$ | 1 | Yes |
| Top-Right | $(0, 2)$ | 0 | No |
| Mid-Left | $(1, 0)$ | 1 | Yes |
| Mid-Right | $(1, 2)$ | 0 | No |
| Bottom-Left | $(2, 0)$ | 0 | No |
| Bottom-Mid | $(2, 1)$ | 1 | Yes |
| Bottom-Right | $(2, 2)$ | 1 | Yes |

**4-Adjacency with $p$:** $q \in N_4(p)$ AND $f(q) \in V$.
- $N_4(p) = \{(0, 1),\ (2, 1),\ (1, 0),\ (1, 2)\}$
- Pixels with value in $V$: $(0, 1)$, $(2, 1)$, $(1, 0)$.
- **4-adjacent pixels to $p$: $\{(0, 1),\ (2, 1),\ (1, 0)\}$** (3 pairs).

**8-Adjacency with $p$:** $q \in N_8(p)$ AND $f(q) \in V$.
- All 8 positions checked above; pixels with value in $V$: $(0, 1)$, $(1, 0)$, $(2, 1)$, $(2, 2)$.
- **8-adjacent pixels to $p$: $\{(0, 1),\ (1, 0),\ (2, 1),\ (2, 2)\}$** (4 pairs).

**m-Adjacency with $p$:** Check each diagonal neighbor with the additional rule.
- For $(2, 2)$ — diagonal: $N_4(p) \cap N_4(2, 2) = \{(1, 2),\ (2, 1)\}$. Values: $(1, 2) = 0 \notin V$, $(2, 1) = 1 \in V$. Intersection with $V$ is non-empty. → **NOT m-adjacent**.
- All other 8-adjacent pixels were already in $N_4(p)$, so they remain m-adjacent.
- **m-adjacent pixels to $p$: $\{(0, 1),\ (1, 0),\ (2, 1)\}$** (3 pairs).

**Conclusion:** m-adjacency removed the diagonal link to $(2, 2)$ because the 4-neighbor $(2, 1)$ already provides a connecting path of value 1.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define **adjacency**, **connectivity**, **region**, and **boundary** in the context of digital image processing. Explain the three types of adjacencies with neat sketches. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the three distance measures $D_e$, $D_4$, and $D_8$ between two pixels. For the pixels $p = (1, 2)$ and $q = (5, 7)$, compute all three distances. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Solution:**

**Definitions:**

- **Adjacency:** Two pixels $p$ and $q$ are adjacent if $q$ is in the neighborhood of $p$ AND both have values from the same intensity set $V$.
- **Connectivity:** Two pixels are connected if there exists a path of adjacent pixels (each in $V$) between them.
- **Region:** A connected set of pixels (under a defined adjacency) sharing the same value in $V$.
- **Boundary:** The set of pixels in a region that have at least one neighbor (in the chosen adjacency) that does NOT belong to the region.

**Three Types of Adjacency (with sketch description):**

| Type | Definition | Visual Sketch (center = $p$) |
|---|---|---|
| **4-Adjacency** | $q \in N_4(p)$ AND $f(q) \in V$ | ` . * . ` / ` * p * ` / ` . * . ` (only the 4 cardinal `*` are adjacent) |
| **8-Adjacency** | $q \in N_8(p)$ AND $f(q) \in V$ | ` * * * ` / ` * p * ` / ` * * * ` (all 8 surrounding pixels) |
| **m-Adjacency** | $q \in N_4(p)$ OR ($q \in N_D(p)$ AND no $V$-valued common 4-neighbor) | ` . * . ` if $N_4(p) \cap N_4(q) \cap V = \emptyset$, else falls back to 4-adjacency |

**Engineering Insight:** m-adjacency is critical for **region growing** and **connected component labeling** because it guarantees a **unique** labeling for each connected region.

**(b) Solution:**

**Distance Measure Definitions:**

- **Euclidean Distance** $D_e$: True geometric straight-line distance.
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(x - s)^2 + (y - t)^2}$$

- **$D_4$ Distance (City-Block)**: Sum of absolute coordinate differences — represents the minimum number of horizontal/vertical moves.
$$D_4(p, q) = \vert x - s \vert + \vert y - t \vert$$

- **$D_8$ Distance (Chessboard)**: Maximum of absolute coordinate differences — represents the minimum number of king-moves on a chessboard.
$$D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert x - s \vert,\ \vert y - t \vert)$$

**Computation for $p = (1, 2)$ and $q = (5, 7)$:**

**Step 1: Compute coordinate differences.**
- $\vert x_p - x_q \vert = \vert 1 - 5 \vert = 4$
- $\vert y_p - y_q \vert = \vert 2 - 7 \vert = 5$

**Step 2: Compute $D_e$.**
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{4^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{16 + 25} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.403\ \text{pixels}$$

**Step 3: Compute $D_4$.**
$$D_4(p, q) = 4 + 5 = 9\ \text{pixels}$$

**Step 4: Compute $D_8$.**
$$D_8(p, q) = \max(4, 5) = 5\ \text{pixels}$$

**Verification of ordering:** $D_8 \leq D_e \leq D_4$ → $5 \leq 6.403 \leq 9$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Coordinate convention error:** Students often swap $x$ and $y$. Remember: in image notation, $(x, y)$ where $x$ is the **row index** and $y$ is the **column index** (NOT Cartesian $(x, y)$). Always confirm the convention used in the question.
> 2. **m-Adjacency oversight:** The most common error is forgetting to check the $N_4(p) \cap N_4(q) \cap V$ condition. Marks are explicitly allotted for showing this step.
> 3. **Boundary pixel handling:** For pixels on the image boundary, the neighborhood is smaller (fewer than 4 or 8 neighbors). Don't blindly list 8 neighbors — verify they lie within the $M \times N$ image.
> 4. **Confusing $D_4$ and $D_8$ shapes:** $D_4$ gives a **diamond**, $D_8$ gives a **square**. Students frequently interchange them in sketch questions.
> 5. **Forgetting $V$ in adjacency:** Adjacency requires BOTH the neighborhood condition AND intensity-in-$V$ condition. Missing the second part leads to a full mark deduction.
