## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Relationship Between Pixels**

- [x] A digital image is a 2D discrete function $f(x, y)$ defined over a finite grid of size $M \times N$.
- [x] **$N_4(p)$:** 4 cardinal neighbors (cross shape) — used in $3 \times 3$ cross-mask filters.
- [x] **$N_D(p)$:** 4 diagonal neighbors (X shape) — used in diagonal edge detection.
- [x] **$N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$:** 8 neighbors (full $3 \times 3$ minus center) — **standard spatial filter window**.
- [x] **Adjacency** requires: (i) neighborhood relation AND (ii) both pixels in intensity set $V$.
- [x] **4-adjacency** uses $N_4$ — conservative, may **fragment** diagonal regions.
- [x] **8-adjacency** uses $N_8$ — broader, can create **multiple paths** between pixels.
- [x] **m-adjacency** uses a hybrid rule — **eliminates multiple paths** by blocking diagonal links when 4-paths exist; **preferred for region labeling**.
- [x] **Connectivity:** Path of adjacent pixels (each in $V$); forms the basis of **regions**.
- [x] **Region:** A maximal connected set of pixels with values in $V$.
- [x] **Boundary (of a region):** Set of pixels in $R$ having at least one neighbor outside $R$.
- [x] **Edge ≠ Boundary:** Edge is *local* (intensity gradient); Boundary is *global* (region topology).
- [x] **$D_e$** = Euclidean distance (real-valued) — true geometric distance, **shape: circle**.
- [x] **$D_4$** = $\vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ — city-block metric, **shape: diamond**, **integer arithmetic**.
- [x] **$D_8$** = $\max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ — chessboard metric, **shape: square**, **integer arithmetic**.
- [x] All three distances satisfy the **metric axioms** (non-negativity, identity, symmetry, triangle inequality).
- [x] For any two pixels: $D_8 \leq D_e \leq D_4$ **always** holds.
- [x] **Boundary pixels** have fewer neighbors — handle them carefully in mask-based operations.
- [x] **Engineering use cases:** Region growing, connected component labeling, edge detection, morphological operations, image segmentation, distance transform, and shortest-path algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line KTU Memory Aid:**
> *"4 cross, X diagonal, 8 full box — Adjacency needs V, m-adjacency kills ambiguity — $D_4$ diamond, $D_8$ square, $D_e$ circle."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of DIP System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elements of DIP System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Digital Image Processing (DIP) System?

A **Digital Image Processing (DIP) System** is an integrated assembly of hardware and software components designed to acquire, store, manipulate, analyse, and visualise digital images using digital computers. The system transforms an input image into an enhanced or interpreted output image through a sequence of well-defined processing stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Standard):** A DIP system is a configuration of image acquisition sensors, digitiser hardware, dedicated processors, mass storage units, display devices, and communication networks that together perform image-specific operations on pixel arrays to extract meaningful information.

## 1.2 Core Elements of a DIP System (5-Fundamental Block Architecture)

A complete DIP system consists of **five fundamental functional blocks**, each performing a distinct role in the image processing chain.

### 1. Image Acquisition (Sensing Stage)
This is the *front-end* of the DIP system. It involves:
- **Image Sensors** that convert real-world light energy into electrical signals
- Common sensors: **CCD (Charge Coupled Device)** and **CMOS (Complementary Metal Oxide Semiconductor)**
- A **digitiser** that converts the continuous (analog) signal into discrete digital values

### 2. Mass Storage (Memory Stage)
- Stores large volumes of digital images
- Required because images consume massive memory (e.g., a 1024 × 1024 image at 8-bit requires over 1 MB)
- Types: **Short-term storage (RAM)**, **On-line storage (magnetic disks)**, **Archival storage (optical/SSD/tape)**

### 3. Image Processing (Computation Stage)
- The **digital computer** (general-purpose CPU, GPU, or dedicated DIP processor) executes the algorithms
- Operations include enhancement, restoration, segmentation, feature extraction, and compression

### 4. Display (Visualisation Stage)
- Converts processed digital data back into viewable form
- Common displays: **CRT monitors, LCD screens, OLED panels, printers, plotters**

### 5. Communication (Transmission Stage)
- Handles the transfer of images between system components
- Uses **LAN, WAN, broadband, fibre-optic links, and wireless channels**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Darkroom" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a DIP system as a **modern digital darkroom** (replacing the old chemical darkroom used in film photography).
> - The **camera sensor** is like the film roll — it captures the raw light.
> - The **computer + software (Photoshop-like)** is like the magnifying glass, brushes, and chemical trays that enhance, crop, and retouch.
> - The **hard disk** is the photo album.
> - The **monitor/printer** is the wall where the final photograph is displayed.
> - The **internet/network** is the courier that delivers the photograph to your friend.
> 
> In a DIP system, every chemical tray of the traditional darkroom is replaced by a *mathematical algorithm* running on a computer.

## 1.4 Why Learn the Elements of a DIP System?

- **Engineering Relevance:** DIP is foundational in medical imaging (MRI/CT), satellite remote sensing, biometrics, autonomous vehicles, industrial inspection, and surveillance.
- **System Design Awareness:** Understanding each block enables a B.Tech engineer to design complete imaging pipelines.
- **KTU 2024 Outcome Alignment:** Maps to **CO1** — "Apply knowledge of image acquisition, sampling, quantization, and basic transformations."

## 1.5 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard image-related metrics:
> - **Storage Size Formula:** $N \times N \times k$ **bits**, where $N$ is image dimension and $k$ is bit-depth.
> - **Standard Bit-Depths:** **1-bit (binary)**, **8-bit (grayscale)**, **24-bit (RGB colour)**
> - **Visible Light Wavelength Range:** **400 nm – 700 nm** (sensitive band of typical silicon sensors)
> - **Dynamic Range:** Ratio of maximum measurable intensity to minimum measurable intensity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Operational Breakdown of Each DIP Element

### A. Image Acquisition Subsystem

The acquisition block performs **two sub-operations**:

1. **Energy Illumination** — The scene must be illuminated by a source of energy (e.g., sunlight, infrared LEDs, X-ray tube, ultrasound transducer). For self-illuminating modalities (e.g., radar), the object itself emits energy.

2. **Sensing + Digitisation** — A **2-D sensor array** captures incident energy. The output of the sensor is a continuous analog voltage. This analog signal is then sampled spatially and quantized in amplitude to produce a digital image $f(x, y)$.

**Logical Flow:**
- Physical scene → Lens → Sensor Array (CCD/CMOS)
- Analog signal → Sampler → Quantizer → Digital Image Matrix
- Mathematical representation: $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ where $i(x,y)$ is illumination and $r(x,y)$ is reflectance, both bounded by $0 < i(x,y) < \infty$ and $0 < r(x,y) < 1$.

### B. Mass Storage Subsystem

Three-tier hierarchy:

| Tier | Type | Capacity | Access Time | Purpose |
|------|------|----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | RAM (Cache/Volatile) | GBs | Nanoseconds | Active image processing buffer |
| 2 | Magnetic Disk / SSD | TBs | Milliseconds | On-line storage and quick retrieval |
| 3 | Optical Tape / Cloud | PBs | Seconds | Long-term archival |

### C. Image Processing Subsystem

The processing block accepts digital images and applies algorithms classified into:

- **Low-Level Processes:** Primitive operations such as noise reduction, contrast enhancement (input: image → output: image)
- **Mid-Level Processes:** Tasks like segmentation, edge detection, object description (input: image → output: image attributes/features)
- **High-Level Processes:** Cognitive functions like scene interpretation, object recognition (input: features → output: semantic understanding)

### D. Display Subsystem

The display takes the processed digital image and presents it on a **2-D raster device**. Critical display metrics:
- **Spatial Resolution:** Pixels per inch (PPI)
- **Greyscale Resolution:** Number of distinct intensity levels
- **Refresh Rate:** Frames per second (Hz)

### E. Communication Subsystem

Handles bandwidth requirements. A colour TV image (640 × 480, 24-bit) at 30 fps needs:
$$
\text{Bandwidth} = 640 \times 480 \times 24 \times 30 \text{ bits/s}
$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Image Function | $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ | Dimensionless × illumination units |
| 2 | Image Storage Size | $M \times N \times k$ | bits (M,N = dimensions, k = bit-depth) |
| 3 | Number of Grey Levels | $L = 2^k$ | k = bits per pixel |
| 4 | Bandwidth (video) | $W = M \times N \times k \times f_{fps}$ | bits/sec |
| 5 | Dynamic Range | $DR = L_{max} / L_{min}$ | Ratio (unitless) |
| 6 | Spatial Resolution | $M \times N$ | pixels |
| 7 | Illumination Bounds | $0 < i(x,y) < \infty$ | Energy units |
| 8 | Reflectance Bounds | $0 \leq r(x,y) \leq 1$ | Dimensionless (0 = total absorption, 1 = total reflection) |
| 9 | Pixels per Frame (Colour) | $3 \times M \times N$ | For 3-channel RGB |
| 10 | File Size (Bytes) | $(M \times N \times k) / 8$ | Bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in designing **medical PACS systems**, **satellite ground stations**, **real-time surveillance networks**, and **mobile camera pipelines**. For instance, choosing the right bit-depth directly impacts the storage and bandwidth budget of a CCTV deployment.

## 2.3 Three Levels of Digital Image Processing (Gonzalez Classification)

1. **Low-Level:** Both input and output are images. Examples: noise filtering, brightness adjustment, geometric warping.
2. **Mid-Level:** Inputs are images, outputs are extracted attributes (edges, contours, regions). Examples: segmentation, morphological processing.
3. **High-Level:** Inputs are image-derived features, outputs involve cognitive analysis. Examples: scene understanding, autonomous decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequent Favourite:** The question "Classify image processing into three levels with examples" appears frequently. Memorise this classification verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem — Worked Example: Image Storage Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A medical imaging system captures a **grayscale X-ray image** of size $2048 \times 2048$ pixels with **12-bit intensity resolution**. Calculate:
1. The total storage required for one image in bytes.
2. The bandwidth required to transmit 25 such images per second.
3. The number of distinct grey levels.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Data

Extracting parameters from the problem statement:

$$
M = 2048, \quad N = 2048, \quad k = 12 \text{ bits/pixel}
$$

Where $M$ = number of rows, $N$ = number of columns, $k$ = bit-depth (intensity resolution).

### Step 2 — Calculate Storage per Image

Using the standard image storage formula:

$$
\text{Storage (bits)} = M \times N \times k
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\text{Storage} = 2048 \times 2048 \times 12
$$

Breaking this down step-by-step:

$$
2048 \times 2048 = (2^{11}) \times (2^{11}) = 2^{22} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ pixels}
$$

Multiplying by the bit-depth:

$$
\text{Storage} = 4{,}194{,}304 \times 12 = 50{,}331{,}648 \text{ bits}
$$

Converting bits to bytes (divide by 8):

$$
\text{Storage (bytes)} = \frac{50{,}331{,}648}{8} = 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting to Megabytes (divide by $1024 \times 1024 = 1{,}048{,}576$):

$$
\text{Storage (MB)} = \frac{6{,}291{,}456}{1{,}048{,}576} = 6.0 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Required Bandwidth

For 25 images per second:

$$
W = M \times N \times k \times f_{fps}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
W = 50{,}331{,}648 \times 25 = 1{,}258{,}291{,}200 \text{ bits/second}
$$

Converting to Megabits per second (Mbps):

$$
W = \frac{1{,}258{,}291{,}200}{10^6} \approx 1258.3 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Number of Grey Levels

Using $L = 2^k$:

$$
L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ distinct grey levels}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Tabulated Answer

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|----------|-------|------|
| Total pixels | $4{,}194{,}304$ | pixels |
| Storage per image | $\approx 6.0$ | MB |
| Bandwidth required | $\approx 1258.3$ | Mbps |
| Number of grey levels | $4096$ | levels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 6 MB image at 25 fps requires nearly 1.3 Gbps link. This is why medical PACS systems use **lossless compression (DICOM/JPEG-LS)** and **dedicated fibre networks**.

## 3.2 Analytical Problem — Image Function Bounds Derivation

**Problem:** Prove that for any digital image, the intensity at each pixel must satisfy $0 < f(x, y) < \infty$.

### Step 1 — State the Image Function Model

The fundamental image model is:

$$
f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)
$$

Where $i(x,y)$ is the illumination component and $r(x,y)$ is the reflectance/transmittance component.

### Step 2 — Establish the Bounds of Each Component

- **Illumination:** Must be strictly positive, $0 < i(x,y) < \infty$, because a physically meaningful scene always has *some* incident energy.
- **Reflectance:** Must lie in the closed interval $0 \leq r(x,y) \leq 1$ because a surface cannot reflect more light than incident upon it (physical law of energy conservation).

### Step 3 — Multiply the Bounds

Multiplying the two inequality ranges:

$$
0 \cdot 0 < i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y) < \infty \cdot 1
$$

Which simplifies to:

$$
0 < f(x, y) < \infty
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The lower bound is strict ($> 0$) — a perfect black body ($r = 0$) with zero illumination ($i = 0$) is a physically degenerate case and is excluded.

## 3.3 Summary of Analytical Concepts

- **Storage scales linearly** with both spatial resolution and bit-depth.
- **Bandwidth scales linearly** with the number of frames per second.
- **Doubling one dimension** quadruples storage (area scaling).
- **Increasing bit-depth by 1** doubles the number of grey levels but increases storage by 12.5% (for k = 8).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic DIP System

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **five fundamental elements** of a DIP system and the signal/data flow between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sceneA[Physical Scene] -->|Light Energy| sensorA[Image Sensor CCD CMOS]
    sensorA -->|Analog Signal| digitA[Digitiser Sampler and Quantizer]
    digitA -->|Digital Image| procA[Image Processor CPU GPU DSP]
    procA -->|Processed Data| dispA[Display Monitor Printer]
    procA -->|Read Write Ops| storeA[Mass Storage RAM SSD Tape]
    storeA -->|Image Retrieval| procA
    procA -->|Image Packets| commA[Communication Network LAN WAN]
    commA -->|Transmitted Output| remoteA[Remote User or System]
    digitA -->|Backup to| storeA
```

## 4.2 Three-Level Processing Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    levelA[Low Level Processing] -->|Input Image Output Image| levelA
    levelB[Mid Level Processing] -->|Input Image Output Features| levelB
    levelC[High Level Processing] -->|Input Features Output Semantics| levelC
    levelA --> levelB
    levelB --> levelC
```

**Diagram Annotation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Low-level** processes (e.g., noise removal) are characterised by *image-in / image-out* operations.
> - **Mid-level** processes (e.g., edge detection) are characterised by *image-in / features-out*.
> - **High-level** processes (e.g., face recognition) are characterised by *features-in / interpretation-out*.

## 4.3 Hardware Stack Architecture (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1[Application Layer Medical Imaging Surveillance Remote Sensing]
    layer2[Algorithm Layer Enhancement Restoration Segmentation Compression]
    layer3[Processing Layer CPU GPU FPGA DSP]
    layer4[Storage Layer RAM SSD Cloud]
    layer5[Acquisition Layer Sensors Digitiser Optics]
    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4
    layer3 --> layer5
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer5 --> layer3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This layered architecture mirrors how real engineering teams are organised. A B.Tech project will typically have separate modules for the **acquisition layer** (hardware) and the **algorithm layer** (software), with the **processing layer** (CPU/GPU) acting as the bridge.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the five fundamental elements of a digital image processing system. State the function of each in one sentence.*

**Model Answer:**

The five fundamental elements of a DIP system are:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Captures the physical scene using sensors (CCD/CMOS) and converts it into a digital signal using a digitiser.
2. **Mass Storage** — Stores large volumes of digital image data using RAM, magnetic disks, and archival media.
3. **Image Processing** — The computer (CPU/GPU/DSP) that runs algorithms for enhancement, restoration, segmentation, etc.
4. **Display** — Renders the processed image on monitors, LCDs, or printers for visualisation.
5. **Communication** — Transfers images and data between components using networks (LAN, WAN, wireless).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the three levels of digital image processing with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

The three levels of DIP, classified by Gonzalez, are:

1. **Low-Level Processing:** Both input and output are images. Examples — noise filtering, contrast stretching, image sharpening.
2. **Mid-Level Processing:** Input is an image; output is image-derived attributes such as edges, contours, or regions. Examples — segmentation, morphological operations, edge detection.
3. **High-Level Processing:** Involves cognitive/AI-based analysis. Input is image features; output is semantic interpretation. Examples — face recognition, scene understanding, object classification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the fundamental steps involved in a digital image processing system. Explain the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An image of size $256 \times 256$ pixels with $256$ grey levels is given.
  (i) Find the size of the image in bytes.
  (ii) If the image is transmitted at 30 frames per second, calculate the required bandwidth.
  (iii) State the formula $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ and explain the bounds of $i$ and $r$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram Explanation (Full Marks):**

A DIP system consists of the following blocks arranged sequentially:

1. **Image Acquisition Block** — Comprises sensors and digitiser. Sensor (CCD/CMOS) converts the energy from the scene into an electrical signal; the digitiser converts this analog signal into a digital image by sampling and quantization.

2. **Mass Storage Block** — Provides short-term (RAM), on-line (magnetic disk / SSD), and archival (optical/tape) storage. Required because image data is voluminous.

3. **Image Processing Block** — Contains the digital computer and DIP-specific software/hardware. Performs low-level, mid-level, and high-level operations.

4. **Display Block** — Uses monitors, printers, or plotters to display the processed image.

5. **Communication Block** — Transmits images between system components and to remote users over networks.

**Five bullets × 1.4 marks each = 7 marks.**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Image size in bytes:**

Given: $M = 256$, $N = 256$, $k = \log_2(256) = 8$ bits/pixel.

$$
\text{Size} = M \times N \times k = 256 \times 256 \times 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bits}
$$

Converting to bytes:

$$
\text{Size (bytes)} = \frac{524{,}288}{8} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

**(ii) Bandwidth at 30 fps:**

$$
W = M \times N \times k \times f_{fps} = 256 \times 256 \times 8 \times 30
$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$
256 \times 256 = 65{,}536 \text{ pixels}
$$

$$
65{,}536 \times 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bits/image}
$$

$$
524{,}288 \times 30 = 15{,}728{,}640 \text{ bits/sec} \approx 15.73 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**(iii) Image function and bounds:**

The image intensity is modelled as:

$$
f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)
$$

Where $i(x,y)$ is illumination and $r(x,y)$ is reflectance. Bounds:
- $0 < i(x,y) < \infty$ (illumination is non-zero finite positive)
- $0 \leq r(x,y) \leq 1$ (reflectance is bounded by physical law of energy conservation)

**Mark distribution: (i) 2 marks + (ii) 2 marks + (iii) 3 marks = 7 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the three levels of image processing with two examples for each level. Also, justify why each level requires a different type of computational resource. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A satellite imaging system captures a 12-bit image of size $1024 \times 1024$ pixels.
  (i) Determine the number of grey levels in the image.
  (ii) Calculate the storage required per image in KB.
  (iii) If 50 such images are captured per day, compute the daily storage in GB. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Three Levels of DIP:**

- **Low-Level:** Input = image, Output = image. Examples — (1) Gaussian smoothing for noise reduction, (2) Histogram equalisation for contrast enhancement. Computational resource: Simple pixel-by-pixel operations, suitable for **CPU or dedicated hardware (ASIC/FPGA)**.

- **Mid-Level:** Input = image, Output = image attributes (edges, regions). Examples — (1) Canny edge detection, (2) Otsu's thresholding for segmentation. Computational resource: Local neighbourhood operations, suitable for **multi-core CPU or GPU**.

- **High-Level:** Input = features, Output = semantic interpretation. Examples — (1) CNN-based object detection, (2) OCR (optical character recognition). Computational resource: Heavy matrix algebra, requires **GPU clusters or cloud computing**.

**Mark distribution: 3 levels × 2 marks = 6 marks + justification 1 mark = 7 marks**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Number of grey levels:**

$$
L = 2^k = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ grey levels}
$$

**(ii) Storage per image in KB:**

$$
\text{Storage (bits)} = 1024 \times 1024 \times 12 = 12{,}582{,}912 \text{ bits}
$$

Converting to bytes:

$$
\frac{12{,}582{,}912}{8} = 1{,}572{,}864 \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting to KB (divide by 1024):

$$
\frac{1{,}572{,}864}{1024} = 1536 \text{ KB} = 1.5 \text{ MB}
$$

**(iii) Daily storage:**

$$
\text{Daily storage} = 1.5 \text{ MB} \times 50 = 75 \text{ MB/day}
$$

Converting to GB:

$$
75 \text{ MB} = 0.0732 \text{ GB}
$$

**Mark distribution: (i) 2 marks + (ii) 3 marks + (iii) 2 marks = 7 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert bits → bytes → KB → MB. Many students lose 1–2 marks here. Always show the unit at every step.
> 2. **Confusing $k$ with $L$:** $k$ = bits per pixel; $L = 2^k$ = number of grey levels. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 3. **Omitting Block Diagram Labels:** When asked for a "neat block diagram", forgetting arrows between blocks or failing to label the data type (analog vs digital) costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong Bounds on $r(x, y)$:** Writing $0 < r(x,y) < \infty$ instead of $0 \leq r(x,y) \leq 1$. This is a direct textbook definition; mark loss is guaranteed.
> 5. **Skipping Steps in Bandwidth Calculation:** Always show $M \times N \times k \times f_{fps}$ explicitly — do not collapse the calculation into a single line.
> 6. **Forgetting Internal Choice Declaration:** In KTU ESE, students must write "Choice A" or "Choice B" at the top. Missing this costs 0 marks but the paper may be wrongly graded.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Elements of DIP System"**

- ☐ A DIP system has **5 fundamental elements**: Image Acquisition, Mass Storage, Image Processing, Display, Communication.
- ☐ Acquisition uses **CCD/CMOS** sensors and a **digitiser** (sampler + quantizer).
- ☐ Storage follows a **3-tier hierarchy**: RAM → SSD/Disk → Archival media.
- ☐ Display devices include **monitors, LCDs, OLEDs, printers, plotters**.
- ☐ Communication uses **LAN, WAN, fibre, wireless networks**.
- ☐ The image function is modelled as $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$.
- ☐ Illumination bounds: $0 < i(x,y) < \infty$ (strict inequality).
- ☐ Reflectance bounds: $0 \leq r(x,y) \leq 1$ (closed interval).
- ☐ Image storage formula: $M \times N \times k$ **bits**.
- ☐ Grey levels formula: $L = 2^k$.
- ☐ Bandwidth formula: $W = M \times N \times k \times f_{fps}$ bits/second.
- ☐ Three levels of DIP: **Low-level** (image→image), **Mid-level** (image→features), **High-level** (features→semantics).
- ☐ Standard image sizes in KTU problems: $256 \times 256$, $512 \times 512$, $1024 \times 1024$, $2048 \times 2048$.
- ☐ Standard bit-depths: 1, 4, 8 (grayscale), 16, 24 (RGB colour).
- ☐ **Don't forget to convert units** (bits ↔ bytes ↔ KB ↔ MB ↔ GB) using powers of 2 (1024) for memory and powers of 10 (1000) for transmission bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Visual Perception, Simple Image Formation Model, Digital Camera Working Principles - Brightness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elements of Visual Perception, Simple Image Formation Model, Digital Camera Working Principles - Brightness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Elements of Visual Perception

**Visual Perception** is the brain's ability to interpret, organize, and make sense of the visual information captured by the human eye. In Digital Image Processing (DIP), understanding the Human Visual System (HVS) is critical because it defines *why* certain image processing operations (like contrast stretching or histogram equalization) are perceived as "good" by human observers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Visual perception in DIP refers to the physiological and psychological mechanisms by which the human eye captures light and the brain interprets it as a meaningful image. The two fundamental physical quantities involved are **luminance** (light intensity) and **photometric brightness** (perceived intensity).

#### Structural Components of the Human Eye

The human eye acts as a sophisticated optical instrument. The key anatomical components that directly influence image formation are:

1. **Cornea** — A transparent, dome-shaped outer layer that provides the primary refracting power (about **2/3** of the eye's total focusing ability).
2. **Iris** — The pigmented, muscular ring that controls the diameter of the pupil and thereby regulates the amount of light entering the eye. It is the eye's natural aperture stop.
3. **Pupil** — The variable-diameter opening in the centre of the iris (diameter ranges roughly from **2 mm** in bright light to **8 mm** in darkness).
4. **Lens** — A flexible, crystalline structure behind the pupil that changes shape (via the ciliary muscles) to focus light onto the retina. This process is called **accommodation**.
5. **Retina** — The light-sensitive inner lining at the back of the eyeball where the image is actually formed. It contains the photoreceptor cells.
6. **Photoreceptors** — Two specialized types:
   - **Cones** (≈ **6 to 7 million**) — Responsible for photopic (daylight) vision and color discrimination. Highly sensitive to detail.
   - **Rods** (≈ **75 to 150 million**) — Responsible for scotopic (low-light) vision. Provide overall brightness perception but no color/detail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in DIP:** Image quality metrics like sharpness, contrast, and noise tolerance are designed around HVS limitations. For instance, compression algorithms (JPEG) exploit the fact that the human eye is less sensitive to high-frequency chrominance than luminance.

#### Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Eye as a Camera:**
> Think of the human eye exactly like a digital camera. The **cornea + lens** is the camera lens (focuses light), the **iris + pupil** is the aperture (controls exposure), and the **retina** is the image sensor (CCD/CMOS). Just as a camera outputs a digital file, the retina outputs a neural signal to the brain (the "processor"). The famous saying in DIP is *"the best image processor is the human eye + brain combo."*

### 1.2 Brightness Adaptation & Discrimination

**Brightness** is the subjective perception of light intensity. It is *not* linearly proportional to the actual light intensity (luminance); rather, it is a logarithmic-like perceptual response.

**Brightness Adaptation** is the human eye's remarkable ability to adjust to a vast range of illumination levels (from starlight ≈ **0.001 cd/m²** to bright sunlight ≈ **10000 cd/m²**, spanning **10⁶** orders of magnitude). The eye accomplishes this through:
- **Pupillary reflex** (mechanical adjustment of light entry).
- **Chemical adaptation** (photoreceptor sensitivity change).
- **Neural adaptation** (brain-level processing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brightness Discrimination** refers to the smallest discernible change $\Delta I$ in luminance $I$ that a human observer can detect. It is governed empirically by **Weber's Law**:
> $$\frac{\Delta I}{I} \approx \text{constant} \approx 0.02$$
> This constant value of **0.02** (or 2%) holds for a wide mid-range of luminance. At very low or very high luminance, the ratio rises sharply, meaning the eye becomes less discriminative in extreme lighting conditions.

#### Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Whisper Test:**
> Imagine you are in a quiet room where someone whispers at volume $I$. You can easily hear a slight increase $\Delta I$. But if they suddenly shout at $1000 \times I$, you can no longer detect the same *absolute* $\Delta I$; you need a much louder jump. This is exactly Weber's Law — the *relative* (not absolute) change matters for perception.

### 1.3 Simple Image Formation Model

In DIP, an image is modeled as a **two-dimensional function $f(x, y)$** where $(x, y)$ are spatial (planar) coordinates and the value of $f$ at any point is proportional to the brightness (or intensity, or gray level) of the image at that point.

The **Simple Image Formation Model** (Gonzalez & Woods framework) decomposes $f(x, y)$ into two fundamental components:

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$

where:
- $i(x, y)$ = **Illumination component** — the amount of light incident on the scene from a light source.
- $r(x, y)$ = **Reflectance component** — the amount of light reflected by the objects in the scene.

The valid ranges are:
$$0 < i(x, y) < \infty \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \leq r(x, y) \leq 1$$

The product $f(x, y)$ represents the intensity captured by the sensor. A digital image is obtained by digitizing both spatial coordinates (sampling) and intensity values (quantization).

### 1.4 Digital Camera Working Principles

A **digital camera** is an electronic device that captures and stores images as digital data rather than on photographic film. Its core functional components mirror those of the human eye.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Operating Pipeline:** Light from a scene → Lens system (focusing) → Aperture (light regulation) → Shutter (exposure control) → Image Sensor (CCD/CMOS) (photoelectric conversion) → Analog Signal → ADC (digitization) → Digital Image Storage.

The two principal sensor technologies are:
- **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device)** — High-quality, low-noise, used in professional/machine vision systems.
- **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)** — Lower power, faster readout, integrated on-chip, used in smartphones and consumer cameras.

The **brightness** captured by a pixel is determined by:
$$\text{Pixel Intensity} \propto \text{Exposure Time} \times \text{Irradiance} \times \text{Sensor Responsivity}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Human Visual System — Operational Breakdown

The HVS processes light through the following sequential logic:

- **Step 1: Refraction** — Light enters through the cornea, which refracts it.
- **Step 2: Aperture Control** — The iris dynamically adjusts pupil diameter to regulate incoming flux.
- **Step 3: Focusing** — The lens changes shape (accommodation) to focus light onto the retina.
- **Step 4: Photo-conversion** — Photoreceptors (rods for brightness, cones for color) convert photons into neural electrical impulses.
- **Step 5: Transduction & Transmission** — Bipolar, ganglion, and optic nerve cells transmit signals to the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN).
- **Step 6: Cortical Processing** — The visual cortex (V1, V2, V4 areas) interprets edges, motion, depth, and color.

#### Why Each Step Matters
- Refraction determines geometric accuracy.
- Aperture control determines **dynamic range** (the eye's effective dynamic range spans **~160 dB**).
- Photo-conversion is the actual "digitization" step of biology (photons → electrons → neural spikes).

### 2.2 Mach Bands & Simultaneous Contrast

Two perceptual phenomena critical to image processing:

- **Mach Bands** — An optical illusion where bands of slightly different shades appear with exaggerated brightness differences at their boundaries. Caused by lateral inhibition in retinal neurons.
- **Simultaneous Contrast** — A region's perceived brightness depends on its surrounding context. A gray patch on a black background appears lighter than the same gray on a white background.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Mach bands and simultaneous contrast explain why simple linear gray-level transformations may produce visually unappealing results. They justify the use of perceptual models (e.g., gamma correction) in display pipelines.

### 2.3 Pinhole Imaging Model

The simplest mathematical model of image formation is the **pinhole camera**. It assumes a barrier with a tiny aperture such that each point in the scene projects to exactly one point on the imaging plane.

For a 3D scene point $(X, Y, Z)$ projected onto a 2D image plane at distance $f$ (focal length):

$$x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

This forms the foundation of **camera calibration**, **3D reconstruction**, and **photogrammetry**.

### 2.4 Digitization — Sampling and Quantization

A continuous image $f(x, y)$ becomes a digital image through:

1. **Sampling** — Discretization of spatial coordinates $(x, y)$ into a finite grid of $M \times N$ pixels.
2. **Quantization** — Discretization of the intensity value into a finite number of levels $L = 2^k$, where $k$ is the number of bits per pixel.

The total data size of a digital image is:
$$\text{Size} = M \times N \times k \quad \text{bits}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity / Definition** | **Equation / Relation** | **Units / Range** |
|------------|--------------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| $f(x, y)$ | Image intensity function | $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ | Gray-level value |
| $i(x, y)$ | Illumination component | $0 < i(x, y) < \infty$ | lux (lm/m²) |
| $r(x, y)$ | Reflectance component | $0 \leq r(x, y) \leq 1$ | dimensionless |
| $L$ | Number of gray levels | $L = 2^k$ | integer |
| $k$ | Bits per pixel | $k = \log_2 L$ | bits |
| $N_b$ | Image storage size | $N_b = M \cdot N \cdot k$ | bits |
| $\Delta I_c / I$ | Weber ratio (contrast sensitivity) | $\Delta I_c / I \approx 0.02$ | dimensionless |
| $B$ | Subjective brightness | $B \approx \log(I) + \text{const}$ | perceptual units |
| $x, y$ | Image plane projection | $x = f \cdot X / Z$, $y = f \cdot Y / Z$ | meters |
| $f$ | Focal length | camera/sensor parameter | millimeters |
| $f_\text{number}$ | F-stop / aperture ratio | $f_\text{number} = f / D$ | dimensionless |
| $E$ | Sensor irradiance | $E \propto \pi \cdot L \cdot \cos^4\theta / (4 \cdot f_\text{number}^2)$ | W/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Always remember to state the *range* of $i(x, y)$ and $r(x, y)$ separately and explicitly state that their product gives the final image intensity. Failing to mention the range is the most common cause of lost marks in 3-mark questions.

### 2.6 Engineering Applications

- **Medical Imaging** (MRI, CT, X-ray): Contrast enhancement algorithms mimic HVS luminance adaptation.
- **Remote Sensing**: Atmospheric correction uses the image formation model $f = i \cdot r$ to separate illumination from true ground reflectance.
- **HDRI Photography**: Captures the full HVS dynamic range using multiple exposures.
- **Computer Graphics / Rendering**: Physically-based rendering uses the same reflectance equation to simulate photorealistic scenes.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Camera calibration using pinhole models for depth estimation and SLAM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Image Formation Equation

We begin from first principles. Consider a 2D scene with illumination $i(x, y)$ arriving from a source and reflectance $r(x, y)$ characterizing the surface.

The light reflected from a surface element $dA$ is captured by the sensor. By the principle of conservation of energy:

$$\text{Total captured intensity} = \text{(incident illumination)} \times \text{(fraction reflected)}$$

In differential form for an infinitesimal surface element at $(x, y)$:

$$df(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot dr(x, y)$$

Integrating both sides over the surface gives:

$$\int df(x, y) = \int i(x, y) \cdot dr(x, y)$$

Assuming illumination is approximately uniform over the small surface region under consideration, we can factor it out:

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot \int dr(x, y)$$

By the definition of reflectance as the ratio of reflected to incident flux, the integral $\int dr(x, y)$ over a region converges to the local reflectance $r(x, y)$:

$$\boxed{f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)}$$

The constraints on the two factors are:
$$0 < i(x, y) < \infty \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \leq r(x, y) \leq 1$$

This means the minimum of $f(x, y)$ is **0** (no light reflected, e.g., a perfect black body), and the maximum is theoretically unbounded but practically capped by the illumination $i(x, y)$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Pinhole Camera Projection

Consider a pinhole camera with focal length $f$. A 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ is projected onto a 2D image plane at distance $f$ from the pinhole.

By similar triangles formed by the projection ray:

The horizontal projection ratio: $\dfrac{x}{f} = \dfrac{X}{Z}$

The vertical projection ratio: $\dfrac{y}{f} = \dfrac{Y}{Z}$

Solving each for the image coordinates:

$$\boxed{x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}}$$

In matrix form using homogeneous coordinates:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \frac{1}{Z} \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Weber's Law (Empirical)

Empirically, the minimum perceivable intensity change $\Delta I$ at background intensity $I$ is observed to follow:

$$\Delta I = k \cdot I$$

where $k$ is the **Weber fraction**, an experimentally determined constant. The ratio is:

$$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k = \text{constant}$$

For a wide range of mid-level luminance, $k \approx 0.02$ (2%). Dividing both sides by $I$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta I}{I} \approx 0.02}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Image Storage Size

For a digital image of resolution $M \times N$ pixels with $k$ bits per pixel:

$$\text{Total bits} = (\text{number of pixels}) \times (\text{bits per pixel})$$

$$\boxed{N_b = M \cdot N \cdot k \text{ bits}}$$

Converting to bytes: $N_b / 8$ bytes. To kilobytes: $N_b / (8 \times 1024)$ KB.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A digital camera captures an image at $1920 \times 1080$ resolution with 8 bits/pixel grayscale. Calculate (a) the number of gray levels, (b) total file size, (c) whether the file fits in a 2 MB memory card.

**(a) Number of gray levels:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 2^k \\
L &= 2^8 \\
L &= 256 \text{ levels}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) File size in bits:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_b &= M \cdot N \cdot k \\
N_b &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 8 \\
N_b &= 16,588,800 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Convert to MB:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Size in bytes} &= \frac{16,588,800}{8} = 2,073,600 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Size in MB} &= \frac{2,073,600}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 1.977 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, the file (≈ 1.977 MB) fits in a 2 MB card.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Aspect** | **Human Eye** | **Digital Camera** |
|------------|--------------|--------------------|
| Lens | Cornea + Crystalline lens | Glass/Plastic optical lens |
| Aperture | Iris / Pupil (2–8 mm) | Mechanical iris (f/1.4 to f/22) |
| Shutter | Eyelid + Neural integration | Mechanical or electronic shutter |
| Sensor | Retina (rods + cones) | CCD or CMOS array |
| Resolution | ≈ 576 megapixels (theoretical) | Typically 12–100 MP (modern) |
| Dynamic Range | ≈ 160 dB | ≈ 60–90 dB (HDR extends this) |
| Output | Neural spikes | Digital bits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Human Visual System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Scene With Light Source] --> A1[Cornea Refraction]
    A1 --> A2[Iris And Pupil Aperture Control]
    A2 --> A3[Crystalline Lens Accommodation Focusing]
    A3 --> A4[Retina Image Plane]
    A4 --> A5A[Cones Photopic Color Vision]
    A4 --> A5B[Rods Scotopic Brightness Vision]
    A5A --> A6[Bipolar And Ganglion Cells]
    A5B --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Optic Nerve Transmission]
    A7 --> A8[LGN Thalamic Relay]
    A8 --> A9[Visual Cortex V1 V2 V4 Interpretation]
    A9 --> A10[Perceived Image In Brain]
```

### 4.2 Image Formation Model Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Light Source Illumination i of x y] --> B1[Scene Surface Reflectance r of x y]
    B1 --> B2[Reflected Light Intensity f of x y]
    B2 --> B3[Camera Lens Optical Focusing]
    B3 --> B4[Aperture And Shutter Exposure Control]
    B4 --> B5[CCD Or CMOS Sensor Photoelectric Conversion]
    B5 --> B6[Analog Electrical Signal]
    B6 --> B7[Analog To Digital Converter ADC]
    B7 --> B8[Sampling Spatial Discretization M x N]
    B8 --> B9[Quantization Intensity Discretization L Levels]
    B9 --> B10[Digital Image Output Pixel Array]
```

### 4.3 Brightness Perception and Weber Law Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Incident Luminance I] --> C1[Retinal Photoreceptor Response]
    C1 --> C2[Neural Signal To Cortex]
    C2 --> C3[Subjective Brightness B Approximately Log I]
    C3 --> C4{Brightness Change Delta I Detectable}
    C4 --> C5[Weber Ratio Delta I divided by I Approximately 0.02]
    C5 --> C6[Just Noticeable Difference JND]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Explain the simple image formation model used in digital image processing. State the range of illumination and reflectance components.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The simple image formation model expresses a 2D image as a function of two multiplicative components:

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$

where $i(x, y)$ is the **illumination** component representing the amount of light incident on the scene from a light source, and $r(x, y)$ is the **reflectance** component representing the fraction of incident light reflected by the objects.

- Illumination range: $0 < i(x, y) < \infty$
- Reflectance range: $0 \leq r(x, y) \leq 1$

This model assumes that the total image intensity at any point is the product of incident light and surface reflectivity. (3 marks)

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
Define brightness adaptation and brightness discrimination. State Weber's Law.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Brightness Adaptation:** The ability of the human visual system to adjust its sensitivity to operate over a very wide range of luminance levels (from ≈ **0.001 cd/m²** to ≈ **10000 cd/m²**). This is achieved by the pupil reflex, chemical adaptation of photoreceptors, and neural adaptation.
- **Brightness Discrimination:** The smallest change in luminance $\Delta I$ that the human eye can detect at a given background intensity $I$.
- **Weber's Law:** $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \approx 0.02$ — the ratio of the just-perceivable change to the background intensity is approximately constant (about 2%) over a wide mid-range of illumination. (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the structure and functioning of the human eye as an image acquisition system. Compare its components with those of a digital camera. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, derive the pinhole camera imaging equations. Explain how they are used in camera calibration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structure of the Human Eye (7 Marks):**

The human eye is a nearly spherical organ (~20 mm diameter) that functions like a high-performance camera.

| **Component** | **Function** | **Camera Equivalent** |
|---|---|---|
| Cornea | Primary refraction | Lens front element |
| Iris | Controls pupil diameter (2–8 mm) | Aperture diaphragm |
| Pupil | Variable aperture opening | Aperture opening |
| Crystalline lens | Variable focus (accommodation) | Focusing lens group |
| Retina | Light-sensitive image plane | CCD/CMOS sensor |
| Rods (~120M) | Brightness (scotopic) vision | Monochrome pixels |
| Cones (~7M) | Color (photopic) vision | Color filter array pixels |
| Optic nerve | Signal transmission | Data bus to memory |

Light enters through the cornea, is focused by the lens onto the retina. The retina's photoreceptors convert photons into electrical signals, which travel via the optic nerve to the visual cortex for interpretation. The fovea (a small depression at the centre of the retina) contains the highest density of cones and is responsible for sharp central vision.

**Comparison:** Both systems have an aperture (iris/aperture stop), a variable-focus lens (crystalline lens/optical lens), a photosensitive surface (retina/CCD/CMOS), and a method of converting light into recordable signals (neural impulses/digital bits). The eye has superior dynamic range (~160 dB vs ~70 dB) but the camera has superior spatial resolution in captured output.

**(b) Pinhole Camera Model (7 Marks):**

Consider a pinhole camera where the aperture is a single point at the origin. A 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects onto a 2D image plane located at distance $f$ (focal length) behind the pinhole.

By similar triangles formed between the projection ray and the optical axis:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{x}{f} &= \frac{X}{Z} \\
\frac{y}{f} &= \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for image coordinates:

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \\
y &= f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}$$

In homogeneous matrix form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \frac{1}{Z} \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Use in Camera Calibration:** The intrinsic matrix containing $f$ (and additional parameters like principal point and skew) is determined by observing known calibration targets (e.g., checkerboards). Once calibrated, the equations allow reconstruction of 3D scene geometry from 2D images, depth estimation, and rectification of lens distortion in computer vision pipelines.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with relevant equations the simple image formation model. Show how a grayscale image's intensity range is bounded. Compute the storage size for a $4096 \times 2160$ image with 12 bits per pixel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define brightness adaptation. Discuss Mach bands and simultaneous contrast with diagrams and their significance in image processing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Image Formation Model and Storage (7 Marks):**

The image intensity at point $(x, y)$ is:

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$

**Bounds derivation:**

Since illumination is strictly positive and physically finite, and reflectance is between 0 (perfect black) and 1 (perfect white/mirror):

$$0 < i(x, y) < \infty \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \leq r(x, y) \leq 1$$

Therefore:

$$0 \leq f(x, y) < \infty$$

In practice, the sensor saturates at a maximum intensity $f_\text{max} = i_\text{max} \cdot 1 = i_\text{max}$. For a $k$-bit digital image, intensity is quantized to $L = 2^k$ discrete levels in the range $[0, L-1]$.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $M = 4096$, $N = 2160$, $k = 12$ bits/pixel.

Number of gray levels:

$$L = 2^k = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

Total file size:

$$N_b = M \cdot N \cdot k = 4096 \times 2160 \times 12$$

$$N_b = 106,168,320 \text{ bits}$$

Converting to megabytes:

$$\text{Size} = \frac{106,168,320}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} \approx 12.66 \text{ MB}$$

**(b) Brightness Adaptation and Visual Phenomena (7 Marks):**

**Brightness Adaptation:** The HVS can perceive an enormous range of luminance values, from ≈ 0.001 to ≈ 10,000 cd/m² (a range of 10⁷). The eye cannot simultaneously discriminate over this entire range; instead, it adapts to a current operating range (the current adaptation level). Subjective brightness $B$ is an approximately logarithmic function of actual luminance $I$:

$$B \approx k_1 \cdot \log(I) + k_2$$

**Mach Bands:** When bands of constant but different luminance are placed adjacent to each other, the human eye exaggerates the brightness difference near the boundaries, perceiving dark and light strips that are not physically present. This is due to **lateral inhibition** between neighboring retinal neurons.

*Mach band effect (ASCII schematic):*

```
True intensity:        Perceived intensity:
   ░░░▒▒▒               ░░░░▒▒▒▒
```
(Light/dark illusion at edges)

**Simultaneous Contrast:** The perceived brightness of a region depends on the luminance of its surroundings. A gray patch on a dark background appears brighter than the identical gray patch on a light background.

*Simultaneous contrast (ASCII schematic):*

```
Background: Black      Background: White
   ████                   ████
   ▓▓██                   ██▓▓
   ████                   ████
(Same gray ▓ looks lighter on left)
```

**Significance in DIP:**
- Mach bands justify the use of **unsharp masking** and **edge-aware filtering** to enhance perceived sharpness.
- Simultaneous contrast explains why **histogram equalization** dramatically improves visual quality—it remaps the local intensity distribution relative to context.
- Gamma correction ($\gamma < 1$) compensates for the eye's nonlinear brightness response so that stored digital values match perceived brightness.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the range constraints** on $i(x, y)$ and $r(x, y)$ will cost you 1 mark in 3-mark questions. Always write them separately.
> - **Mixing up rods and cones:** Cones = color/day vision (~7 million); Rods = brightness/night vision (~120 million). Examiners look for this distinction explicitly.
> - **Confusing luminance with brightness:** Luminance is a physical quantity (cd/m²); Brightness is the *subjective perceptual* response. Don't interchange them.
> - **Wrong units in storage problems:** $N_b$ is in **bits**, not bytes. Always convert explicitly with a clear step.
> - **Skipping the derivation step** in pinhole camera questions: Examiners want the similar-triangle argument, not just the final equation.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The human eye is essentially a **camera analogue**: cornea = lens, iris/pupil = aperture, retina = sensor.
- ✅ Photoreceptor distribution: **Cones ≈ 7M** (color, photopic); **Rods ≈ 120M** (brightness, scotopic).
- ✅ Brightness perception is **logarithmic**, not linear, in luminance.
- ✅ **Weber's Law:** $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} \approx 0.02$ (2% discrimination threshold).
- ✅ **Image formation equation:** $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$.
- ✅ Illumination range: $0 < i(x, y) < \infty$. Reflectance range: $0 \leq r(x, y) \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Pinhole projection:** $x = fX/Z$, $y = fY/Z$.
- ✅ **Number of gray levels:** $L = 2^k$. **Storage size:** $N_b = M \cdot N \cdot k$ bits.
- ✅ Digital camera components: **Lens + Aperture + Shutter + CCD/CMOS sensor + ADC**.
- ✅ **Mach bands** and **simultaneous contrast** arise from lateral inhibition in the retina.
- ✅ Brightness adaptation is the eye's ability to operate over a luminance range of **≈ 10⁶ to 10⁷**.
- ✅ Photopic vision uses **cones**; scotopic vision uses **rods**; mesopic vision uses both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Contrast

**Contrast** in digital image processing is formally defined as the **measure of the difference in visual properties (specifically luminance or intensity) that makes an object (or its representation in an image) distinguishable from other objects and the background**. It is the difference between the highest and lowest intensity values, or more rigorously, the ratio of luminance difference to the average luminance of the surrounding region.

Mathematically, for a digital image $f(x, y)$, the global contrast $C$ over an image region is expressed as:

$$C = \frac{I_{max} - I_{min}}{I_{max} + I_{min}}$$

where $I_{max}$ and $I_{min}$ are the maximum and minimum intensity values in the region of interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Conceptual):** Contrast is the difference between the maximum and minimum pixel intensity values in an image, and contrast manipulation techniques (stretching, histogram equalization) aim to redistribute these intensity values to utilize the full dynamic range of the display medium.

## 1.2 Types of Contrast

| Type | Definition | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Global Contrast** | Contrast computed over the entire image using the global maximum and minimum intensities | General image enhancement, batch processing |
| **Local Contrast** | Contrast computed within a small neighborhood (kernel/window) around each pixel | Detail enhancement, edge sharpening |
| **Michelson Contrast** | $\frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{L_{max} + L_{min}}$ based on luminance $L$ | Periodic patterns (sinusoidal gratings) |
| **Weber Contrast** | $\frac{\Delta L}{L_{background}}$ for an object against a uniform background | Vision science, threshold detection |
| **RMS Contrast** | Standard deviation of pixel intensities | Statistical characterization of textures |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Foggy Window:** Imagine you are looking at a beautiful mountain landscape through a foggy window. The mountains (objects) and the sky (background) appear almost in the same shade of grey — they are **low contrast**. Now imagine wiping the window clean on a bright sunny day. Suddenly, the dark green forests, white snow caps, and blue sky all stand out distinctly. This is **high contrast**. Contrast manipulation in DIP is essentially the digital equivalent of "wiping the window clean" — it redistributes pixel intensities so that dark pixels become darker, bright pixels become brighter, and the visual difference between regions is amplified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examination Insight:** Examiners frequently test three core contrast operations: **(1) Linear Contrast Stretching**, **(2) Piecewise Linear Contrast Stretching (Min-Max / Percentile clipping)**, and **(3) Histogram Equalization**. A thorough understanding of the transformation function $s = T(r)$ and the cumulative distribution function (CDF) is mandatory for full marks in 14-mark questions.

## 1.4 Low Contrast vs High Contrast — Visual Distinction

A **low contrast image** exhibits a histogram concentrated in a narrow range of gray levels (e.g., 80–120 in an 8-bit system), making features indistinguishable. A **high contrast image** has pixel intensities spread across the full range (0 to 255), providing clear visual differentiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Metric:** The **dynamic range** of a display medium (8-bit: 256 levels, 16-bit: 65536 levels) determines the theoretical maximum achievable contrast. For 8-bit grayscale images, $C_{max} = \frac{255 - 0}{255 + 0} = 1$ (100%).
</br>

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Contrast Manipulation is Required

When an image is acquired under poor lighting conditions, with a faulty sensor, or due to incorrect exposure settings, the histogram becomes compressed into a narrow band. The result is a **washed-out, dull image** where details are obscured. Contrast manipulation redistributes pixel intensities to span the entire available dynamic range, thereby enhancing visual information.

## 2.2 Linear Contrast Stretching

### 2.2.1 Operational Concept
Linear contrast stretching is the simplest form of contrast enhancement. It applies a linear transformation function $s = T(r)$ that maps the original intensity range $[r_1, r_2]$ to a new (typically full) output range $[s_1, s_2]$.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Identify the minimum and maximum intensity values in the input image, $r_{min}$ and $r_{max}$.
- **Step 2:** Define the desired output range. By default, for an 8-bit image, this is $[s_1, s_2] = [0, 255]$.
- **Step 3:** Apply the linear transformation to every pixel $r$ in the image.
- **Step 4:** Clip any out-of-range values (saturate to 0 or 255) to prevent artifacts.

### 2.2.3 Why Linear Stretching Works
The linear function $T(r) = a \cdot r + b$ preserves the relative ordering of pixel intensities while uniformly expanding the dynamic range. The slope $a = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}$ determines the stretching factor, and the intercept $b = s_1 - a \cdot r_1$ shifts the result into the desired output range.

## 2.3 Piecewise Linear Contrast Stretching

When specific intensity ranges require selective enhancement (e.g., darkening shadows, brightening highlights), a piecewise linear function is used. The intensity axis is divided into multiple intervals, each with its own linear mapping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use Case:** In medical imaging (X-rays, MRI), piecewise stretching is used to enhance subtle tissue variations in specific gray-level windows (e.g., the "lung window" or "bone window" in CT scans).

## 2.4 Histogram Equalization (Global)

### 2.4.1 Conceptual Foundation
Histogram equalization is a non-linear contrast enhancement technique that redistributes pixel intensities such that the output image has a **uniform (flat) histogram**. This is achieved by using the **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** of the input image as the transformation function.

### 2.4.2 Operational Logic
- **Step 1:** Compute the histogram $h(r_k)$ of the input image — the number of pixels at each intensity level $r_k$.
- **Step 2:** Normalize the histogram to obtain the Probability Density Function (PDF): $p(r_k) = \frac{h(r_k)}{MN}$, where $M \times N$ is the total number of pixels.
- **Step 3:** Compute the Cumulative Distribution Function: $CDF(r_k) = \sum_{j=0}^{k} p(r_j)$.
- **Step 4:** Map each input intensity $r_k$ to a new output intensity $s_k$ using the transformation $s_k = (L - 1) \cdot CDF(r_k)$, where $L$ is the total number of gray levels.
- **Step 5:** Round $s_k$ to the nearest valid integer and reconstruct the image.

### 2.4.3 Why Histogram Equalization Works
The CDF is a monotonically non-decreasing function with values in $[0, 1]$. Multiplying by $(L-1)$ and rounding maps the distribution to a uniform spread across the gray levels, effectively maximizing the entropy (information content) of the output image.

## 2.5 Adaptive (Local) Histogram Equalization

In global histogram equalization, a single transformation is applied to the entire image. This may cause over-enhancement in some regions and under-enhancement in others. **Local histogram equalization** computes the CDF over a moving window (neighborhood) around each pixel, providing region-specific enhancement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Global HE uses one CDF for the whole image. Local (Adaptive) HE uses a sliding window. CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive HE) additionally clips the histogram to a predefined limit to prevent noise amplification.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Medical Imaging** | Enhancing X-ray, MRI, CT scan features for diagnosis |
| **Satellite Remote Sensing** | Improving visibility of terrain features in low-light images |
| **Forensic Science** | Enhancing surveillance footage to identify suspects |
| **Industrial Inspection** | Detecting surface defects in manufactured products |
| **Astronomy** | Revealing faint celestial objects in deep-sky photographs |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Enhancing road scene visibility under varying lighting conditions |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C = \frac{I_{max} - I_{min}}{I_{max} + I_{min}}$ | Michelson Contrast Formula | Dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$ |
| $s = \frac{(r - r_{min}) \cdot (s_{max} - s_{min})}{r_{max} - r_{min}} + s_{min}$ | Linear Contrast Stretching (Min-Max Normalization) | $r, s$ are intensity values |
| $p(r_k) = \frac{n_k}{MN}$ | Normalized Histogram (PDF) | $\sum p(r_k) = 1$ |
| $CDF(r_k) = \sum_{j=0}^{k} p(r_j)$ | Cumulative Distribution Function | $CDF \in [0, 1]$ |
| $s_k = (L-1) \cdot CDF(r_k)$ | Histogram Equalization Transform | $s_k \in [0, L-1]$ |
| $T(r) = \int_{0}^{r} p_r(w) \, dw$ | Continuous Form of HE Transform (CDF Integral) | Continuous variable $r$ |
| $s_{k+1} = s_k + p(r_k) \cdot (L-1)$ | Recursive HE Computation (preferred for coding) | Integer output |
| $r_{new} = \text{round}\left[\frac{r - r_{min}}{r_{max} - r_{min}} \times 255\right]$ | Min-Max Stretching (8-bit output) | $r_{new} \in [0, 255]$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **Histogram Specification (Matching)** with **Histogram Equalization**. HE is a special case of Histogram Specification where the target histogram is uniform. Specification requires an additional inverse CDF mapping step.
</br>

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Histogram Equalization Transformation Function

We begin with the continuous case and then discretize for digital implementation. Let $r$ denote the input gray level and $s$ the output gray level, both in the range $[0, L-1]$.

### Step 1: Define the Transformation Function
We seek a transformation $s = T(r)$ that produces a uniform probability density function for $s$. A fundamental result from probability theory states that if $T(r)$ is the cumulative distribution function of $r$, then $s$ will be uniformly distributed on $[0, 1]$.

### Step 2: Apply the CDF Integral
The continuous CDF is defined as:

$$
T(r) = \int_{0}^{r} p_r(w) \, dw
$$

where $p_r(w)$ is the probability density function of the input gray levels.

### Step 3: Justify the Uniformity of Output
Differentiating both sides with respect to $r$:

$$
\frac{ds}{dr} = p_r(r)
$$

By the theorem for transformation of random variables, the PDF of $s$ is:

$$
p_s(s) = p_r(r) \cdot \left\vert \frac{dr}{ds} \right\vert = p_r(r) \cdot \frac{1}{p_r(r)} = 1
$$

This confirms $p_s(s) = 1$ for $s \in [0, 1]$, i.e., $s$ is uniformly distributed.

### Step 4: Discretize for Digital Images
For a digital image with $L$ gray levels ($L = 256$ for 8-bit), the discrete HE transformation becomes:

$$
s_k = T(r_k) = (L - 1) \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{k} p_r(r_j) = (L - 1) \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{k} \frac{n_j}{MN}
$$

where $n_j$ is the number of pixels with intensity $r_j$, and $MN$ is the total pixel count.

### Step 5: Simplify to Recursive Form
The above can be computed recursively to avoid redundant summation:

$$
s_0 = (L-1) \cdot p_r(r_0)
$$

$$
s_{k+1} = s_k + (L-1) \cdot p_r(r_{k+1})
$$

This form is computationally efficient and is the standard implementation in MATLAB's `histeq()` function.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Histogram Equalization (3-bit, 8-level image)

Consider a $64 \times 64$ pixel, 3-bit image ($L = 8$, so $L-1 = 7$) with the following intensity distribution:

| Intensity $r_k$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Pixel Count $n_k$ | 790 | 1023 | 850 | 656 | 329 | 245 | 122 | 81 |
| PDF $p(r_k) = n_k / 4096$ | 0.193 | 0.250 | 0.207 | 0.160 | 0.080 | 0.060 | 0.030 | 0.020 |
| CDF (Cumulative) | 0.193 | 0.443 | 0.650 | 0.810 | 0.890 | 0.950 | 0.980 | 1.000 |
| $s_k = 7 \times CDF$ | 1.35 | 3.10 | 4.55 | 5.67 | 6.23 | 6.65 | 6.86 | 7.00 |
| Rounded $s_k$ | **1** | **3** | **5** | **6** | **6** | **7** | **7** | **7** |

**Verification:** The total pixel count $790 + 1023 + 850 + 656 + 329 + 245 + 122 + 81 = 4096 = 64 \times 64$ ✓

**Output Histogram Verification:**
- Intensity 1: 790 pixels
- Intensity 3: 1023 pixels
- Intensity 5: 850 pixels
- Intensity 6: 656 + 329 = 985 pixels
- Intensity 7: 245 + 122 + 81 = 448 pixels

The output image now uses gray levels 1, 3, 5, 6, and 7 — covering a wider, more uniformly distributed range than the heavily skewed input.

## 3.3 Derivation of Linear Contrast Stretching Equation

Given an input image with intensity range $[r_1, r_2]$ that needs to be mapped to $[s_1, s_2]$, the linear transformation is:

$$
s = T(r) = \left(\frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}\right)(r - r_1) + s_1
$$

**Derivation Steps:**

### Step 1: Define the Slope
The slope of the linear transformation determines the stretching factor:

$$
a = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Point-Slope Form
Using the point-slope form of a line passing through $(r_1, s_1)$ with slope $a$:

$$
s - s_1 = a \cdot (r - r_1)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Slope

$$
s = \left(\frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}\right)(r - r_1) + s_1
$$

### Step 4: Simplify (Min-Max Stretching Special Case)
For the most common case where $[s_1, s_2] = [0, 255]$ and $[r_1, r_2] = [r_{min}, r_{max}]$:

$$
s = \frac{255}{r_{max} - r_{min}}(r - r_{min})
$$

This is the standard Min-Max Normalization formula used universally in image preprocessing.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Contrast Techniques

| Parameter | Linear Stretching | Histogram Equalization | Adaptive HE (CLAHE) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transformation Type** | Linear, global | Non-linear, global | Non-linear, local |
| **Computational Cost** | Very Low | Low | High |
| **Enhancement Quality** | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| **Noise Amplification** | Minimal | Moderate | Controlled (clipped) |
| **Preserves Brightness** | Yes | No (may alter mean) | Partially |
| **Best Use Case** | Quick contrast fix | General purpose | Medical/scientific images |
| **Output Histogram** | Linear copy of input | Approximately uniform | Locally uniform |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When a 14-mark question asks "explain contrast stretching with an example," the expected answer must include: (1) the transformation equation, (2) identification of input/output ranges, (3) a numerical worked example, and (4) a clear sketch of the transformation function $T(r)$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Linear Contrast Stretching Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Image f_xy] --> B[Scan Pixel Intensities]
    B --> C[Find r_min and r_max]
    C --> D[Compute Slope a = 255 divided by r_max minus r_min]
    D --> E[Apply Transform s = a times r minus r_min]
    E --> F{Value out of 0 to 255 range}
    F -->|Yes| G[Clip to 0 or 255]
    F -->|No| H[Store Output Pixel]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Output Image g_xy]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Histogram Equalization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image f_xy] --> B[Compute Histogram h_rk]
    B --> C[Normalize to PDF pr_rk = nk divided by MN]
    C --> D[Compute CDF by Cumulative Sum]
    D --> E[Multiply CDF by L minus 1]
    E --> F[Round to nearest integer sk]
    F --> G[Build LUT Lookup Table]
    G --> H[Map each input pixel rk to sk]
    H --> I[Output Image g_xy with uniform histogram]
```

## 4.3 Transformation Function Visualization — Linear vs Piecewise vs HE

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LINEAR["LINEAR CONTRAST STRETCHING"]
        L1["r: 80 to 120 range"] --> L2["s: 0 to 255 range"]
        L2 --> L3["Single Straight Line Mapping"]
    end

    subgraph PIECEWISE["PIECEWISE LINEAR STRETCHING"]
        P1["r: 0 to 80 low slope"] --> P2["r: 80 to 180 high slope"]
        P2 --> P3["r: 180 to 255 low slope"]
    end

    subgraph HISTEQ["HISTOGRAM EQUALIZATION"]
        H1["r: Compressed input range"] --> H2["CDF based Non-linear S-curve"]
        H2 --> H3["s: Expanded full range uniform output"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Contrast Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Image has low contrast] --> Q1{Is histogram narrowly concentrated}
    Q1 -->|Yes, single peak| Q2{Is the image globally or locally degraded}
    Q1 -->|No, bimodal or wide| Q3[Use Piecewise Linear Stretching for selective regions]
    Q2 -->|Globally| Q4[Apply Global Histogram Equalization]
    Q2 -->|Locally varying| Q5[Apply Adaptive CLAHE with clip limit]
    Q4 --> Q6{Is noise acceptable}
    Q6 -->|Yes| End1[Output Enhanced Image]
    Q6 -->|No| Q5
    Q5 --> End1
    Q3 --> End1
```

## 4.5 Histogram State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Input: Narrow Concentrated Histogram] -->|HE Transform| S2[Output: Flat Uniform Histogram]
    S1 -->|Linear Stretch| S3[Output: Stretched but Same Shape]
    S1 -->|Piecewise| S4[Output: Selectively Reshaped Histogram]
    S2 --> S5[Enhanced Image with Maximum Entropy]
    S3 --> S6[Enhanced Image with Preserved Distribution]
    S4 --> S7[Enhanced Image with Targeted Regions Boosted]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation Guide:** In the diagrams above, notice that histogram equalization transforms the shape of the histogram to be approximately flat, while linear stretching merely scales the horizontal axis. This is why HE provides more aggressive enhancement but may introduce artifacts, whereas linear stretching is safer for production pipelines.
</br>

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Contrast Stretching
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Contrast stretching is a point processing technique in spatial domain image enhancement that expands the range of intensity levels in an image to utilize the full dynamic range of the display medium. It applies a piecewise linear transformation function $s = T(r)$ to map a narrow input intensity range $[r_1, r_2]$ to a wider output range $[s_1, s_2]$, thereby increasing the visual difference between dark and bright regions. The simplest form is min-max stretching: $s = \frac{(r - r_{min}) \times 255}{r_{max} - r_{min}}$.

*(Examiner expects: definition + transformation equation + 1-line significance — total 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: What is Histogram Equalization? State its Purpose.
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Histogram Equalization (HE) is a non-linear contrast enhancement technique that redistributes the intensity values of an image so that the output histogram is approximately uniform. Its purpose is to maximize the visual information content (entropy) of the image by spreading out the most frequent intensity values.

The transformation function is the normalized cumulative distribution function: $s_k = (L-1) \sum_{j=0}^{k} p_r(r_j)$, where $p_r(r_j) = \frac{n_j}{MN}$ is the probability of intensity $r_j$.

*(Examiner expects: definition + purpose + CDF formula — total 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**Question:** With a neat sketch of the transformation function, explain **linear contrast stretching** and **piecewise linear contrast stretching** in detail. Illustrate with a suitable numerical example for each technique. **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Linear Contrast Stretching — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** Linear contrast stretching is a spatial domain point operation that applies a linear transformation to remap the input intensity range $[r_1, r_2]$ to a desired output range $[s_1, s_2]$.

**Step 2 — Transformation Equation:**

$$
s = T(r) = \left(\frac{s_2 - s_1}{r_2 - r_1}\right)(r - r_1) + s_1
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**
Consider an 8-bit image with $r_1 = 80$, $r_2 = 160$, and we want to stretch to $[s_1, s_2] = [0, 255]$.

Slope: $a = \frac{255 - 0}{160 - 80} = \frac{255}{80} = 3.1875$

For input pixel $r = 100$:
$$
s = 3.1875 \times (100 - 80) = 3.1875 \times 20 = 63.75 \approx 64
$$

For input pixel $r = 120$:
$$
s = 3.1875 \times (120 - 80) = 3.1875 \times 40 = 127.5 \approx 128
$$

For input pixel $r = 140$:
$$
s = 3.1875 \times (140 - 80) = 3.1875 \times 60 = 191.25 \approx 191
$$

**Step 4 — Sketch Description:** The transformation function is a single straight line from $(80, 0)$ to $(160, 255)$, with slope $3.1875$.

#### Part (b): Piecewise Linear Contrast Stretching — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition:** Piecewise linear contrast stretching divides the input intensity axis into multiple intervals, each with its own linear mapping. This allows selective enhancement of specific gray-level ranges.

**Step 2 — Numerical Example:**
Consider an 8-bit image with three regions:
- Region 1: $r \in [0, 70] \rightarrow s \in [0, 50]$ (slope = $50/70 \approx 0.714$)
- Region 2: $r \in [70, 140] \rightarrow s \in [50, 200]$ (slope = $150/70 \approx 2.143$)
- Region 3: $r \in [140, 255] \rightarrow s \in [200, 255]$ (slope = $55/115 \approx 0.478$)

**Step 3 — Calculation for $r = 100$ (in Region 2):**

$$
s = 50 + 2.143 \times (100 - 70) = 50 + 64.29 = 114.29 \approx 114
$$

**Step 4 — Sketch Description:** The transformation is a polyline with three connected segments, each with a different slope, allowing the middle gray levels (skin tones, mid-range features) to be expanded more aggressively than the shadows or highlights.

**Conclusion:** Piecewise stretching provides flexible, region-specific enhancement at the cost of requiring manual specification of break points.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**Question:** Explain the **Histogram Equalization** technique in detail. Derive the transformation function and perform histogram equalization on a $64 \times 64$, 8-level (3-bit) image with the following pixel intensity distribution:

| $r_k$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $n_k$ | 1020 | 850 | 600 | 450 | 350 | 200 | 50 | 0 |

**Show all steps clearly. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Theory and Derivation — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept:** Histogram equalization transforms an image such that its output histogram is approximately uniform, maximizing entropy and visual contrast.

**Step 2 — Continuous Derivation:**
Let $p_r(r)$ be the input PDF. The transformation $s = T(r) = \int_0^r p_r(w) dw$ (the CDF) ensures $s$ is uniformly distributed on $[0, 1]$.

**Proof:** By the change-of-variables theorem:

$$
p_s(s) = p_r(r) \left\vert \frac{dr}{ds} \right\vert = p_r(r) \cdot \frac{1}{p_r(r)} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Discrete Form:**

$$
s_k = T(r_k) = (L-1) \sum_{j=0}^{k} p_r(r_j) = (L-1) \sum_{j=0}^{k} \frac{n_j}{MN}
$$

where $L = 8$, $L-1 = 7$, $MN = 64 \times 64 = 4096$.

#### Part (b): Numerical Computation — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Verify Total Pixels:**
$1020 + 850 + 600 + 450 + 350 + 200 + 50 + 0 = 3520$... 

**Note for examiner:** The problem provides $n_k$ values totaling 3520, which is less than $4096$. The remaining 576 pixels can be assumed to have intensity 0, making $n_0 = 1020 + 576 = 1596$, OR the student should explicitly state the assumption. *(For full marks, the student must show awareness of this discrepancy.)*

**Step 2 — Assume total = 4096 by adding 576 to $n_0$:**

| $r_k$ | $n_k$ (adjusted) | $p_r(r_k)$ | CDF | $s_k = 7 \times CDF$ | Rounded $s_k$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 1596 | 0.3896 | 0.3896 | 2.727 | **3** |
| 1 | 850 | 0.2075 | 0.5972 | 4.180 | **4** |
| 2 | 600 | 0.1465 | 0.7437 | 5.206 | **5** |
| 3 | 450 | 0.1099 | 0.8535 | 5.975 | **6** |
| 4 | 350 | 0.0854 | 0.9390 | 6.573 | **7** |
| 5 | 200 | 0.0488 | 0.9878 | 6.915 | **7** |
| 6 | 50 | 0.0122 | 1.0000 | 7.000 | **7** |
| 7 | 0 | 0.0000 | 1.0000 | 7.000 | **7** |

**Step 3 — Output Histogram:**
- $s = 3$: 1596 pixels
- $s = 4$: 850 pixels
- $s = 5$: 600 pixels
- $s = 6$: 450 pixels
- $s = 7$: 350 + 200 + 50 + 0 = 600 pixels

**Step 4 — Verification:** Total output pixels = $1596 + 850 + 600 + 450 + 600 = 4096$ ✓

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The output image now spans gray levels 3 through 7 (instead of the input's 0 through 6), with a more balanced distribution, indicating successful contrast enhancement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply CDF by $(L-1)$:** Many students write $s_k = CDF(r_k)$ directly, forgetting the scaling factor. This results in output values between 0 and 1, which is wrong. **Deduction: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Not rounding $s_k$ to integers:** The output must be an integer gray level. Forgetting to round loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing PDF with CDF:** Students often use the histogram $n_k$ instead of the normalized PDF $n_k/MN$ in the CDF computation. **Deduction: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Skipping the sketch of the transformation function:** The transformation curve $T(r)$ is a mandatory visual element for full marks. **Deduction: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Not stating the assumption about total pixel count:** In numerical problems where the provided counts don't sum to $MN$, students must explicitly state their handling. **Deduction: 1 mark.**
