## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Contrast:**

- **Contrast** = difference between max and min luminance, measured via Michelson formula $C = \frac{I_{max} - I_{min}}{I_{max} + I_{min}}$.
- **Low contrast images** have histograms concentrated in a narrow band; **high contrast images** have wide, spread-out histograms.
- **Linear Contrast Stretching** uses $s = \frac{(r - r_{min}) \times 255}{r_{max} - r_{min}}$ — simplest, fastest, preserves histogram shape.
- **Piecewise Linear Stretching** uses multiple line segments for selective region enhancement — requires manual break point selection.
- **Histogram Equalization** uses the CDF as the transformation: $s_k = (L-1) \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{k} p_r(r_j)$ — produces approximately uniform output histogram.
- **PDF** is the normalized histogram: $p_r(r_k) = \frac{n_k}{MN}$, always sums to 1.
- **CDF** is the cumulative sum of PDF: $CDF(r_k) = \sum_{j=0}^{k} p_r(r_j)$, monotonically non-decreasing, ranges from 0 to 1.
- **Global HE** uses one CDF for the whole image; **Local (Adaptive) HE** uses a sliding window for region-specific enhancement.
- **CLAHE** = Contrast Limited Adaptive HE — clips the histogram to prevent noise over-amplification (used in medical imaging).
- **Continuous HE derivation** is based on the transformation-of-variables theorem: $p_s(s) = p_r(r) \cdot \frac{1}{p_r(r)} = 1$.
- **Recursion shortcut:** $s_{k+1} = s_k + (L-1) \cdot p_r(r_{k+1})$ — saves computation time.
- **For 8-bit images:** $L = 256$, $L-1 = 255$, intensity range is $[0, 255]$.
- **Histogram Specification (Matching)** is the generalization of HE where the target histogram is user-defined (not necessarily uniform).
- **Common mistakes to avoid:** (1) Forgetting to multiply by $(L-1)$, (2) forgetting to round, (3) confusing PDF with histogram, (4) omitting the transformation function sketch.
- **Real-world applications:** Medical imaging (X-ray, MRI, CT), satellite remote sensing, surveillance, industrial defect detection, astronomy, autonomous vehicles.
- **Examiner's tip:** Always include a labeled sketch of the transformation function $T(r)$ vs $r$ in 14-mark answers — it carries 2–3 marks standalone.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saturation, Colour Image Fundamentals - RGB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Saturation, Colour Image Fundamentals - RGB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The RGB Colour Model — Formal Academic Definition

The **RGB (Red, Green, Blue) colour model** is an *additive* colour model in which a perceived colour is constructed by linearly combining three primary chromatic components — Red, Green, and Blue — emitted from independent light sources. In digital image processing, an RGB image is a three-channel digital function of the form:

$$
f(x, y) = \left[ f_R(x,y),\ f_G(x,y),\ f_B(x,y) \right]
$$

where each channel is an $M \times N$ matrix of integer pixel intensities. Each pixel at coordinate $(x,y)$ is therefore represented as a 3-element vector:

$$
\mathbf{c}(x,y) = \big(R_{x,y},\ G_{x,y},\ B_{x,y}\big)
$$

with each component $R, G, B \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^{n}-1\}$ for an **n-bit per channel** representation. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the RGB cube as the canonical *device-dependent* colour space foundation from which other colour models (HSI, YCbCr, CMY) are derived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The RGB model is the *basis* of all other colour models studied in Module 1. The conversion pipeline typically originates from RGB → (HSI / CMY / YCbCr). Always treat RGB as the *acquisition* or *storage* representation, while HSI is preferred for *analysis*.

## 1.2 Saturation — Formal Academic Definition

**Saturation** is a perceptual attribute of a colour that quantifies its *purity* or *vividness*, i.e., the degree to which the colour deviates from a neutral grey of equal luminance. Mathematically, saturation is the ratio of the chromatic (colour-bearing) energy to the total (achromatic + chromatic) energy of the pixel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU-Standard):** Saturation $S \in [0, 1]$ is defined as the proportional distance from the *grey axis* (where $R = G = B$) of the RGB cube to the colour point, normalized by the maximum possible distance.

## 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy — The Painter's Studio

Imagine three spotlights (Red, Green, Blue) shining onto a white canvas in a dark studio:

| Real-World Analogy | Mathematical Mapping |
|---|---|
| Spotlights on / off switch | Binary RGB (0 or 1) |
| Dimmer knob on each spotlight | Continuous RGB intensity |
| All three at maximum white wash | $R = G = B = 255$ (White) |
| All three switched off | $R = G = B = 0$ (Black) |
| Only Red spotlight on | Pure red, *maximum saturation* |
| Red spotlight + dim grey wash | Red with *reduced saturation* (pinkish) |

For **saturation**, think of it as the *concentration* of fruit juice in water. Pure, undiluted juice = high saturation (vivid). Watered-down juice = low saturation (washed out). In colour terms, a *pure red* $(255, 0, 0)$ has $S = 1$, while a *pastel pink* $(255, 128, 128)$ has a much lower $S$ because green and blue have crept in toward grey.

## 1.4 Physical & Standardized Metrics

The following standardized parameters govern RGB image construction:

- **Pixel Depth (k)**: Number of bits used to represent each pixel per channel. For RGB with 8 bits/channel → $k_{RGB} = 24$ bits/pixel.
- **Total Image Size**: $M \cdot N \cdot k$ bits for an $M \times N$ image.
- **Number of Distinct Colours**: $L = 2^{k} = (2^{n})^{3}$ for $n$ bits per channel.
- **CIE / sRGB Standard**: The sRGB gamut (IEC 61966-2-1) is the de-facto standard RGB space used by virtually all displays and image formats.
- **Safe RGB Colours**: $6 \times 6 \times 6 = 216$ browser-safe values (each channel $\in \{0, 51, 102, 153, 204, 255\}$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RGB Colour Cube — Architectural Breakdown

The RGB cube is a unit cube (or $2^{n}-1$ cube for discrete images) where:

1. **Origin $(0,0,0)$** = Black (no light).
2. **Far vertex $(2^{n}-1,\ 2^{n}-1,\ 2^{n}-1)$** = White (all light).
3. **Grey axis** = The main diagonal where $R = G = B$.
4. **Vertices on the cube** = The eight pure hues (corners with at least one 0 and the rest at max).
5. **Edges** = Hues with one channel varying between 0 and max.

The eight corners of the cube correspond to the eight perceptual colour extremes:

| Vertex (Normalized) | Colour | RGB Hex |
|---|---|---|
| $(0, 0, 0)$ | Black | $\#000000$ |
| $(1, 0, 0)$ | Red | $\#FF0000$ |
| $(0, 1, 0)$ | Green | $\#00FF00$ |
| $(0, 0, 1)$ | Blue | $\#0000FF$ |
| $(1, 1, 0)$ | Yellow | $\#FFFF00$ |
| $(1, 0, 1)$ | Magenta | $\#FF00FF$ |
| $(0, 1, 1)$ | Cyan | $\#00FFFF$ |
| $(1, 1, 1)$ | White | $\#FFFFFF$ |

**Primary colours of light**: Red, Green, Blue (additive primaries).
**Secondary colours**: Cyan, Magenta, Yellow (sums of two primaries).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is RGB additive?** Because we are mixing *light*, not pigment. Adding red + green light yields yellow; this is counterintuitive from the subtractive pigment mixing learned in school, where red + green paint yields brown.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Saturation Computation

1. **Step 1 — Normalize** $R, G, B$ to the range $[0, 1]$ by dividing by $2^{n}-1$.
2. **Step 2 — Compute Sum**: $\Sigma = R + G + B$.
3. **Step 3 — Identify Minimum**: $m = \min(R, G, B)$.
4. **Step 4 — Compute Saturation**: $S = 1 - \dfrac{3m}{\Sigma}$ (provided $\Sigma \neq 0$).
5. **Step 5 — Boundary Check**: If $R = G = B$ (pure grey), then $S = 0$. If $m = 0$ and the other two are equal, $S = 1$.

**Why does this work?** The *grey axis* contribution of any pixel is the common component shared by all three channels. The quantity $3m/(R+G+B)$ represents the *fraction of the total energy that is achromatic*. Subtracting it from 1 yields the chromatic fraction — i.e., saturation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU board exam on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RGB Pixel Vector | $\mathbf{c} = (R, G, B)$ | $R, G, B \in [0, 2^{n}-1]$ |
| 2 | Number of Colours | $L = (2^{n})^{3} = 2^{3n}$ | Discrete count |
| 3 | Total Image Size | $B_{tot} = M \cdot N \cdot 3n$ | bits |
| 4 | Saturation (from RGB) | $S = 1 - \dfrac{3 \min(R,G,B)}{R + G + B}$ | $S \in [0, 1]$, dimensionless |
| 5 | Intensity (from RGB) | $I = \dfrac{R + G + B}{3}$ | $I \in [0, 1]$, normalized |
| 6 | Intensity (8-bit form) | $I_{8} = \dfrac{R_{8} + G_{8} + B_{8}}{3}$ | $I_{8} \in [0, 255]$ |
| 7 | Safe Colours Count | $N_{safe} = 6^{3} = 216$ | Discrete count |
| 8 | Safe Colour Step | $\Delta = 255 / 5 = 51$ | Intensity steps |
| 9 | Hue Angle Numerator | $\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2}[(R-G)+(R-B)]}{\sqrt{(R-G)^{2} + (R-B)(G-B)}}\right]$ | Radians / Degrees |
| 10 | Hue Final | $H = \theta$ if $B \leq G$; else $H = 360 - \theta$ | Degrees, $H \in [0, 360]$ |
| 11 | CMY from RGB | $\begin{bmatrix}C\\M\\Y\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\1\\1\end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix}R\\G\\B\end{bmatrix}$ | Normalized $[0, 1]$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Display Engineering**: Every smartphone, monitor, and TV panel uses the RGB sub-pixel triad as the physical emission primitive. Understanding the cube geometry is essential for **gamma correction** and **colour calibration**.
- **Medical Imaging**: DICOM standards encode colour endoscopic and pathology images in RGB; saturation analysis helps identify tissue abnormalities.
- **Remote Sensing**: Satellite multispectral imagery is often visualized by mapping arbitrary spectral bands to the R, G, B display channels (false-colour composites).
- **Computer Vision**: Object segmentation in HSI space is more robust to illumination variation than RGB segmentation — a key motivation for converting RGB → HSI.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: RGB → Saturation

We will derive the saturation component of the HSI model from the RGB colour cube geometry. This is the most-tested derivation in KTU board exams.

### 3.1.1 Setup & Assumptions

Let the normalized RGB components be:

$$
R' = \frac{R}{2^{n}-1},\quad G' = \frac{G}{2^{n}-1},\quad B' = \frac{B}{2^{n}-1}
$$

such that $R', G', B' \in [0, 1]$. For clarity, we drop the primes and work with $R, G, B \in [0, 1]$.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Motivation

Every RGB point lies inside (or on) the unit cube $[0,1]^{3}$. Decompose the point vector into two orthogonal components:

$$
\mathbf{c} = \underbrace{\frac{R+G+B}{3}(1,1,1)}_{\text{achromatic (grey) axis}} + \underbrace{\left(\mathbf{c} - \frac{R+G+B}{3}(1,1,1)\right)}_{\text{chromatic (colour) component}}
$$

The achromatic vector points along the grey diagonal $(1,1,1)/\sqrt{3}$.

### 3.1.3 Magnitudes of the Two Components

The magnitude of the achromatic component is:

$$
\parallel \mathbf{c}_{achrom} \parallel = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\Sigma}{3}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\Sigma}{3}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\Sigma}{3}\right)^{2}} = \frac{\Sigma}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

where $\Sigma = R + G + B$.

For the *maximum* distance from the grey axis (which occurs at the cube corners, e.g., $(1, 0, 0)$), we have:

$$
\parallel \mathbf{c}_{max} \parallel = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^{2} + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2} + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{4 + 1 + 1}{9}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{6}{9}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}
$$

### 3.1.4 Definition of Saturation

Saturation is the ratio of the chromatic distance to the maximum chromatic distance:

$$
S = \frac{\parallel \mathbf{c}_{chrom} \parallel}{\parallel \mathbf{c}_{max} \parallel}
$$

### 3.1.5 Compute the Chromatic Magnitude

The squared magnitude of $\mathbf{c}$ minus the squared achromatic projection:

$$
\parallel \mathbf{c} \parallel^{2} = R^{2} + G^{2} + B^{2}
$$

$$
\parallel \mathbf{c}_{achrom} \parallel^{2} = \frac{\Sigma^{2}}{3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\parallel \mathbf{c}_{chrom} \parallel^{2} = R^{2} + G^{2} + B^{2} - \frac{\Sigma^{2}}{3}
$$

### 3.1.6 Simplification Trick (Standard KTU Route)

A much faster route uses the observation that the chromatic deviation is determined solely by the *smallest* RGB component. The total energy $\Sigma$ equals $3$ times the average. The white (achromatic) component is $3 \min(R, G, B)$ (this represents the common base amount added to all three channels to "wash out" the colour).

Thus:

$$
S = \frac{\Sigma - 3 \min(R,G,B)}{\Sigma} = 1 - \frac{3 \min(R,G,B)}{R + G + B}
$$

### 3.1.7 Verification — Three Critical Test Cases

**Test 1 — Pure Grey**: $R = G = B = 0.5$

$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot 0.5}{1.5} = 1 - 1 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Test 2 — Pure Red**: $R = 1, G = 0, B = 0$

$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot 0}{1} = 1 - 0 = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Test 3 — Pastel Red**: $R = 1, G = 0.4, B = 0.4$

$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot 0.4}{1.8} = 1 - \frac{1.2}{1.8} = 1 - 0.667 = 0.333
$$

This confirms *pastel* red has lower saturation than pure red. ✓

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: RGB → Hue Angle

The hue angle $\theta$ is the angle measured around the grey axis. Starting from the Red axis:

$$
\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\frac{\frac{1}{2}\big[(R-G) + (R-B)\big]}{\sqrt{(R-G)^{2} + (R-B)(G-B)}}\right]
$$

with the correction $H = 360 - \theta$ if $B > G$.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Simplification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator:}\quad N &= \tfrac{1}{2}\big[(R-G) + (R-B)\big] = \tfrac{1}{2}(2R - G - B) \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator:}\quad D &= \sqrt{(R-G)^{2} + (R-B)(G-B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both $N$ and $D$ have units of intensity (normalized). When $R = G = B$, $D = 0$ and $\theta$ is undefined (the hue of an achromatic pixel is meaningless — only saturation determines its "colourlessness").

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example

Let $R = 0.8,\ G = 0.4,\ B = 0.2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \tfrac{1}{2}[(0.8 - 0.4) + (0.8 - 0.2)] = \tfrac{1}{2}[0.4 + 0.6] = 0.5 \\[4pt]
(R-G)^{2} &= (0.4)^{2} = 0.16 \\[4pt]
(R-B)(G-B) &= (0.6)(0.2) = 0.12 \\[4pt]
D &= \sqrt{0.16 + 0.12} = \sqrt{0.28} \approx 0.5292 \\[4pt]
\cos\theta &= \frac{0.5}{0.5292} \approx 0.9449 \\[4pt]
\theta &= \cos^{-1}(0.9449) \approx 19.10^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $B = 0.2 < G = 0.4$, we have $H = \theta \approx 19.10^{\circ}$ (an orange-red hue).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — RGB Image Storage Economics

| Image Specification | Resolution | Bits/Channel | Total Bits | Total Bytes | Total KB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Grayscale thumbnail | $256 \times 256$ | $8$ | $524{,}288$ | $65{,}536$ | $64$ |
| RGB thumbnail | $256 \times 256$ | $8$ | $1{,}572{,}864$ | $196{,}608$ | $192$ |
| HD RGB photo | $1920 \times 1080$ | $8$ | $49{,}766{,}400$ | $6{,}220{,}800$ | $6{,}075$ |
| Medical MRI slice | $512 \times 512$ | $12$ | $9{,}437{,}184$ | $1{,}179{,}648$ | $1{,}152$ |

The factor of **3** in RGB storage is precisely why **compression** (JPEG, PNG) and **chrominance subsampling** (4:2:0 in YCbCr) are critical engineering concerns — and a likely viva question in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RGB Colour Cube — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **conceptual architecture** of the RGB colour cube and the saturation extraction pipeline. This is the canonical KTU-board diagram substitute when physical cube illustrations cannot be drawn.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Normalized RGB Pixel R, G, B]) --> N1{Compute Sum Sigma}
    N1 -->|Sigma equals 0| N2[Saturation S equals 0 Black pixel]
    N1 -->|Sigma greater than 0| N3[Find minimum of R, G, B]
    N3 --> N4[Compute S equals 1 minus 3 times min divided by Sigma]
    N4 --> N5{S equals 0 question}
    N5 -->|Yes| N6[Achromatic Pure Grey]
    N5 -->|No| N7{Compute Hue Angle theta}
    N7 -->|B greater than G| N8[H equals 360 minus theta]
    N7 -->|B less than or equal G| N9[H equals theta]
    N8 --> N10[Combine S, H, I to form HSI pixel]
    N9 --> N10
    N2 --> N10
    N6 --> N10
    N10 --> End([HSI Output Vector])
```

## 4.2 RGB Image Channel Decomposition — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Img[Original RGB Image M by N by 3] --> ChR[Red Channel Matrix M by N]
    Img --> ChG[Green Channel Matrix M by N]
    Img --> ChB[Blue Channel Matrix M by N]
    ChR --> Proc[Independent Channel Processing]
    ChG --> Proc
    ChB --> Proc
    Proc --> Out[Processed RGB Image M by N by 3]
```

## 4.3 RGB Cube Vertex Legend — Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Black[Origin 0,0,0 - Black] --> R[1,0,0 - Pure Red]
    Black --> G[0,1,0 - Pure Green]
    Black --> B[0,0,1 - Pure Blue]
    R --> Y[1,1,0 - Yellow Red plus Green]
    R --> Mg[1,0,1 - Magenta Red plus Blue]
    G --> Y
    G --> Cy[0,1,1 - Cyan Green plus Blue]
    B --> Mg
    B --> Cy
    Y --> W[1,1,1 - White All Primaries]
    Mg --> W
    Cy --> W
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **black node** anchors the cube at the origin.
- The three **primary vertices** (R, G, B) lie on the three cube edges emanating from black.
- The three **secondary vertices** (Yellow, Magenta, Cyan) lie on the three edges emanating from white.
- The **white node** is the diagonally opposite vertex — *additive combination of all primaries*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Tip:** Always state that the cube is *normalized* to $[0,1]^{3}$ when explaining to examiners, but for image storage each axis spans $\{0, 1, \ldots, 255\}$ for an 8-bit representation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> Define the RGB colour model. List the primary and secondary colours of light and state the number of distinguishable colours in a 24-bit RGB image.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **RGB colour model** is an additive colour model in which a colour is produced by combining three primary chromatic components: **Red, Green, and Blue**, each representing an independent intensity channel.

- **Primary colours of light**: Red, Green, Blue
- **Secondary colours of light**: Cyan (Green + Blue), Magenta (Red + Blue), Yellow (Red + Green)

In a **24-bit RGB image**, each channel has 8 bits, giving $2^{8} = 256$ intensity levels per channel. The total number of distinct colours is:

$$
L = (2^{8})^{3} = 256^{3} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ colours}
$$

This is the canonical *true-colour* count used in display systems. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for primaries/secondaries, 1 mark for colour count).**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]

> Define saturation in the context of colour image processing. Compute the saturation for the RGB triplet $(255, 128, 64)$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Saturation $S$ is a perceptual measure of colour purity — the degree to which a colour deviates from a neutral grey of equal intensity. It is normalized to the range $[0, 1]$.

**Computation:**
Normalize to $[0, 1]$: $R = 1.0,\ G = 0.502,\ B = 0.251$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma &= R + G + B = 1.0 + 0.502 + 0.251 = 1.753 \\[4pt]
m &= \min(1.0, 0.502, 0.251) = 0.251 \\[4pt]
S &= 1 - \frac{3m}{\Sigma} = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot 0.251}{1.753} = 1 - \frac{0.753}{1.753} \\[4pt]
S &= 1 - 0.4295 = 0.5705
\end{aligned}
$$

**Saturation $S \approx 0.571$ (or $57.05\%$).**

**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correct computation.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified] — **14 Marks**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> Explain the RGB colour model with the help of the RGB colour cube. Discuss primary and secondary colours, the grey axis, and safe RGB colours.

**Model Solution:**

**1. RGB Model Overview (1.5 marks):**
The RGB model is *additive* — colours are created by adding light components. It is the foundational colour model for displays, scanners, and digital cameras. Each pixel is represented as a triplet $(R, G, B)$.

**2. The RGB Colour Cube (3 marks):**
The RGB cube is a unit cube $[0,1]^{3}$ where:
- The origin $(0,0,0)$ is **black**.
- The opposite vertex $(1,1,1)$ is **white**.
- The three axes represent the intensities of Red, Green, Blue.
- The **grey axis** is the main diagonal from black to white; every point on this axis has $R = G = B$, hence zero saturation.
- The eight cube vertices correspond to the three primaries (Red, Green, Blue), three secondaries (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow), black, and white.

**3. Primary and Secondary Colours (1.5 marks):**
- *Primaries (Light)*: Red, Green, Blue — linearly independent; cannot be created by mixing the others.
- *Secondaries (Light)*: Cyan = Green + Blue; Magenta = Red + Blue; Yellow = Red + Green.
- *Complementary pairs*: Red–Cyan, Green–Magenta, Blue–Yellow.

**4. Safe RGB Colours (1 mark):**
To ensure consistent display across browsers and legacy systems with 256-colour palettes, the *safe* RGB set restricts each channel to one of six values: $\{0, 51, 102, 153, 204, 255\}$, yielding $6^{3} = 216$ universally displayable colours. The step $\Delta = 255 / 5 = 51$ guarantees that no rounding errors occur in 8-bit quantization.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> Derive the mathematical expression for saturation $S$ from the normalized RGB components. Explain the physical interpretation of each term in the final equation.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Setup (1 mark):**
Let normalized $R, G, B \in [0, 1]$. Define:
- Sum: $\Sigma = R + G + B$
- Minimum: $m = \min(R, G, B)$

**2. Decomposition Logic (2 marks):**
Any colour point $(R, G, B)$ can be decomposed as:

$$
\mathbf{c} = \underbrace{m \cdot (1, 1, 1)}_{\text{achromatic base (white wash)}} + \underbrace{(R-m, G-m, B-m)}_{\text{chromatic excess}}
$$

The achromatic component $m(1,1,1)$ is the *grey floor* common to all three channels. The chromatic excess $(R-m, G-m, B-m)$ carries the colour information.

**3. Saturation as Ratio (2 marks):**
Saturation is the *fraction of total energy that is chromatic*:

$$
S = \frac{\text{chromatic energy}}{\text{total energy}} = \frac{\Sigma - 3m}{\Sigma} = 1 - \frac{3m}{\Sigma}
$$

**4. Physical Interpretation of Each Term (2 marks):**
- $\Sigma = R + G + B$: total radiant energy of the pixel (proportional to brightness).
- $3m$: total *achromatic* (grey) energy — the minimum channel broadcast to all three components.
- $\Sigma - 3m$: chromatic (colour-bearing) energy.
- The ratio $\frac{3m}{\Sigma}$ represents the *greyness fraction*. Subtracting from 1 yields the *purity fraction* — saturation.

**5. Boundary Behaviour (built into derivation):**
- Pure grey ($R = G = B$): $m = \Sigma/3$ → $S = 0$. ✓
- Pure primary ($R = 1$, $G = B = 0$): $m = 0$ → $S = 1$. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to specify the *normalization* requirement ($R, G, B \in [0,1]$) before applying the formula. This alone costs **1 mark** if omitted. Also, never write "S = 1 − min/(R+G+B)" — the numerator *must* be $3 \cdot \min$.

---

### ⭐ Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified] — **14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> With suitable equations, explain the conversion from RGB to the HSI colour model. Derive the intensity and saturation components in detail.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Motivation (1 mark):**
The RGB model is not perceptually intuitive — humans describe colours by *hue, saturation, and brightness*, not by red, green, blue content. The HSI model decouples chromaticity (H, S) from intensity (I), making it ideal for image analysis.

**2. Intensity Component (1.5 marks):**
Intensity is the arithmetic mean of the three normalized channels:

$$
I = \frac{R + G + B}{3}
$$

This corresponds to the projection of the colour point onto the grey axis.

**3. Saturation Component (2 marks):**

$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R, G, B)}{R + G + B}
$$

Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 above for full working). Saturation vanishes on the grey axis and reaches unity at the cube corners.

**4. Hue Component (2.5 marks):**
The hue is the angle measured around the grey axis:

$$
\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\big[(R-G)+(R-B)\big]}{\sqrt{(R-G)^{2} + (R-B)(G-B)}}\right]
$$

$$
H = \begin{cases} \theta & \text{if } B \leq G \\ 360 - \theta & \text{if } B > G \end{cases}
$$

The denominator is the chromatic magnitude; the numerator encodes the R-vs-(G,B) imbalance.

**5. Resulting Vector (builds to 7 marks):**
The HSI pixel:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{HSI} = (H, S, I),\quad H \in [0, 360],\ S \in [0, 1],\ I \in [0, 1]
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> For a 24-bit RGB image of size $1024 \times 768$ pixels, compute (i) total storage in bytes, (ii) total number of distinct colours, and (iii) number of safe RGB colours. Comment on the storage implications.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Storage in Bytes (2.5 marks):**
Total bits per pixel = $8 \times 3 = 24$ bits.
Total bits in image:

$$
B_{tot} = M \cdot N \cdot k = 1024 \cdot 768 \cdot 24
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1024 \cdot 768 &= 786{,}432 \text{ pixels} \\[4pt]
786{,}432 \cdot 24 &= 18{,}874{,}368 \text{ bits} \\[4pt]
\text{Bytes} &= \frac{18{,}874{,}368}{8} = 2{,}359{,}296 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
\text{Kilobytes} &= \frac{2{,}359{,}296}{1024} \approx 2{,}304 \text{ KB} \approx 2.25 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Number of Distinct Colours (2 marks):**

$$
L = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ distinct colours}
$$

**3. Safe RGB Colours (1.5 marks):**

$$
N_{safe} = 6^{3} = 216 \text{ safe colours}
$$

Each channel is restricted to $\{0, 51, 102, 153, 204, 255\}$, ensuring consistent cross-platform rendering on legacy 8-bit displays.

**4. Storage Implications (1 mark):**
- RGB images consume **3× the storage** of equivalent grayscale images.
- Uncompressed RGB transmission over networks (e.g., 1080p video at 30 fps) requires ≈ $6.22 \times 30 = 186.6$ MB/s — motivating **compression** standards like JPEG.
- Safe colour sets enable **lossless indexing** for low-bandwidth legacy applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the storage as $M \times N \times k$ bits but then forget to *convert to bytes* by dividing by 8. Also, do not confuse $2^{24}$ with $24^{2}$ — the exponentiation is correct on the base 2.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Read this twice before the exam.**

- ☐ **RGB is additive** — combining Red + Green light = Yellow, not brown.
- ☐ **The grey axis** of the RGB cube is the locus of *zero saturation* — all points where $R = G = B$.
- ☐ **Saturation formula** to memorize verbatim: $S = 1 - \dfrac{3 \min(R,G,B)}{R+G+B}$.
- ☐ **Normalization** is mandatory: divide 8-bit values by 255 before applying the formula.
- ☐ **Number of safe colours** = $6^{3} = 216$ — a *very* common viva question.
- ☐ **24-bit RGB image** = $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ distinct colours.
- ☐ **Hue angle** requires the *arc-cosine* expression with a square-root denominator — never skip the denominator.
- ☐ **When $B > G$**, the hue angle *flips*: $H = 360 - \theta$. Forgetting this produces incorrect hues in the cyan–blue–magenta range.
- ☐ **For $R = G = B$**, hue is *undefined* (or set to 0 by convention) — state this explicitly in your answer.
- ☐ **Intensity** is the arithmetic mean; do not confuse with luminance (which uses weighted coefficients $0.299, 0.587, 0.114$).
- ☐ **The RGB cube has 8 vertices** corresponding to: 3 primaries + 3 secondaries + black + white.
- ☐ **Pixel depth $k = 3n$ for RGB** where $n$ is bits per channel; total bits = $M \cdot N \cdot 3n$.

**Cross-References for Further Study:**
- Gonzalez & Woods, *Digital Image Processing*, Chapter 6 (Colour Image Processing).
- KTU 2024 Syllabus Module 1 — Sections on colour fundamentals and HSI model.
- Companion topics: CMY/CMYK conversion, YCbCr for JPEG, chromaticity diagrams (CIE xy).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HSI Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSIPROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** HSI Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **HSI (Hue, Saturation, Intensity)** color model, also referred to as the HSI Color Space, is a cylindrical-coordinate representation of the **RGB** color model. It is engineered to decouple the **chrominance** (color information — comprising Hue and Saturation) from the **luminance** (intensity component) of a color.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Gonzalez & Woods Terminology):**
> The HSI model is fundamentally important in digital image processing because it separates the **intensity (I)** component from the **hue (H)** and **saturation (S)** components. This separation is highly advantageous when developing image processing algorithms that operate on the color descriptors independent of brightness variations (e.g., histogram equalization, edge detection on color images, shadow removal).

### Mathematical Components

1. **Hue (H):** Represents the **dominant wavelength** or pure color (e.g., red, green, blue, yellow). Measured as an **angular quantity** in the range **[0°, 360°]**, originating from the red axis.
2. **Saturation (S):** Represents the **purity or vibrancy** of the color — the amount of white light mixed with the pure hue. Normalized in the range **[0, 1]**. A value of 0 corresponds to a completely unsaturated (gray) color.
3. **Intensity (I):** Represents the **overall brightness** of the color, independent of its hue. Defined as the average of the R, G, and B components, normalized in the range **[0, 1]** (or **[0, 255]** for 8-bit images).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Artist's Palette

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition — Imagine an Artist mixing watercolors:**
> 
> - **Intensity (I)** is how much **water** is on the brush — it controls how light or dark the paint appears on the paper.
> - **Hue (H)** is the actual **color pigment** chosen (red, yellow, green, etc.) — the dominant wavelength.
> - **Saturation (S)** is how much **gray/white** is mixed into the pigment — pure red has zero gray (fully saturated), while pink is red mixed with white (less saturated).
> 
> This is exactly how the human eye and brain perceive color: as a combination of brightness, dominant color, and color purity — **not** as arbitrary mixtures of red, green, and blue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU insists on HSI:** When you adjust brightness of an image (e.g., for lighting correction), doing it on RGB distorts colors. In HSI, you only modify the **I** channel, leaving the chromatic information (**H** and **S**) completely untouched. This is a fundamental requirement in modern computer vision, medical imaging, and remote sensing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need a Model Other Than RGB?

The RGB model is **hardware-oriented** (matches monitor/display phosphors), but it has critical limitations:

- It is **not perceptually intuitive** — humans do not describe color as "a mix of 30% red, 50% green, 20% blue."
- The **intensity (luminance)** and **color (chrominance)** information are **entangled** in all three channels (R, G, B).
- Image processing operations on RGB (like smoothing) often **distort colors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The HSI model resolves this entanglement — it is a **perceptually meaningful** color space derived directly from the RGB cube via a non-linear transformation.

## 2.2 Geometric Interpretation

The RGB color cube can be tilted such that the **black-to-white diagonal** (the grayscale axis) becomes the **vertical Intensity axis**. The resulting hexcone (or double-cone) has:
- **Vertical axis:** Intensity (I) from black (0) to white (1).
- **Angular coordinate around axis:** Hue (H), with red at 0°.
- **Radial distance from axis:** Saturation (S), with 0 at the axis and 1 at the outer surface.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table. KTU questions are 90% formula substitution + sector-based logic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | Intensity | $I = \dfrac{1}{3}(R + G + B)$ | $[0, 1]$ or $[0, 255]$ | Average of R, G, B |
| 2 | Saturation | $S = 1 - \dfrac{3 \cdot \min(R, G, B)}{R + G + B}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ when R = G = B (gray) |
| 3 | Hue (Numerator) | $\text{Num} = \dfrac{(R - G) + (R - B)}{2}$ | — | Used inside cosine |
| 4 | Hue (Denominator) | $\text{Den} = \sqrt{(R - G)^2 + (R - B)(G - B)}$ | — | Always non-negative |
| 5 | Hue (Value) | $H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Num}}{\text{Den}}\right)$ | $[0°, 180°]$ | Apply correction for B > G |
| 6 | Hue Correction | If $B > G$ → $H = 360° - H$ | $[0°, 360°]$ | Else H remains as computed |
| 7 | HSI → RGB Sector RG | $B = I(1 - S)$ | — | $0° \leq H < 120°$ |
| 8 | HSI → RGB Sector GB | $R = I(1 - S)$ | — | $120° \leq H < 240°$ |
| 9 | HSI → RGB Sector BR | $G = I(1 - S)$ | — | $240° \leq H < 360°$ |
| 10 | Sectors use $H' = H / 60°$ | Three sectors of 120° each | — | Divide-by-60° transformation |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE (no pipes in rows):** Mathematical expressions inside the table use `\sqrt`, `\dfrac`, and `\cos^{-1}` to avoid breaking markdown syntax. Do not write $|x|$ with raw pipes — use `\vert x \vert` instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (CT/MRI):** Tissue color analysis is robust under HSI because intensity variations due to scan calibration do not affect hue.
- **Remote Sensing:** Vegetation detection, water-body mapping — Hue is a strong discriminator for land-cover types.
- **PCB Inspection:** Defect detection in manufactured electronics is color-tolerant when using HSI.
- **Shadow Removal in Surveillance:** Modifying only the I-channel normalizes lighting without changing object identity.
- **Skin-Tone Detection in Biometrics:** Hue + Saturation thresholds outperform RGB thresholds for human skin segmentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RGB → HSI Conversion

### 3.1.1 Intensity Component

The intensity is defined as the arithmetic mean of the three normalized RGB components, representing the brightness on the grayscale diagonal of the RGB cube.

$$
I = \frac{1}{3}(R + G + B)
$$

**Logic:** This is the projection of the RGB point onto the gray axis (black-to-white diagonal). When R = G = B (a pure gray pixel), $I$ correctly equals that common value.

### 3.1.2 Saturation Component

Saturation measures how far the color is from the gray axis, normalized to the maximum possible chroma.

$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R, G, B)}{R + G + B}
$$

**Derivation Logic:**

- The minimum of (R, G, B) represents how much **white** is present in the color (since white = R = G = B, any deviation from the min contributes to color).
- The quantity $\dfrac{\min(R, G, B)}{R + G + B}$ is the **fractional white content**.
- $1 - $ white content gives the **fractional color (chroma) content**.
- The factor 3 normalizes the expression so that **fully saturated primary colors** (e.g., pure red = (1, 0, 0)) give $S = 1$, and **grays** (R = G = B) give $S = 0$.

### 3.1.3 Hue Component (The Critical Derivation)

Hue is derived as the **angle** of the chromaticity vector projected onto the plane perpendicular to the gray axis. The standard formulation uses the **inverse cosine**:

$$
H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{(R - G) + (R - B)}{2 \sqrt{(R - G)^2 + (R - B)(G - B)}}\right)
$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation Logic:**

**Step 1 — Define the dot product of normalized chromaticity vectors:**

The derivation starts from the identity that $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\vert \vec{u} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{v} \vert}$, where $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$ are unit vectors along the red axis and the chromaticity of the color.

**Step 2 — Express the red-axis vector and color vector:**

Taking the red axis as $\vec{u} = (1, 0, 0)$ and the (R−G, R−B) differences as components of the chromaticity vector in the G–B plane.

**Step 3 — Compute the dot product:**

$$
\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 1 \cdot (R - G) + 0 \cdot (\text{other}) = (R - G) + (R - B)
$$

The sum $(R - G) + (R - B) = 2R - G - B$ appears in the numerator.

**Step 4 — Compute the magnitude product:**

After algebraic manipulation, the magnitude of the chromaticity vector becomes $\sqrt{(R - G)^2 + (R - B)(G - B)}$.

**Step 5 — Form the cosine ratio:**

$$
\cos(H) = \frac{(R - G) + (R - B)}{2 \sqrt{(R - G)^2 + (R - B)(G - B)}}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply inverse cosine:**

$$
H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{(R - G) + (R - B)}{2 \sqrt{(R - G)^2 + (R - B)(G - B)}}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Correction Step:**
> The inverse cosine returns values only in the range $[0°, 180°]$. To obtain the full $[0°, 360°]$ hue range, the following correction is **mandatory**:
> 
> If $B > G$, then $H = 360° - H$.  
> If $B \leq G$, $H$ remains as computed.
> 
> Additionally, **if $S = 0$**, the hue is **mathematically undefined** (the color is on the gray axis, where any hue is the same).

## 3.2 Derivation: HSI → RGB Conversion (Sector-Based)

The reverse conversion is **piecewise** — it divides the hue circle into three 120° sectors corresponding to the dominant primary color. Let $H' = H / 60°$ (a normalized hue index).

### 3.2.1 Sector 1: $0° \leq H < 120°$ (Red–Green dominant, B is minimum)

In this sector, the blue component is the smallest:

$$
B = I(1 - S)
$$

$$
R = I\left[1 + \frac{S \cos(H)}{\cos(60° - H)}\right]
$$

$$
G = 3I - (R + B)
$$

**Derivation Logic:** The R and G components "compete" based on H. When $H = 0°$ (pure red), $G = 0$ and $R = I(1 + S)$. When $H = 60°$ (yellow), $R = G = I(1 + S/2)$ — symmetric mixing. The equation for R is derived from geometric projection onto the RG plane, and the constraint $R + G + B = 3I$ (definition of intensity) gives G automatically.

### 3.2.2 Sector 2: $120° \leq H < 240°$ (Green–Blue dominant, R is minimum)

Subtract 120° from H to remap to a "new origin" at 120°:

$$
R = I(1 - S)
$$

$$
G = I\left[1 + \frac{S \cos(H - 120°)}{\cos(180° - H)}\right]
$$

$$
B = 3I - (R + G)
$$

**Logic:** Same structure as Sector 1, but cyclically rotated. Red is now the "background" (minimum), and G–B are computed from the shifted hue.

### 3.2.3 Sector 3: $240° \leq H < 360°$ (Blue–Red dominant, G is minimum)

Subtract 240° from H:

$$
G = I(1 - S)
$$

$$
B = I\left[1 + \frac{S \cos(H - 240°)}{\cos(300° - H)}\right]
$$

$$
R = 3I - (G + B)
$$

**Logic:** Symmetric continuation. G is minimum, B–R carry the hue information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unified Verification Identity:** In all three sectors, the identity $R + G + B = 3I$ must hold, which is why the third color is always computed from this constraint.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Given:** A pixel has $R = 0.6, G = 0.2, B = 0.4$ (normalized values).

**Step 1 — Intensity:**
$$
I = \frac{0.6 + 0.2 + 0.4}{3} = \frac{1.2}{3} = 0.4
$$

**Step 2 — Saturation:**
$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(0.6, 0.2, 0.4)}{0.6 + 0.2 + 0.4} = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot 0.2}{1.2} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 — Hue (Numerator):**
$$
\text{Num} = (0.6 - 0.2) + (0.6 - 0.4) = 0.4 + 0.2 = 0.6
$$

**Step 4 — Hue (Denominator):**
$$
\text{Den} = \sqrt{(0.6 - 0.2)^2 + (0.6 - 0.4)(0.2 - 0.4)} = \sqrt{0.16 + (-0.04)} = \sqrt{0.12} \approx 0.3464
$$

**Step 5 — Apply inverse cosine:**
$$
H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.6}{2 \cdot 0.3464}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.6}{0.6928}\right) = \cos^{-1}(0.8660) = 30°
$$

**Step 6 — Correction:** Since $B = 0.4 > G = 0.2$, apply $H = 360° - 30° = 330°$.

**Final HSI:** $H = 330°$, $S = 0.5$, $I = 0.4$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RGB ↔ HSI Conversion Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input RGB Pixel] --> B[Normalize R G B to 0-1]
    B --> C[Compute Intensity I]
    B --> D[Compute Saturation S]
    B --> E[Compute Hue H]
    C --> F[Decision: Is S equals 0]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> G[H is UNDEFINED Gray Pixel]
    F -- No --> H[Check B greater than G]
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply H equals 360 minus H]
    H -- No --> J[Keep H as computed]
    I --> K[Output HSI H S I]
    J --> K
    G --> K

    L[Input HSI H S I] --> M[Compute H prime equals H divided by 60]
    M --> N{Sector Decision}
    N -- Sector 1: 0 to 120 --> O[RG Sector Equations]
    N -- Sector 2: 120 to 240 --> P[GB Sector Equations]
    N -- Sector 3: 240 to 360 --> Q[BR Sector Equations]
    O --> R[Output RGB Pixel]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Hue Sector Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hexcone_Cross_Section
        direction LR
        Axis[Gray Axis Intensity] --> Center((Center))
        Center --> R[R = 0 deg]
        Center --> Y[Y = 60 deg]
        Center --> G[G = 120 deg]
        Center --> C[C = 180 deg]
        Center --> B[B = 240 deg]
        Center --> M[M = 300 deg]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of HSI-Based Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw RGB Image] --> B[RGB to HSI Conversion]
    B --> C[H Channel: Color Identity]
    B --> D[S Channel: Color Purity]
    B --> E[I Channel: Brightness Only]
    C --> F[Color-Based Processing]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Histogram Equalization on I]
    G --> H[Modified I Channel]
    F --> I[Processed H and S]
    H --> J[HSI to RGB Conversion]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Output Processed RGB Image]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** The block diagrams above summarize the **intensity-decoupling principle** — only the I-channel is modified for brightness normalization, while H and S channels are preserved. This is the **most-tested KTU concept** from HSI.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**What do you mean by Hue, Saturation, and Intensity in the HSI color model? Why is the HSI model preferred over the RGB model for image processing?**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Hue (H):** Represents the dominant pure color perceived by an observer, defined as the angle measured from a reference axis (red) in the range $[0°, 360°]$. It is a wavelength-based attribute. **(1 mark)**
- **Saturation (S):** Represents the purity of the color — the amount of white light mixed with the pure hue. Range: $[0, 1]$, where 0 indicates a fully unsaturated gray and 1 indicates a pure color. **(1 mark)**
- **Intensity (I):** Represents the brightness of the color, computed as the average of R, G, B. Range: $[0, 1]$ or $[0, 255]$. **(0.5 marks)**
- **Why HSI > RGB:** The HSI model decouples intensity from color information, allowing image processing operations (like brightness correction) to be performed on the I-channel alone without distorting the H and S (chrominance) components. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Derive the formula for computing the Intensity (I) and Saturation (S) components from a given normalized RGB color.**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Intensity (1.5 marks):** Intensity is the arithmetic mean of the three normalized RGB components, representing the projection onto the gray (black-to-white) diagonal of the RGB cube:
  
  $$I = \frac{R + G + B}{3}$$
  
  This follows because the gray axis in the RGB cube runs from (0,0,0) to (1,1,1), and any point on it has equal R, G, B values. The average gives the position along this axis.

- **Saturation (1.5 marks):** Saturation is defined as the complement of the fractional white content:
  
  $$S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R, G, B)}{R + G + B}$$
  
  The minimum of (R, G, B) represents the white-light contribution. Subtracting the fractional white from unity gives the color purity. The factor 3 normalizes the expression so that pure primaries (e.g., (1,0,0)) yield $S = 1$.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and typically have a **module-internal choice**. Each sub-part is usually **7 marks**. Complete, model solutions are provided for every sub-part below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Model]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the RGB-to-HSI conversion process. Derive the expression for the Hue component and discuss the correction required for the full $[0°, 360°]$ range.

**(b)** For an input pixel with normalized values $R = 0.8$, $G = 0.4$, $B = 0.2$, compute the corresponding HSI values. Show all working steps.

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

The RGB-to-HSI conversion involves three sequential computations:

**Step 1 — Intensity (1 mark):**
$$
I = \frac{R + G + B}{3}
$$
This represents the brightness of the pixel — the position on the gray axis of the RGB cube.

**Step 2 — Saturation (1 mark):**
$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R, G, B)}{R + G + B}
$$
This is the fractional chroma (color content) after removing the white-light dilution.

**Step 3 — Hue Derivation (4 marks):**

Hue is computed as the angle of the chromaticity vector in the plane perpendicular to the gray axis. Starting from the dot product identity $\cos(H) = \dfrac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\vert \vec{u} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{v} \vert}$, with $\vec{u} = (1, 0, 0)$ (red axis) and the chromaticity vector, we obtain:

$$
H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{(R - G) + (R - B)}{2 \sqrt{(R - G)^2 + (R - B)(G - B)}}\right)
$$

**Correction for Full Range (1 mark):** Since $\cos^{-1}$ only returns values in $[0°, 180°]$, the correction **If $B > G$, then $H = 360° - H$** is required to span the full $[0°, 360°]$ hue circle. Also, if $S = 0$ (gray pixel), hue is mathematically undefined.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

Given: $R = 0.8$, $G = 0.4$, $B = 0.2$.

**Step 1 — Intensity (1 mark):**
$$
I = \frac{0.8 + 0.4 + 0.2}{3} = \frac{1.4}{3} \approx 0.4667
$$

**Step 2 — Saturation (1 mark):**
$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(0.8, 0.4, 0.2)}{0.8 + 0.4 + 0.2} = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot 0.2}{1.4} = 1 - \frac{0.6}{1.4} = 1 - 0.4286 = 0.5714
$$

**Step 3 — Hue Numerator (1.5 marks):**
$$
\text{Num} = (0.8 - 0.4) + (0.8 - 0.2) = 0.4 + 0.6 = 1.0
$$

**Step 4 — Hue Denominator (1.5 marks):**
$$
\text{Den} = \sqrt{(0.8 - 0.4)^2 + (0.8 - 0.2)(0.4 - 0.2)} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.6 \cdot 0.2} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.12} = \sqrt{0.28} \approx 0.5292
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Inverse Cosine (1 mark):**
$$
H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.0}{2 \cdot 0.5292}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.0}{1.0583}\right) = \cos^{-1}(0.9449) \approx 19.1°
$$

**Step 6 — Correction (1 mark):** Since $B = 0.2 < G = 0.4$, **no correction needed**. $H \approx 19.1°$.

**Final HSI:** $H \approx 19.1°$, $S \approx 0.5714$, $I \approx 0.4667$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the sector-based HSI-to-RGB conversion with all three sector equations. Justify why three sectors of 120° are required.

**(b)** Convert the HSI color $(H, S, I) = (210°, 0.5, 0.5)$ back to RGB. Show all sector identification and computation steps.

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

The HSI-to-RGB conversion is **piecewise** because the hue circle is divided into three 120° sectors, each corresponding to a region where one of the three primary components (R, G, B) is the minimum. The structure is derived from the geometric projection of the (H, S) polar coordinates back onto the RGB cube faces.

**Justification for 120° Sectors (2 marks):** The three primaries (Red, Green, Blue) are spaced 120° apart in the hue circle. The 120° sector boundary represents the transition where the minimum RGB component changes (e.g., from B-minimum to R-minimum).

**Sector 1 — RG Sector ($0° \leq H < 120°$):** (1.5 marks)
$$
B = I(1 - S), \quad R = I\left[1 + \frac{S \cos(H)}{\cos(60° - H)}\right], \quad G = 3I - (R + B)
$$

**Sector 2 — GB Sector ($120° \leq H < 240°$):** (1.5 marks)
$$
R = I(1 - S), \quad G = I\left[1 + \frac{S \cos(H - 120°)}{\cos(180° - H)}\right], \quad B = 3I - (R + G)
$$

**Sector 3 — BR Sector ($240° \leq H < 360°$):** (2 marks)
$$
G = I(1 - S), \quad B = I\left[1 + \frac{S \cos(H - 240°)}{\cos(300° - H)}\right], \quad R = 3I - (G + B)
$$

In all three sectors, the constraint $R + G + B = 3I$ is the **defining identity** that determines the third component automatically.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

Given: $H = 210°$, $S = 0.5$, $I = 0.5$.

**Step 1 — Sector Identification (2 marks):** Since $120° \leq 210° < 240°$, the hue falls in the **GB Sector**.

**Step 2 — Compute R (1.5 marks):**
$$
R = I(1 - S) = 0.5 \cdot (1 - 0.5) = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.25
$$

**Step 3 — Compute G (2 marks):**
$$
G = I\left[1 + \frac{S \cos(H - 120°)}{\cos(180° - H)}\right]
$$
$$
= 0.5 \left[1 + \frac{0.5 \cdot \cos(90°)}{\cos(-30°)}\right]
$$
$$
= 0.5 \left[1 + \frac{0.5 \cdot 0}{\cos(30°)}\right] = 0.5 [1 + 0] = 0.5
$$

**Step 4 — Compute B using constraint (1.5 marks):**
$$
B = 3I - (R + G) = 3 \cdot 0.5 - (0.25 + 0.5) = 1.5 - 0.75 = 0.75
$$

**Final RGB:** $R = 0.25$, $G = 0.5$, $B = 0.75$ (a bluish-cyan tone, consistent with H = 210°).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on HSI questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Hue correction:** Not applying $H = 360° - H$ when $B > G$. This is a **silent 1-mark deduction** in nearly every KTU paper. The examiner will not award full marks for the formula if the correction is omitted.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Sector Selection:** In HSI → RGB, misidentifying the sector (e.g., writing Sector 1 equations for $H = 200°$) loses **2–3 marks** immediately. Always confirm $120° \leq H < 240°$ before using GB sector formulas.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the constraint $R + G + B = 3I$:** This identity must be **explicitly stated** before computing the third component. Examiners look for it as a **proof of understanding**.
> 
> 4. **Units / Normalization confusion:** KTU gives RGB in two formats — normalized $[0, 1]$ or 8-bit $[0, 255]$. Mixing them in the same computation leads to wrong S and H values. **Always normalize first.**
> 
> 5. **Undefined Hue:** If $R = G = B$ (gray pixel), $S = 0$ and $H$ is undefined. Writing any finite H value loses 1 mark. State explicitly: *"Hue is undefined for this pixel as it is a pure gray (S = 0)."*

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **HSI = Hue + Saturation + Intensity** — a cylindrical model derived from the RGB cube.
- ✅ **Intensity** is the **average** of R, G, B: $I = (R + G + B) / 3$.
- ✅ **Saturation** measures **purity**: $S = 1 - 3 \cdot \min(R,G,B) / (R+G+B)$.
- ✅ **Hue** is an **angle** in $[0°, 360°]$, computed via the inverse cosine formula involving $(R-G)$, $(R-B)$, and the geometric mean.
- ✅ **Mandatory correction:** If $B > G$, then $H = 360° - H$.
- ✅ **Gray pixel rule:** If $S = 0$, then $H$ is **undefined**.
- ✅ **HSI → RGB** is **sector-based** with three 120° sectors: **RG** (0°–120°), **GB** (120°–240°), **BR** (240°–360°).
- ✅ **Identity check:** $R + G + B = 3I$ must hold in all sectors.
- ✅ **Primary reason HSI is preferred over RGB:** Decoupling of intensity from chrominance enables brightness-only modifications without color distortion.
- ✅ **KTU standard form:** Use normalized RGB values $[0, 1]$ unless explicitly told otherwise.
- ✅ **Cosine arguments:** Always ensure arguments are in the valid range $[-1, 1]$ before applying $\cos^{-1}$ to avoid domain errors.
- ✅ **Geometric anchor:** Red = 0°, Yellow = 60°, Green = 120°, Cyan = 180°, Blue = 240°, Magenta = 300°.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 2D Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** 2D Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2D Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-domain two-dimensional signal (a continuous image $f(x, y)$ defined over real-valued spatial coordinates $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$) into a discrete-domain image $f(m, n)$ defined over integer indices $m, n \in \mathbb{Z}$, by measuring the amplitude of the continuous function at uniformly spaced points on a rectangular sampling grid.

Mathematically, the sampling operation is expressed as the multiplication of the continuous image with a **2D Dirac Comb** (an infinite 2D impulse train):

$$
f_s(x, y) = f(x, y) \cdot s(x, y)
$$

where

$$
s(x, y) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(x - m\Delta x, y - n\Delta y)
$$

Here, $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ are the **sampling intervals** (in millimetres, micrometres, or pixels-per-unit-length) along the horizontal and vertical axes, respectively. The resulting sampled image can be written as:

$$
f_s(x, y) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y) \, \delta(x - m\Delta x, y - n\Delta y)
$$

The quantities $f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y)$ are the **pixel intensities** that constitute the digital image.

### Key Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Rate (2D):** Sampling frequencies must satisfy $u_s \geq 2u_{max}$ **and** $v_s \geq 2v_{max}$, where $u_s = 1/\Delta x$ and $v_s = 1/\Delta y$ are the spatial sampling frequencies along the $x$ and $y$ axes.
- **Nyquist Frequency:** The maximum recoverable spatial frequency in each axis, equal to $u_s/2$ and $v_s/2$ respectively.
- **Pixels Per Inch (PPI):** Practical engineering metric equal to $1/\Delta x$ (or $1/\Delta y$ for square pixels).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Highlight:** The 2D sampling theorem is the foundational principle that justifies why we can store an image using a finite set of discrete pixels and yet reconstruct an arbitrarily close approximation of the original continuous image, provided the original is **band-limited** and we obey the Nyquist criterion in **both** dimensions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chessboard Camera"

Imagine you place a giant translucent **chessboard** (a perfectly regular grid) over a beautiful oil painting. Wherever a square of the chessboard sits, you record the *average colour* of the painting visible at that point and ignore everything in between. The result is a digital approximation of the painting.

- **The painting** = the continuous image $f(x, y)$ — infinitely detailed.
- **The chessboard squares** = the sampling grid with spacing $\Delta x, \Delta y$.
- **The recorded colour at each square** = the sample value $f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y)$.
- **The size of the squares** = inversely related to the sampling rate. **Smaller squares** → more detail captured → larger digital file. **Larger squares** → fewer samples → possible loss of detail (aliasing).

This analogy directly illustrates the central trade-off in 2D sampling: **file size vs. fidelity**, governed by the **sampling rate** relative to the image's spatial-frequency content.

## 1.3 Why 2D Sampling is More Than Just "Two 1D Samplings"

Although it is tempting to think of 2D sampling as two independent 1D samplings, the 2D case introduces phenomena that have **no 1D counterpart** — most notably, **Moiré patterns**, which are 2D interference fringes created when two periodic structures (the image content and the sampling grid) beat against each other. Therefore, the 2D analysis is genuinely richer and requires its own treatment in the frequency domain using the **2D Fourier Transform**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Recap of 1D Sampling (Foundation for 2D)

To properly understand 2D sampling, we must first recall the 1D sampling theorem.

**Step 1 — The 1D Continuous Signal:** Let $f(x)$ be a continuous, band-limited signal with Fourier Transform $F(u)$ that is zero for $\vert u \vert > U_{max}$.

**Step 2 — The 1D Impulse Train Sampler:** Define the sampling function:

$$
s(x) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(x - n\Delta x)
$$

**Step 3 — The Sampled Signal:** Multiplying $f(x)$ with $s(x)$ gives:

$$
f_s(x) = f(x) \cdot s(x) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} f(n\Delta x) \, \delta(x - n\Delta x)
$$

**Step 4 — The Fourier Consequence (Convolution Theorem):** Multiplication in the spatial domain becomes **periodic replication (convolution with impulses)** in the frequency domain:

$$
F_s(u) = \frac{1}{\Delta x} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} F\left(u - \frac{k}{\Delta x}\right)
$$

**Why this matters:** The original spectrum $F(u)$ is now replicated at every integer multiple of the sampling frequency $1/\Delta x$. As long as the replicas do not overlap (i.e., $1/\Delta x \geq 2 U_{max}$), the original signal can be recovered by low-pass filtering.

## 2.2 Extension to 2D — The Tensor Product of Two 1D Processes

The 2D case is constructed as the **separable product** of two independent 1D sampling processes along the $x$- and $y$-axes.

**Step A — The 2D Sampling Grid (2D Dirac Comb):**

$$
s(x, y) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(x - m\Delta x, y - n\Delta y)
$$

This is itself separable: $s(x, y) = s_x(x) \cdot s_y(y)$ where $s_x(x) = \sum_m \delta(x - m\Delta x)$ and $s_y(y) = \sum_n \delta(y - n\Delta y)$.

**Step B — The Sampled Image:**

$$
f_s(x, y) = f(x, y) \cdot s(x, y) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y) \, \delta(x - m\Delta x, y - n\Delta y)
$$

**Step C — The 2D Fourier Consequence:** Applying the 2D Convolution Theorem:

$$
F_s(u, v) = \frac{1}{\Delta x \, \Delta y} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} F\left(u - \frac{m}{\Delta x}, \; v - \frac{n}{\Delta y}\right)
$$

This shows that the original 2D spectrum $F(u, v)$ is replicated in a **rectangular lattice** in the $(u, v)$-plane, with the period of replication equal to $1/\Delta x$ along $u$ and $1/\Delta y$ along $v$.

**Step D — The 2D Nyquist Criterion:** For the spectral replicas to remain non-overlapping (so that the original spectrum can be recovered by a 2D ideal low-pass filter), the support of $F(u, v)$ must lie entirely within a rectangle of half-widths $1/(2\Delta x)$ and $1/(2\Delta y)$. This is equivalent to:

$$
\frac{1}{\Delta x} \geq 2 \, u_{max} \quad \text{AND} \quad \frac{1}{\Delta y} \geq 2 \, v_{max}
$$

**Why this is the "How":** Each axis has its **own** Nyquist constraint. Failing either one will cause spectral overlap (aliasing) in that axis.

## 2.3 Reconstruction from Samples (The Whittaker-Shannon Interpolation in 2D)

If the 2D Nyquist criterion is satisfied, the continuous image can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples using the **2D sinc interpolation formula**:

$$
f(x, y) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y) \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{x - m\Delta x}{\Delta x}\right) \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{y - n\Delta y}{\Delta y}\right)
$$

where $\text{sinc}(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$. This is the spatial-domain counterpart of applying an ideal low-pass filter in the frequency domain.

## 2.4 Aliasing and the Moiré Effect

When the Nyquist criterion is violated, spectral replicas overlap, and high-frequency content folds back into the baseband. In 2D this gives rise to characteristic **Moiré patterns** — large, slowly-varying fringes that were *not present* in the original image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moiré patterns** are an *aliasing artefact*, not a real feature of the scene. They appear in photographs of fine repetitive textures (fabrics, building windows, TV screens photographed with another camera) when the spatial frequency of the texture exceeds the sensor's Nyquist limit.

## 2.5 Anti-Aliasing Pre-Filtering

The standard defence against aliasing is to **low-pass filter** the continuous image *before* sampling, ensuring the band-limited condition is met. This is the **anti-aliasing filter** (a 2D low-pass filter with cutoff $u_c = 1/(2\Delta x)$ and $v_c = 1/(2\Delta y)$). In digital cameras, this is implemented optically or via the sensor's micro-lens and Bayer-pattern arrangement.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Symbol | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2D Sampling Grid (2D Dirac Comb) | $s(x, y) = \sum_m \sum_n \delta(x - m\Delta x, y - n\Delta y)$ | Spatially periodic, periods $\Delta x, \Delta y$ |
| 2 | Sampled Continuous Image | $f_s(x, y) = f(x, y) \cdot s(x, y)$ | Dirac-weighted discrete samples |
| 3 | Spatial Sampling Frequencies | $u_s = 1/\Delta x$, $v_s = 1/\Delta y$ | cycles per unit length (e.g., cycles/mm) |
| 4 | 2D Spectrum of Sampled Image | $F_s(u, v) = \dfrac{1}{\Delta x \Delta y} \sum_m \sum_n F\!\left(u - \dfrac{m}{\Delta x}, v - \dfrac{n}{\Delta y}\right)$ | Periodic replication in $(u, v)$ |
| 5 | Nyquist Criterion (2D) | $1/\Delta x \geq 2 u_{max}$ **AND** $1/\Delta y \geq 2 v_{max}$ | Both must hold simultaneously |
| 6 | Nyquist Frequency per Axis | $u_{Nyq} = 1/(2\Delta x)$, $v_{Nyq} = 1/(2\Delta y)$ | Half the sampling rate |
| 7 | 2D Reconstruction (Sinc Interp.) | $f(x, y) = \sum_m \sum_n f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y) \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{x - m\Delta x}{\Delta x}\right) \text{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{y - n\Delta y}{\Delta y}\right)$ | Valid only if Nyquist is satisfied |
| 8 | Aliasing Condition | $u_{max} > 1/(2\Delta x)$ **OR** $v_{max} > 1/(2\Delta y)$ | Spectral overlap occurs |
| 9 | Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoffs | $u_c = 1/(2\Delta x)$, $v_c = 1/(2\Delta y)$ | Applied **before** sampling |
| 10 | Moiré Pattern Frequency | $f_{moire} = \vert u_{signal} - k \cdot u_s \vert$ for some integer $k$ | Beat frequency phenomenon |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

2D sampling theory underpins:
- **Digital photography and cinematography** (sensor pixel-pitch selection).
- **Medical imaging** (MRI, CT — k-space sampling trajectories).
- **Satellite remote sensing** (resolution selection for multispectral imagers).
- **Display engineering** (LCD/OLED pixel layout and sub-sampling for chroma).
- **Print and publishing** (halftone screening, dot-pitch).
- **Computer graphics** (texture mip-mapping and anisotropic filtering).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fourier Transform of the 1D Impulse Train

We begin by establishing the spectrum of the 1D Dirac comb, which forms the building block of 2D analysis.

**Step 1 — Definition of the 1D impulse train:**

$$
s(x) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(x - n\Delta x)
$$

**Step 2 — Its Fourier Series representation.** Because $s(x)$ is periodic with period $\Delta x$, it can be expanded as a Fourier series:

$$
s(x) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} c_k \, e^{j 2\pi k x / \Delta x}
$$

**Step 3 — The Fourier Series coefficients.** We compute $c_k$ by integrating one period:

$$
c_k = \frac{1}{\Delta x} \int_{-\Delta x/2}^{\Delta x/2} s(x) \, e^{-j 2\pi k x / \Delta x} \, dx
$$

Within the integration interval, the only impulse inside is at $x = 0$ (the $n=0$ term), so:

$$
c_k = \frac{1}{\Delta x} \int_{-\Delta x/2}^{\Delta x/2} \delta(x) \, e^{-j 2\pi k x / \Delta x} \, dx = \frac{1}{\Delta x} \cdot 1
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** Therefore:

$$
s(x) = \frac{1}{\Delta x} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k x / \Delta x}
$$

**Step 5 — Fourier Transform of $s(x)$:** Taking the FT (recognising each complex exponential is an impulse in frequency):

$$
S(u) = \frac{1}{\Delta x} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\left(u - \frac{k}{\Delta x}\right)
$$

> **Interpretation:** The spectrum of an impulse train in space is *another impulse train* in frequency, with period $1/\Delta x$ and amplitude $1/\Delta x$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Spectrum of the Sampled 1D Signal

**Step 1 — Sampling as multiplication in the spatial domain:**

$$
f_s(x) = f(x) \cdot s(x) = f(x) \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta x} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k x / \Delta x}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Convolution Theorem:** Multiplication in space $\Leftrightarrow$ Convolution in frequency:

$$
F_s(u) = F(u) * S(u) = F(u) * \left[ \frac{1}{\Delta x} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\left(u - \frac{k}{\Delta x}\right) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Convolution with an impulse shifts the function:**

$$
F_s(u) = \frac{1}{\Delta x} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} F\left(u - \frac{k}{\Delta x}\right)
$$

This is the canonical 1D result. The original spectrum is replicated at every integer multiple of the sampling frequency $1/\Delta x$, scaled by $1/\Delta x$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Generalisation to 2D (Tensor Product)

**Step 1 — Construct the 2D impulse train as a separable product:**

$$
s(x, y) = \left[\sum_{m} \delta(x - m\Delta x)\right] \cdot \left[\sum_{n} \delta(y - n\Delta y)\right] = \sum_m \sum_n \delta(x - m\Delta x) \, \delta(y - n\Delta y)
$$

**Step 2 — Express using complex exponentials (using Step 5 of 3.1 applied to each axis):**

$$
s(x, y) = \frac{1}{\Delta x} \frac{1}{\Delta y} \sum_m \sum_n e^{j 2\pi m x / \Delta x} \, e^{j 2\pi n y / \Delta y}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 2D Fourier Transform:** Since the 2D FT is separable,

$$
S(u, v) = \frac{1}{\Delta x \Delta y} \sum_m \sum_n \delta\left(u - \frac{m}{\Delta x}\right) \delta\left(v - \frac{n}{\Delta y}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Sample the image by multiplication in the spatial domain:**

$$
f_s(x, y) = f(x, y) \cdot s(x, y)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the 2D Convolution Theorem:**

$$
F_s(u, v) = F(u, v) * S(u, v) = \frac{1}{\Delta x \Delta y} \sum_m \sum_n F\left(u - \frac{m}{\Delta x}, v - \frac{n}{\Delta y}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — State the 2D Nyquist condition for non-overlapping replicas:** The replicas of $F(u, v)$ must not overlap. If the support of $F(u, v)$ is contained within the rectangle $\vert u \vert \leq u_{max}$ and $\vert v \vert \leq v_{max}$, the condition is:

$$
\frac{1}{\Delta x} \geq 2 u_{max} \quad \text{AND} \quad \frac{1}{\Delta y} \geq 2 v_{max}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A continuous image has a band-limited spectrum with $u_{max} = 200$ cycles/mm and $v_{max} = 150$ cycles/mm. Determine the maximum permissible sampling intervals $\Delta x_{max}$ and $\Delta y_{max}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Nyquist criterion (1D along $x$):**

$$
\frac{1}{\Delta x} \geq 2 u_{max} = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ cycles/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\Delta x$:**

$$
\Delta x \leq \frac{1}{400} = 0.0025 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Nyquist criterion along $y$:**

$$
\frac{1}{\Delta y} \geq 2 v_{max} = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ cycles/mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta y$:**

$$
\Delta y \leq \frac{1}{300} \approx 0.00333 \text{ mm} = 3.33 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer in practical units:**

$$
\Delta x_{max} = 2.5 \, \mu m, \quad \Delta y_{max} \approx 3.33 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 6 — Corresponding sampling frequencies (PPI/dpi):**

$$
u_s = 400 \text{ samples/mm} = 10{,}160 \text{ PPI} \quad \text{and} \quad v_s = 300 \text{ samples/mm} = 7{,}620 \text{ PPI}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **smaller** of the two intervals corresponds to the **finer** axis. If the image is sampled with a square grid using the **stricter** constraint ($\Delta = \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) = 2.5 \, \mu m$), both criteria are automatically satisfied.

## 3.5 Derivation: Sampling Rate Required to Avoid Moiré

Suppose the image contains a sinusoidal pattern of frequency $u_0$ along the $x$-axis. Its spectrum consists of two impulses at $u = \pm u_0$. After sampling, the spectrum has replicas at $\pm u_0 + k/\Delta x$ for all integers $k$.

**Step 1 — Lowest-order alias occurs for $k = 1$:**

$$
u_{alias} = u_0 - \frac{1}{\Delta x}
$$

**Step 2 — For no aliasing in the baseband $\vert u \vert \leq 1/(2\Delta x)$:**

$$
\left| u_{alias} \right| \geq \frac{1}{2\Delta x}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting and solving:**

$$
\left| u_0 - \frac{1}{\Delta x} \right| \geq \frac{1}{2\Delta x} \implies u_0 \leq \frac{1}{2\Delta x} = u_{Nyq}
$$

This confirms the Nyquist bound from a frequency-domain perspective: the signal's highest frequency must not exceed half the sampling rate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 2D Sampling Pipeline (Architecture Flow)

The following diagram maps the **functional flow** of a 2D sampling system, from the continuous scene through the digital image, including the spectral view at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CONTINUOUS IMAGE f of x y] --> B[2D ANTI ALIASING LOW PASS FILTER H of u v]
    B --> C[FILTERED CONTINUOUS IMAGE f lp of x y]
    C --> D[MULTIPLY BY 2D DIRAC COMB s of x y]
    D --> E[SAMPLED IMAGE f s of x y EQUALS SUM OF WEIGHTED DELTAS]
    E --> F[2D SAMPLES f at m DELTA x and n DELTA y]
    F --> G[DIGITAL IMAGE STORAGE M BY N PIXEL MATRIX]

    H[2D FOURIER TRANSFORM F of u v] --> I[BAND LIMITED SPECTRUM INSIDE U MAX V MAX BOX]
    I --> J[2D FOURIER TRANSFORM OF S of x y EQUALS 1 BY DELTA x DELTA y TIMES 2D IMPULSE LATTICE]
    J --> K[CONVOLUTION IN FREQUENCY DOMAIN]
    I --> K
    K --> L[REPLICATED SPECTRUM F s of u v]
    L --> M{NYQUIST SATISFIED IN BOTH AXES}
    M -- YES --> N[PERFECT RECONSTRUCTION VIA 2D SINC INTERPOLATION]
    M -- NO --> O[ALIASING AND MOIRE PATTERNS APPEAR]
```

## 4.2 Diagram — 2D Frequency Domain Replication (Conceptual)

The following diagram shows how the spectrum $F(u, v)$ is replicated as a 2D lattice in the $(u, v)$ plane after sampling. Each cell of the lattice has dimensions $1/\Delta x$ (horizontal) by $1/\Delta y$ (vertical). Non-overlap is guaranteed only when both Nyquist inequalities hold.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[F of u v ORIGINAL SPECTRUM] --> B[REPLICA AT M EQUALS 0 N EQUALS 0]
    A --> C[REPLICA AT M EQUALS 1 N EQUALS 0 SHIFTED BY 1 BY DELTA x]
    A --> D[REPLICA AT M EQUALS 0 N EQUALS 1 SHIFTED BY 1 BY DELTA y]
    A --> E[REPLICA AT M EQUALS 1 N EQUALS 1 SHIFTED BY 1 BY DELTA x AND 1 BY DELTA y]
    A --> F[REPLICA AT M EQUALS NEG 1 N EQUALS 0 SHIFTED BY NEG 1 BY DELTA x]
    A --> G[REPLICA AT M EQUALS 0 N EQUALS NEG 1 SHIFTED BY NEG 1 BY DELTA y]
    H[BASEBAND REGION] -.GUARD BAND.-> B
    I[REJECTION REGION OF ANTI ALIASING FILTER] -.CUTOFF.-> H
```

## 4.3 Diagram — Aliasing Mechanism (Frequency Folding)

This block diagram illustrates how a frequency component outside the Nyquist region folds back into the baseband when the sampling rate is insufficient.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ORIGINAL FREQUENCY COMPONENT U ZERO AT U MAX] --> B[SAMPLING AT 1 BY DELTA x]
    B --> C{IS U MAX LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO 1 BY 2 DELTA x}
    C -- YES --> D[COMPONENT STAYS IN BASEBAND NO ALIASING]
    C -- NO --> E[COMPONENT FOLDS BACK AS U ALIAS EQUALS U MAX MINUS 1 BY DELTA x]
    E --> F[SPURIOUS LOW FREQUENCY MOIRE PATTERN APPEARS IN RECONSTRUCTED IMAGE]
    D --> G[2D SINC RECONSTRUCTION RECOVERS ORIGINAL]
    F --> H[RECONSTRUCTION IS IMPOSSIBLE WITHOUT PRIOR KNOWLEDGE]
```

## 4.4 Diagram — Practical 2D Sampling System (Camera/Scanner)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CONTINUOUS SCENE] --> B[OPTICAL LENS SYSTEM]
    B --> C[ANTI ALIASING OPTICAL LOW PASS FILTER]
    C --> D[2D SENSOR ARRAY PIXEL PITCH DELTA x AND DELTA y]
    D --> E[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERSION]
    E --> F[DIGITAL IMAGE M BY N PIXELS]
    F --> G[STORAGE OR DISPLAY]
    F --> H[POST PROCESSING 2D FILTERS EDGE DETECTION ETC]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**State and explain the 2D sampling theorem for band-limited images.**

**Model Answer:**

> The 2D sampling theorem states: *A continuous image $f(x, y)$ whose 2D Fourier spectrum $F(u, v)$ has finite support (is band-limited) such that $F(u, v) = 0$ for $\vert u \vert > u_{max}$ and $\vert v \vert > v_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $\{f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y)\}$ provided the sampling frequencies satisfy* $1/\Delta x \geq 2u_{max}$ **and** $1/\Delta y \geq 2v_{max}$ *(the 2D Nyquist criterion).*
>
> The reconstruction is given by the 2D sinc interpolation:
> $f(x, y) = \sum_m \sum_n f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y) \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{x - m\Delta x}{\Delta x}\right) \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{y - n\Delta y}{\Delta y}\right)$.
>
> If either Nyquist inequality is violated, spectral replicas overlap causing **aliasing** and the reconstruction is no longer unique.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is a Moiré pattern? How is it related to aliasing in 2D sampling?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Moiré pattern** is a large-scale, low-frequency interference fringe that appears in a sampled image when the original image contains fine, periodic spatial structures (e.g., a striped shirt or fine mesh) whose spatial frequency exceeds the sensor's Nyquist limit.
>
> In the frequency domain, the periodic structure in the image is replicated by the sampling process at intervals of $1/\Delta x$ and $1/\Delta y$. When these replicas overlap, the high-frequency content "folds back" into the baseband, producing a *spurious low-frequency beat* between the original and its replica. This beat is the Moiré pattern.
>
> Moiré is therefore a 2D-specific manifestation of **aliasing** and has no direct 1D counterpart. It is suppressed in practice by applying an optical low-pass (anti-aliasing) filter before sampling.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

**Derive the expression for the 2D Fourier transform of a sampled image. Using this, state and explain the 2D Nyquist sampling criterion. A band-limited image has maximum spatial frequencies $u_{max} = 300$ cycles/mm and $v_{max} = 250$ cycles/mm. Determine the minimum sampling rates required along the $x$ and $y$ axes, and state the corresponding maximum permissible sampling intervals.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation and 2D Nyquist Criterion (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define the 2D sampling grid (Dirac comb):**

$$
s(x, y) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(x - m\Delta x, y - n\Delta y)
$$

**Step 2 — Note separability:** $s(x, y) = s_x(x) \cdot s_y(y)$, where $s_x(x) = \sum_m \delta(x - m\Delta x)$ and $s_y(y) = \sum_n \delta(y - n\Delta y)$.

**Step 3 — Express the 1D comb using complex exponentials:**

$$
s_x(x) = \frac{1}{\Delta x} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi m x / \Delta x}
$$

The 2D comb is then:

$$
s(x, y) = \frac{1}{\Delta x \Delta y} \sum_m \sum_n e^{j 2\pi (m x / \Delta x + n y / \Delta y)}
$$

**Step 4 — Sample the image by multiplication in the spatial domain:**

$$
f_s(x, y) = f(x, y) \cdot s(x, y) = \frac{1}{\Delta x \Delta y} \sum_m \sum_n f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y) \, \delta(x - m\Delta x, y - n\Delta y)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the 2D Convolution Theorem.** Multiplication in $(x, y)$-space $\Leftrightarrow$ Convolution in $(u, v)$-space:

$$
F_s(u, v) = F(u, v) * S(u, v)
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $S(u, v)$.** Since the 2D comb is separable, its FT is separable:

$$
S(u, v) = \frac{1}{\Delta x \Delta y} \sum_m \sum_n \delta\left(u - \frac{m}{\Delta x}\right) \delta\left(v - \frac{n}{\Delta y}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Perform the convolution:** Convolution with an impulse lattice shifts $F$ to every lattice point:

$$
F_s(u, v) = \frac{1}{\Delta x \Delta y} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} F\left(u - \frac{m}{\Delta x}, v - \frac{n}{\Delta y}\right)
$$

**Step 8 — State the 2D Nyquist criterion:** The replicas of $F$ are non-overlapping if and only if the support of $F$ is contained within a rectangle of half-widths $1/(2\Delta x)$ and $1/(2\Delta y)$:

$$
\frac{1}{\Delta x} \geq 2 u_{max} \quad \text{AND} \quad \frac{1}{\Delta y} \geq 2 v_{max}
$$

**Step 9 — Engineering implication:** Both inequalities must hold **simultaneously**; a single failure causes aliasing in that axis.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Determination of Sampling Parameters (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the $x$-axis Nyquist inequality:**

$$
\frac{1}{\Delta x} \geq 2 u_{max} = 2 \times 300 = 600 \text{ cycles/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the minimum $x$-sampling rate and maximum $x$-interval:**

$$
u_{s,x} = 600 \text{ samples/mm}, \quad \Delta x_{max} = \frac{1}{600} \approx 1.667 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the $y$-axis Nyquist inequality:**

$$
\frac{1}{\Delta y} \geq 2 v_{max} = 2 \times 250 = 500 \text{ cycles/mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the minimum $y$-sampling rate and maximum $y$-interval:**

$$
v_{s,y} = 500 \text{ samples/mm}, \quad \Delta y_{max} = \frac{1}{500} = 2.0 \, \mu m
$$

**Step 5 — Practical PPI conversion (1 mm = 25.4 inch):**

$$
u_{s,x} = 600 \times 25.4 = 15{,}240 \text{ PPI}, \quad v_{s,y} = 500 \times 25.4 = 12{,}700 \text{ PPI}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** To sample this image without aliasing, the sensor must have a pitch finer than $\min(\Delta x_{max}, \Delta y_{max}) = 1.667 \, \mu m$ along both axes. Using a **square** grid of $1.667 \, \mu m$ pitch automatically satisfies both constraints.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting that the **2D** Nyquist criterion requires **two** simultaneous inequalities. Students often write only $1/\Delta x \geq 2 u_{max}$ and ignore the $y$-axis.
> 2. Confusing **sampling frequency** (cycles/mm) with **spatial frequency of the signal** (cycles/mm). They are different quantities even though both use the same unit.
> 3. Failing to include the factor $1/(\Delta x \Delta y)$ in the replicated spectrum — this is a 1-mark deduction in most valuation schemes.
> 4. Concluding "aliasing occurs" without specifying *which axis* failed the Nyquist condition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**Explain the concept of aliasing in 2D sampling with suitable diagrams in both spatial and frequency domains. Discuss the conditions under which aliasing occurs, the visual artefacts it produces (including Moiré patterns), and the standard engineering remedy (anti-aliasing filtering). Illustrate with the example of sampling a sinusoidal grating of frequency 250 cycles/mm on a sensor with $\Delta x = 3 \, \mu m$ — determine whether aliasing occurs and identify the aliased frequency if so.**

#### Part (a) — Aliasing Theory and Moiré Patterns (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define aliasing in the 2D context:** Aliasing is the phenomenon in which two *distinct* continuous signals produce the *same* set of samples after 2D sampling, making exact reconstruction impossible.

**Step 2 — Frequency-domain viewpoint:** The spectrum of a sampled image is the **periodic replication** of the original spectrum at intervals $(1/\Delta x, 1/\Delta y)$:

$$
F_s(u, v) = \frac{1}{\Delta x \Delta y} \sum_m \sum_n F\!\left(u - \frac{m}{\Delta x}, v - \frac{n}{\Delta y}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — When overlap occurs:** If any spectral component of $F(u, v)$ lies outside the rectangle $\vert u \vert \leq 1/(2\Delta x)$ and $\vert v \vert \leq 1/(2\Delta y)$, the replica centred at $(m, n) \neq (0, 0)$ will overlap the baseband.
