The overlapping components **add**, producing a corrupted baseband that cannot be separated by any linear filter.

**Step 4 — Visual artefact — Moiré pattern:** When the original image has a periodic high-frequency structure (e.g., a sinusoidal grating or fine textile weave), the overlap of spectral replicas creates a low-frequency beat. The frequency of this beat is:

$$
f_{moire} = \left| u_0 - k \cdot u_s \right| \quad \text{for the integer } k \text{ that places it in the baseband}
$$

This beat is the Moiré pattern, an *artefact* with no counterpart in the original image.

**Step 5 — Real-world occurrences:**
- Photography of a striped shirt on TV.
- Halftone printing (dot pattern interacting with image content).
- Medical ultrasound speckle.
- CT/MRI with insufficient k-space sampling.

**Step 6 — Standard remedy — anti-aliasing pre-filtering:** Insert a 2D low-pass filter with cutoff frequencies $u_c = 1/(2\Delta x)$ and $v_c = 1/(2\Delta y)$ *before* the sampling stage. This guarantees the post-filter spectrum satisfies the band-limited condition and prevents overlap. In cameras, this is implemented by a birefringent crystal (bilateral low-pass) or a micro-lens array; in software, by a Gaussian or Lanczos pre-filter.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Illustration with Sinusoidal Grating (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define the input signal:** A 1D sinusoidal grating (varying along $x$, constant along $y$) with frequency $u_0 = 250$ cycles/mm. Its spectrum consists of two impulses at $u = \pm 250$ cycles/mm.

**Step 2 — Sensor sampling rate:** $\Delta x = 3 \, \mu m = 0.003 \, mm$, so

$$
u_s = \frac{1}{\Delta x} = \frac{1}{0.003} \approx 333.33 \text{ cycles/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Nyquist frequency:**

$$
u_{Nyq} = \frac{u_s}{2} \approx 166.67 \text{ cycles/mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare $u_0$ to $u_{Nyq}$:** $u_0 = 250 > 166.67 = u_{Nyq}$. Therefore, **aliasing occurs**.

**Step 5 — Compute the aliased frequency.** The $k=1$ replica of the impulse at $u_0 = 250$ lies at:

$$
u_0 - u_s = 250 - 333.33 = -83.33 \text{ cycles/mm}
$$

Its absolute value (since frequency is a magnitude in the baseband) is $83.33$ cycles/mm.

**Step 6 — State the aliased (apparent) frequency:** The reconstructed image will show a sinusoid of frequency $\approx 83.33$ cycles/mm, *opposite in phase* to the original (because the sign flipped). This is the Moiré beat.

**Step 7 — Verify via the alias formula:** Using $u_{alias} = \vert u_0 - k \cdot u_s \vert$ with $k = 1$:

$$
u_{alias} = \vert 250 - 333.33 \vert = 83.33 \text{ cycles/mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Practical interpretation:** A grating of true frequency 250 cycles/mm will appear in the digitised image as if it were a much coarser grating of 83.33 cycles/mm — a clear, severe aliasing artefact.

**Step 9 — Remedy:** Either reduce $\Delta x$ below $1/(2 \times 250) = 2 \, \mu m$ (i.e., use a higher-resolution sensor), or pre-filter the input with a low-pass filter of cutoff $u_{Nyq} = 166.67$ cycles/mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value / sign-flip:** When the aliased frequency is reported, students often forget that the apparent frequency is the magnitude $\vert u_0 - k u_s \vert$, and may also miss the sign-flip indicating phase reversal.
> 2. **Choosing the wrong $k$:** The correct $k$ is the one that places the result inside the baseband $\vert u_{alias} \vert \leq u_{Nyq}$. Students sometimes take $k = 2$ or $k = 0$ and obtain nonsensical results.
> 3. **Confusing Moiré with other artefacts:** Moiré is *specifically* an aliasing beat between two periodic structures (image content and sampling grid). It is not the same as noise, blurring, or compression artefact.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — 2D Sampling (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** 2D sampling = measuring a continuous image $f(x, y)$ at points of a rectangular grid with spacings $\Delta x, \Delta y$.
- **2D Dirac Comb:** $s(x, y) = \sum_m \sum_n \delta(x - m\Delta x, y - n\Delta y)$ — the mathematical model of the sampling grid.
- **Sampled Image:** $f_s(x, y) = f(x, y) \cdot s(x, y) = \sum_m \sum_n f(m\Delta x, n\Delta y) \, \delta(x - m\Delta x, y - n\Delta y)$.
- **Spectrum of Sampled Image:** $F_s(u, v) = \dfrac{1}{\Delta x \Delta y} \sum_m \sum_n F\!\left(u - \dfrac{m}{\Delta x}, v - \dfrac{n}{\Delta y}\right)$ — *periodic replication* in the $(u, v)$-plane.
- **2D Nyquist Criterion (mandatory two inequalities):**
  - $1/\Delta x \geq 2 u_{max}$ **AND** $1/\Delta y \geq 2 v_{max}$.
- **Nyquist Frequency per axis:** $u_{Nyq} = 1/(2\Delta x)$ and $v_{Nyq} = 1/(2\Delta y)$.
- **Reconstruction (sinc interpolation):** Valid **only** if Nyquist is satisfied; otherwise reconstruction is non-unique.
- **Aliasing condition:** $u_{max} > u_{Nyq}$ **OR** $v_{max} > v_{Nyq}$ → spectral overlap.
- **Aliasing formula:** Apparent (aliased) frequency $u_{alias} = \vert u_0 - k \cdot u_s \vert$ for the appropriate integer $k$ such that the result lies in the baseband.
- **Moiré Pattern:** A 2D-specific aliasing artefact — low-frequency beat between image content and the sampling grid; absent in 1D.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A 2D low-pass filter applied **before** sampling, with cutoffs $u_c = u_{Nyq}$ and $v_c = v_{Nyq}$.
- **Practical metrics:** Sensor pitch (µm), DPI/PPI (dots/pixels per inch), cycles/mm (spatial frequency). Conversions: $1$ mm $= 25.4$ inch.
- **Tensor product viewpoint:** 2D sampling is the *separable* product of two 1D samplings, but the resulting 2D phenomena (Moiré, anisotropy) require a genuinely 2D analysis.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** For a square-pixel grid, always choose $\Delta = \min(\Delta x_{max}, \Delta y_{max})$ to satisfy both axes simultaneously.
- **Common unit pitfall:** $u_s$ (sampling frequency, cycles/mm) and $u_{max}$ (signal bandwidth, cycles/mm) are numerically comparable but conceptually distinct — they are not the same quantity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantization, Review of Matrix Theory - Row and Column Ordering - Toeplitz
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quantization, Review of Matrix Theory - Row and Column Ordering - Toeplitz

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Quantization in Image Digitization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Quantization** is the process of mapping a continuous range of sampled intensity (or amplitude) values into a finite, discrete set of reconstruction levels. In a digital image, it assigns each sample a value from a finite set of integers (e.g., 0 to 255 for 8-bit images), thereby converting the **continuous-amplitude** representation into a **discrete-amplitude** one.

In the image acquisition chain, two independent discretization operations occur:
- **Sampling** → discretizes the **spatial domain** (x, y).
- **Quantization** → discretizes the **intensity/amplitude domain**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Rounding-Off Analogy"**
> Imagine a temperature reading of 27.834 °C on a digital thermometer that can only display whole numbers. The thermometer rounds the value to **28 °C**. The rounding error is **0.166 °C**. Image quantization works identically: it restricts the infinite continuum of real-world intensities to a small, finite palette of representable levels. The more levels you allow, the smaller the "rounding error" and the more faithful the digital reproduction.

**Key terms to memorize:**
- **Quantization Level:** A specific discrete value from the allowed set.
- **Quantization Interval (Step Size $\Delta$):** The width between two adjacent representable levels.
- **Quantization Error (e):** The difference between the true analog value and the assigned digital level.
- **Dynamic Range:** The ratio of maximum to minimum representable intensity.

For a uniform quantizer with $L$ levels covering the range $[f_{min}, f_{max}]$:
$$\Delta = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{L}$$

---

### 1.2 Review of Matrix Theory — Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **matrix** $\mathbf{A}$ of size $M \times N$ is a rectangular array of $MN$ real or complex numbers arranged in $M$ rows and $N$ columns. It is a fundamental object for representing images (which are 2-D signals) and the linear operators that act on them (filters, transforms).

**Essential notation for KTU Image Processing:**

- **Scalar:** $a$ (lowercase, italic, no brackets)
- **Vector:** $\mathbf{a}$ (lowercase, bold) — $N \times 1$ column vector
- **Matrix:** $\mathbf{A}$ (uppercase, bold) — $M \times N$
- **Transpose:** $\mathbf{A}^T$ — swap rows and columns
- **Identity matrix:** $\mathbf{I}$ — diagonal entries = 1
- **Zero matrix:** $\mathbf{0}$

**Why matrices matter in DIP:** An $M \times N$ image is naturally represented as a matrix where the entry $f(i,j)$ is the intensity at pixel $(i,j)$. Linear filters, geometric transforms, and even 1-D operations are recast as matrix multiplications for analytical convenience.

---

### 1.3 Row and Column Ordering (Vectorization)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Row Ordering (Row-Major)** and **Column Ordering (Column-Major)** are two conventions for converting (lexicographically) a 2-D matrix into a 1-D vector by sequentially reading entries either along rows or along columns.

In KTU 2024 scheme Digital Image Processing, this is the foundation of the **vectorization operator** $\text{vec}(\cdot)$ which underpins algebraic image manipulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Reading a Book"**
> Think of a 2-D grid of cells as a newspaper page. **Row-major ordering** is the Western reading style: you read the first row left-to-right, then move to the next row. **Column-major ordering** is the style used in Fortran and MATLAB for stacking columns: you read the first column top-to-bottom, then move to the next column. The same matrix produces two different 1-D vectors depending on the convention — a critical detail when writing code or expressing filters algebraically.

---

### 1.4 Toeplitz Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Toeplitz matrix** is a matrix in which **each descending diagonal from left to right is constant**. Equivalently, if $\mathbf{T}$ is Toeplitz, then its entries satisfy:
> $$T(i,j) = t_{i-j}$$
> for some scalar sequence $\{t_k\}$. The matrix is therefore fully determined by its **first row** and **first column**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Staircase Cladding"**
> Imagine tiling a floor with rectangular tiles. A Toeplitz matrix is like a staircase that looks identical along every diagonal: each "step" you take downwards-rightwards is the same color. The constant along every northwest-to-southeast diagonal makes the matrix look like it has a "diagonal-periodic" texture — and this exact structure appears whenever we model 1-D convolutions (like a 1-D filter sliding across a row of pixels).

**Variants relevant to KTU syllabus:**
- **Toeplitz matrix:** constant along NW-SE diagonals.
- **Block-Toeplitz matrix:** each block is itself Toeplitz.
- **Circulant matrix:** a Toeplitz matrix that is also **cyclic** — each row is a cyclic shift of the previous row. Used in DFT-based filtering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Quantization — Operational Breakdown

**Step 1 — Determine Quantizer Range**
The image intensity values are bounded: $f \in [f_{min}, f_{max}]$.

**Step 2 — Choose Number of Bits $k$**
The number of discrete levels is $L = 2^k$.
- $k = 8$ → $L = 256$ levels (standard 8-bit grayscale)
- $k = 1$ → $L = 2$ levels (1-bit bilevel)

**Step 3 — Compute Step Size**
For a **uniform** quantizer:
$$\Delta = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{2^k}$$

**Step 4 — Map Each Sample to a Level**
Each continuous value $f$ is assigned to the nearest level $q$:
$$q = \text{round}\left(\frac{f - f_{min}}{\Delta}\right) \cdot \Delta + f_{min}$$

**Step 5 — Quantization Error**
$$e = f - q \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e \le \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)**
For a uniform quantizer with sinusoidal input of full dynamic range, the SQNR simplifies to the **canonical KTU formula**:
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \, k + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "6 dB rule":** Each additional bit of quantization improves SQNR by approximately **6 dB**, equivalent to doubling the precision.

---

### 2.2 Vectorization — Operational Breakdown

For an $M \times N$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$:

**Row-Ordered Vectorization (used in C, Python, Java):**
$$\mathbf{a}_{row} = [A_{1,1}, A_{1,2}, \ldots, A_{1,N}, A_{2,1}, A_{2,2}, \ldots, A_{M,N}]^T$$

**Column-Ordered Vectorization (used in MATLAB, Fortran):**
$$\mathbf{a}_{col} = [A_{1,1}, A_{2,1}, \ldots, A_{M,1}, A_{1,2}, A_{2,2}, \ldots, A_{M,N}]^T$$

A key algebraic identity taught in the KTU matrix review is the **vec-transpose property**:
$$\text{vec}(\mathbf{A}^T) = \mathbf{P} \cdot \text{vec}(\mathbf{A})$$
where $\mathbf{P}$ is the **commutation matrix** (a permutation matrix of size $MN \times MN$).

---

### 2.3 Toeplitz Matrix — Operational Breakdown

**Step 1 — Identify the Generating Sequence**
For a $3 \times 3$ Toeplitz matrix, the generating sequence is
$$[t_{-2}, t_{-1}, t_0, t_1, t_2]$$
where $t_0$ is the diagonal element and $t_k$ for $k \neq 0$ are the off-diagonals.

**Step 2 — Fill by Diagonals**
The entry at position $(i,j)$ is $t_{i-j}$. The matrix becomes:

$$
\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} t_0 & t_1 & t_2 \\ t_{-1} & t_0 & t_1 \\ t_{-2} & t_{-1} & t_0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Use in DIP: 1-D Convolution as Matrix-Vector Product**
A 1-D convolution $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{h} * \mathbf{x}$ can be written as:
$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H} \, \mathbf{x}$$
where $\mathbf{H}$ is a **Toeplitz (or Toeplitz-plus-circulant)** matrix whose rows are shifted copies of the filter $\mathbf{h}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Digital Filtering:** Every 1-D FIR/IIR filter applied to an image row is a Toeplitz (or convolution) matrix.
> - **Image Restoration:** Deconvolution requires inverting (or pseudo-inverting) Toeplitz systems.
> - **Compressed Sensing:** Toeplitz and circulant sensing matrices enable fast, FFT-based image reconstruction.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Notes / Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Number of levels | $L = 2^k$ | $k$ = bits per pixel |
| 2 | Step size | $\Delta = \dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{2^k}$ | Intensity units (e.g., a.u.) |
| 3 | Quantization error range | $-\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \le e \le \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Bounded by half a step |
| 4 | SQNR (full-range sinusoid) | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,k + 1.76$ | dB |
| 5 | Bit-rate / pixel | $b = k$ | bits/pixel |
| 6 | Image size in memory | $M \times N \times k$ | bits |
| 7 | Inner product | $\langle \mathbf{a},\mathbf{b}\rangle = \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b} = \sum_i a_i b_i$ | Scalar |
| 8 | Outer product | $\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b}^T$ produces an $M \times N$ matrix | — |
| 9 | Toeplitz condition | $T(i,j) = t_{i-j}$ | Diagonals constant |
| 10 | Toeplitz matrix size | $M \times N$ determined by $M + N - 1$ values | — |
| 11 | Circulant special case | Each row = cyclic shift of first row | DFT diagonalizes it |
| 12 | vec-trick for Kronecker | $\text{vec}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{C}) = (\mathbf{C}^T \otimes \mathbf{A})\,\text{vec}(\mathbf{B})$ | Used in image transforms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in KTU Context:** These formulas appear in 2-mark derivations, 7-mark problems on filter representations, and image-storage calculations. The SQNR formula is a **board-favourite** and has appeared in multiple KTU past papers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the SQNR Formula for Uniform Quantization

We model a uniform $k$-bit quantizer covering a dynamic range $F = f_{max} - f_{min}$. The step size is:
$$\Delta = \frac{F}{2^k}$$

**Step 1 — Assumed distribution of the input signal**
For a sinusoidal input spanning the full range, the probability density function is non-uniform, but its **mean-square value** is:
$$\overline{f^2} = \frac{F^2}{8}$$

This represents the average signal power.

**Step 2 — Model the quantization error**
Assuming the error $e$ is uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$ and uncorrelated with the signal, the **mean-square error** is:
$$\overline{e^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot p(e)\, de = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de$$

Evaluating the integral:
$$\overline{e^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta^3}{24} - \left(-\frac{\Delta^3}{24}\right) \right) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12}$$

$$\overline{e^2} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Form the SNR**
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{\overline{f^2}}{\overline{e^2}} = \frac{F^2 / 8}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{F^2}{8} \cdot \frac{12}{\Delta^2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{F^2}{\Delta^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta = F / 2^k$**
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{F^2}{(F/2^k)^2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2k} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2k}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 2^{2k}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(2^{2k})$$

Using $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$:
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20k \log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02k$$

Thus:
$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02k + 1.76}$$

> **Conversion logic summary:** We assumed (a) full-range sinusoidal input, (b) uniform error distribution, (c) independence of signal and error. These are the same assumptions used in standard KTU textbooks and they yield the canonical "6-dB-per-bit" rule.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Example — Vectorizing a $2 \times 3$ Image Patch

Let the image patch be:
$$\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 & 30 \\ 40 & 50 & 60 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Row-Ordering (C/Python style):**
$$\text{vec}_{row}(\mathbf{F}) = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60]^T$$

**Column-Ordering (MATLAB style):**
$$\text{vec}_{col}(\mathbf{F}) = [10, 40, 20, 50, 30, 60]^T$$

**Conversion relationship:** The two vectors are related by a fixed permutation:
$$\text{vec}_{row}(\mathbf{F}) = \mathbf{P} \cdot \text{vec}_{col}(\mathbf{F})$$

For our $2 \times 3$ patch, $\mathbf{P}$ is a $6 \times 6$ permutation matrix.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Example — Constructing a Toeplitz Filter Matrix

Consider a 1-D FIR filter with impulse response:
$$\mathbf{h} = [h_0, h_1, h_2] = [1, 2, 3]$$

We want to convolve this with a signal $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4]^T$ (length 4) to produce an output $\mathbf{y}$ of length $4 + 3 - 1 = 6$ (full convolution).

**The convolution matrix (Toeplitz) is:**
$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification of Toeplitz property:**
- $(1,2)$ entry = 0, $(2,3)$ entry = 0, $(3,4)$ entry = 0. All on super-diagonal → constant ✓
- $(2,1)$ entry = 2, $(3,2)$ entry = 2, $(4,3)$ entry = 2. All on sub-diagonal → constant ✓
- Main diagonal entries are all 1 ✓

> **Conversion logic summary:** The Toeplitz structure arises because convolution is a **shift-invariant linear operation** — applying the same filter at every position. Each row of $\mathbf{H}$ is the filter impulse response shifted by one sample to the right relative to the previous row, perfectly satisfying the Toeplitz condition $H(i,j) = h_{i-j}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Example — Quantizer Step-by-Step

Let an image's intensity range be $[0, 15]$ and suppose we use a 4-bit quantizer.

**Step 1 — Number of levels:**
$$L = 2^4 = 16$$

**Step 2 — Step size:**
$$\Delta = \frac{15 - 0}{16} = 0.9375$$

**Step 3 — Input sample:** Suppose $f = 7.42$.

**Step 4 — Quantization index:**
$$q_{idx} = \text{round}\left( \frac{7.42 - 0}{0.9375} \right) = \text{round}(7.914) = 8$$

**Step 5 — Reconstructed level:**
$$q = 8 \times 0.9375 + 0 = 7.5$$

**Step 6 — Quantization error:**
$$e = 7.42 - 7.5 = -0.08$$

**Step 7 — Verification of error bound:**
$$-\Delta/2 = -0.46875 \le -0.08 \le 0.46875 = \Delta/2 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conversion logic summary:** Each real-valued sample is mapped to one of 16 discrete levels. The "round" function uses nearest-neighbor assignment. The reconstructed value is then the chosen level. The error is guaranteed to lie within half a quantization step.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Image Digitization Pipeline

This diagram shows how **sampling** and **quantization** are the two sub-stages of image digitization, and how matrix theory ties in.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    scene[Continuous Scene] --> sampler[2D Spatial Sampler]
    sampler --> continuousSample[Sampled Continuous-Amplitude Image]
    continuousSample --> quantizer[Quantizer L = 2k Levels]
    quantizer --> digitalImage[Digital Image Matrix F MxN]
    digitalImage --> matrixOp[Matrix Algebraic Operations]
    matrixOp --> filterOut[Filtered/Transformed Image]
    filterOut --> display[Display/Storage]

    subgraph "Matrix Theory Layer"
        vectorize[Row / Column Ordering vec Operation]
        toeplitzStruct[Toeplitz / Circulant Filter Matrices]
    end

    matrixOp --> vectorize
    matrixOp --> toeplitzStruct

    classDef stage fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef math fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    class sampler,quantizer,digitalImage,filterOut stage
    class vectorize,toeplitzStruct,matrixOp math
```

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Flow: Quantization Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cont[Continuous Intensity Axis 0 to 15] --> divider[Divide into L = 2k Intervals]
    divider --> assign[Round Each Sample to Nearest Level]
    assign --> reconstructed[Discrete Levels q0, q1, ... qL-1]
    reconstructed --> err[Quantization Error e = f minus q]
    err --> sqnr[SQNR Computation 6.02k + 1.76 dB]

    classDef proc fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef out  fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    class divider,assign cont
    class reconstructed,err,sqnr out
```

---

### 4.3 Toeplitz Structure — Visual Pattern

```mermaid
graph TB
    main[Toeplitz Matrix T MxN] --> cond1[Property T i j equals t subscript i minus j]
    cond1 --> diag[All NW to SE Diagonals are Constant]
    diag --> row1[First Row: t0, t1, t2, ... tN-1]
    diag --> col1[First Column: t0, t-1, t-2, ... t-m+1]
    row1 --> full[Matrix Determined by M + N minus 1 Values]
    col1 --> full

    full --> variant1[Special Case Circulant: Right Wraparound]
    full --> variant2[Special Case Hankel: Anti-Toeplitz]
    full --> variant3[Block-Toeplitz: Each Block is Toeplitz]

    classDef def fill:#fff0e1,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#e1e1ff,stroke:#000066,color:#000
    class main,cond1,diag,full def
    class row1,col1,variant1,variant2,variant3 sub
```

---

### 4.4 Row vs Column Ordering — Vectorization Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mat2D[2D Matrix F M by N] --> choice{Ordering Convention}
    choice -- Row Major C, Python --> rowVec[vec row: 11, 12, ..., 1N, 21, 22, ...]
    choice -- Column Major MATLAB --> colVec[vec col: 11, 21, ..., M1, 12, 22, ...]
    rowVec --> perm[Permutation Matrix P]
    colVec --> perm
    perm --> relation[vec row F equals P times vec col F]
    relation --> filter[Used in Toeplitz Filter Construction]

    classDef inp fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    class mat2D,choice inp
    class rowVec,colVec,relation,filter out
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEAET635]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] Define Quantization. What is quantization error?**
*(Exemplar: This is a foundational definition question — examiners expect precise terminology.)*

**Model Answer:**
Quantization is the process of converting the continuous intensity values of a sampled image into a finite set of discrete amplitude levels. For a $k$-bit quantizer, the number of representable levels is $L = 2^k$.

**Quantization error** $e$ is the difference between the original continuous intensity $f$ and its quantized value $q$:
$$e = f - q$$
For a uniform quantizer, this error is bounded by half the step size:
$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e \le \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

The step size is:
$$\Delta = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{2^k}$$

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] What is a Toeplitz matrix? Give a $3 \times 3$ example.**
*(Exemplar: A standard "state the definition + construct example" question.)*

**Model Answer:**
A **Toeplitz matrix** is a matrix in which each descending diagonal (from top-left to bottom-right) has a constant value. Its entries satisfy $T(i,j) = t_{i-j}$, meaning the matrix is fully specified by its first row and first column.

**Example:** A $3 \times 3$ Toeplitz matrix:
$$\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 1 & 2 \\ 5 & 4 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
- Main diagonal: 1, 1, 1 (constant) ✓
- First super-diagonal: 2, 2 (constant) ✓
- First sub-diagonal: 4, 4 (constant) ✓

This matrix arises in image processing whenever a 1-D filter is convolved with a signal — the convolution matrix is Toeplitz.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model End-Semester Question, PEAET635]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the image quantization process in detail. Derive the SQNR for a uniform $k$-bit quantizer and show that each extra bit adds approximately 6 dB to the SQNR.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and Process:**
Image quantization maps continuous intensities $f \in [f_{min}, f_{max}]$ into $L = 2^k$ discrete levels. The quantizer divides the range into uniform intervals of width:
$$\Delta = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{2^k}$$
Each sample is assigned the value at the **center** of its interval, introducing a quantization error $e = f - q$.

**2. Assumptions for SQNR derivation:**
- Input is a **full-range sinusoid**: $\overline{f^2} = F^2/8$
- Quantization error is **uniformly distributed** in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$
- Error is **uncorrelated** with the signal

**3. Mean-square error computation:**
$$\overline{e^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} de = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**4. SQNR ratio:**
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{\overline{f^2}}{\overline{e^2}} = \frac{F^2/8}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{F^2}{\Delta^2}$$

**5. Substituting $\Delta = F/2^k$:**
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2k} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2k}$$

**6. In decibels:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20k \log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02k$$

**7. Per-bit gain:**
$$\Delta \text{SQNR} = 6.02(k+1) + 1.76 - (6.02k + 1.76) = 6.02 \text{ dB}$$

This proves the **6-dB-per-bit rule**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Define a Toeplitz matrix. Show that the convolution of a 1-D signal with an FIR filter can be represented as multiplication by a Toeplitz matrix. Illustrate with a specific numerical example.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition:** A matrix $\mathbf{T}$ of size $M \times N$ is Toeplitz if $T(i,j) = t_{i-j}$ for all valid $i, j$. Each diagonal parallel to the main diagonal is constant.

**2. 1-D Convolution as Matrix-Vector Product:**
Given a filter $\mathbf{h} = [h_0, h_1, h_2]$ and signal $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4]^T$, the full convolution is:
$$y_n = \sum_{k=0}^{2} h_k \cdot x_{n-k}$$

Writing all 6 output samples in matrix form:
$$\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ y_3 \\ y_4 \\ y_5 \\ y_6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ h_1 & h_0 & 0 & 0 \\ h_2 & h_1 & h_0 & 0 \\ 0 & h_2 & h_1 & h_0 \\ 0 & 0 & h_2 & h_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & h_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \\ x_4 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$$

**3. Numerical example:** Let $\mathbf{h} = [1, 2, 3]$, $\mathbf{x} = [1, 1, 1, 1]^T$.
$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} = [1, 3, 6, 6, 5, 3]^T$$
This matches direct convolution: $1; 1+2; 1+2+3; 1+2+3; 2+3; 3$.

**4. Toeplitz verification:** Each descending diagonal of $\mathbf{H}$ is constant. ✓

**Conclusion:** Convolution with a shift-invariant filter is naturally expressed as multiplication by a Toeplitz (or circulant) matrix — a cornerstone of linear image filtering theory.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain Row and Column Ordering of matrices with a $3 \times 3$ example. State the role of the commutation matrix in converting between the two orderings.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definitions:**
- **Row Ordering (Row-Major):** Matrix entries are linearized by traversing each row from left to right, then moving to the next row.
- **Column Ordering (Column-Major):** Entries are linearized by traversing each column from top to bottom, then moving to the next column.

**2. Example:** Let
$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

- **Row-ordered vector:**
$$\text{vec}_{row}(\mathbf{A}) = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]^T$$

- **Column-ordered vector:**
$$\text{vec}_{col}(\mathbf{A}) = [1, 4, 7, 2, 5, 8, 3, 6, 9]^T$$

**3. Commutation Matrix:**
The two vectors are related by a permutation matrix $\mathbf{P}$ (commutation matrix) of size $9 \times 9$:
$$\text{vec}_{row}(\mathbf{A}) = \mathbf{P} \cdot \text{vec}_{col}(\mathbf{A})$$

For our example, $\mathbf{P}$ simply permutes the positions to swap row-major and column-major orderings. This matrix is symmetric, orthogonal ($\mathbf{P}^T = \mathbf{P}^{-1}$), and $\mathbf{P}^2 = \mathbf{I}$.

**4. Significance in DIP:** Vectorization is essential for:
- Expressing 2-D image filters as 1-D linear systems
- Connecting spatial-domain filtering to Kronecker-product representations
- Reformulating image restoration as a least-squares problem

---

**(b) [7 Marks] An image has continuous intensities in the range $[0, 7]$. Compute the quantization step size, the reconstructed level, and the quantization error for an input sample $f = 3.4$ using (i) a 2-bit uniform quantizer, (ii) a 3-bit uniform quantizer. Comment on the SQNR improvement.**

**Model Solution:**

**Case (i): 2-bit quantizer**

Number of levels: $L = 2^2 = 4$
Step size: $\Delta = (7-0)/4 = 1.75$

Quantization index:
$$q_{idx} = \text{round}\left( \frac{3.4 - 0}{1.75} \right) = \text{round}(1.943) = 2$$

Reconstructed level:
$$q = 2 \times 1.75 = 3.5$$

Quantization error:
$$e_2 = 3.4 - 3.5 = -0.1$$

SQNR (using sinusoidal model):
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02(2) + 1.76 = 13.80 \text{ dB}$$

---

**Case (ii): 3-bit quantizer**

Number of levels: $L = 2^3 = 8$
Step size: $\Delta = (7-0)/8 = 0.875$

Quantization index:
$$q_{idx} = \text{round}\left( \frac{3.4}{0.875} \right) = \text{round}(3.886) = 4$$

Reconstructed level:
$$q = 4 \times 0.875 = 3.5$$

Quantization error:
$$e_3 = 3.4 - 3.5 = -0.1$$

SQNR:
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02(3) + 1.76 = 19.82 \text{ dB}$$

---

**Comment on SQNR improvement:**
$$\Delta\text{SQNR} = 19.82 - 13.80 = 6.02 \text{ dB}$$

This matches the **6-dB-per-bit rule** exactly. Adding one bit doubles the number of quantization levels, halves the step size $\Delta$, quarters the mean-square quantization error, and improves SQNR by 6.02 dB.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing **sampling** with **quantization**. Sampling discretizes space; quantization discretizes amplitude. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> - **Mistake 2:** Using the **wrong base-2 logarithm conversion** in the SQNR derivation. Remember: $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02$, not 3 dB.
> - **Mistake 3:** For Toeplitz, students often draw the matrix but forget to **explicitly state the constant-diagonal property**. Always write: "Each descending diagonal is constant."
> - **Mistake 4:** In row/column ordering, students swap the conventions (labeling MATLAB's style as "row-major"). Recall: MATLAB = **column-major**; C/Python = **row-major**.
> - **Mistake 5:** Skipping the **assumption statement** in SQNR derivations. Always declare: (a) full-range sinusoidal input, (b) uniform error distribution, (c) signal-error independence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Quantization** maps continuous amplitudes → discrete levels; **sampling** maps continuous space → discrete pixels.
- **Number of levels:** $L = 2^k$ where $k$ = number of bits.
- **Step size:** $\Delta = (f_{max} - f_{min}) / 2^k$.
- **Quantization error bound:** $e \in [-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$.
- **SQNR formula (board favourite):** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02k + 1.76$.
- **6-dB rule:** Every extra bit → +6 dB SQNR, OR $\Delta$ halved, OR error variance quartered.
- **Memory needed for image:** $M \times N \times k$ bits.
- **Matrix terminology:** $\mathbf{a}$ = vector, $\mathbf{A}$ = matrix, $\mathbf{A}^T$ = transpose, $\mathbf{I}$ = identity.
- **Row ordering** = read each row left-to-right, then next row (C/Python style).
- **Column ordering** = read each column top-to-bottom, then next column (MATLAB style).
- **Conversion between orderings** uses the **commutation matrix** $\mathbf{P}$.
- **Toeplitz matrix:** $T(i,j) = t_{i-j}$ → all NW-to-SE diagonals are constant.
- **Toeplitz size:** determined by **$M + N - 1$** values.
- **Convolution matrix** is always Toeplitz (and circulant if cyclic boundary conditions are used).
- **Circulant matrix** is a Toeplitz matrix with right-wraparound — diagonalized by the DFT.
- **Block-Toeplitz:** each block is itself Toeplitz — used for 2-D convolution.
- **Use in DIP:** every linear shift-invariant filter (smoothing, sharpening, edge detection) is representable as a Toeplitz (or circulant) matrix.
- **Engineering utility:** Toeplitz systems enable fast solvers (Levinson–Durbin recursion) and FFT-based filtering via circulant embedding.
- **Common KTU trap:** Confusing $10 \log_{10}$ (power ratio) with $20 \log_{10}$ (amplitude ratio). SQNR uses **power ratio → 10 log**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circulant and Block Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Circulant and Block Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — TOPIC: CIRCULANT AND BLOCK MATRIX

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Circulant Matrix?

A **Circulant Matrix** is a special class of square matrix in which every successive row is a **cyclic right-shift (or left-shift)** of the preceding row by exactly one position. Equivalently, each column is also a cyclic shift of the preceding column. This highly structured form makes circulant matrices diagonalizable by the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) matrix — a property of immense importance in Digital Image Processing (DIP) and convolution theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An $N \times N$ matrix $C$ is called *circulant* if it is generated by a single generating vector $\mathbf{c} = [c_0, c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_{N-1}]^T$ such that the $i$-th row is obtained by cyclically shifting $\mathbf{c}$ to the right by $i$ positions. The compact notation used is $C = \text{circ}(c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_{N-1})$.

A canonical $4 \times 4$ circulant matrix generated by $\mathbf{c} = [c_0, c_1, c_2, c_3]^T$ is:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} c_0 & c_1 & c_2 & c_3 \\ c_3 & c_0 & c_1 & c_2 \\ c_2 & c_3 & c_0 & c_1 \\ c_1 & c_2 & c_3 & c_0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 1.2 What is a Block Matrix?

A **Block Matrix** (also called a *partitioned matrix*) is a matrix that is divided into smaller rectangular sub-matrices called *blocks*. The standard arithmetic operations (addition, multiplication, transpose, inverse) are defined on these blocks just as they are on scalar elements, provided that the block-partition dimensions are conformable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Digital Image Processing, the *Block Circulant Matrix with Circulant Blocks* (BCCB) is the matrix representation of **2-D linear convolution** of an image with a finite impulse response (FIR) filter under periodic (circular) boundary conditions.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Circulant Matrix Analogy — "The Musical Round":**
Imagine a *round* song (like *Row, Row, Row Your Boat*). When one group finishes the first phrase, the next group starts it. Every row of a circulant matrix is the *same melody* begun exactly one beat later. The melody (generating vector) is preserved, but the entry point shifts. Because the structure is so symmetric, multiplying by a circulant matrix is mathematically equivalent to performing a **circular convolution** in the spatial domain — a fact we will exploit throughout image filtering.

**Block Matrix Analogy — "The Mosaic Tile Wall":**
A wall of mosaic tiles is made of many small tiles arranged into bigger rectangles (blocks). Each tile-block is a *mini-matrix* in itself. A *Block Circulant* matrix is then a wall where each tile-block is also a circulant pattern — giving a doubly periodic structure. This is precisely what we need to handle the *2-D periodicity* inherent to the DFT of an image.

### 1.4 Why These Matrices Matter in DIP

- **Convolution becomes multiplication in the DFT domain** — a fact that makes filtering $O(N^2 \log N)$ instead of $O(N^4)$.
- **Image restoration (Wiener filtering, deblurring)** requires solving $\mathbf{y} = H\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{n}$ where $H$ is a **block circulant** degradation matrix.
- **DCT-based image compression (JPEG)** relies on circulant-like structure of the basis kernels.
- The matrix $H$ being block circulant guarantees that it can be diagonalized by a 2-D DFT, transforming the deconvolution problem into an element-wise division in the frequency domain.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Circulant Matrix

Let the first row (the *generating vector*) of an $N \times N$ circulant matrix $C$ be:

$$
\mathbf{c} = [c_0, c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_{N-1}]
$$

Then row $i$ (0-indexed) is:

$$
C_i = [c_{-i \bmod N},\ c_{1-i \bmod N},\ \ldots,\ c_{N-1-i \bmod N}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **$i$-th row** of $C$ is the generating vector $\mathbf{c}$ rotated right by $i$ positions. Equivalently, $C_{i,j} = c_{(j-i) \bmod N}$.

### 2.2 The Eigenstructure Theorem of Circulant Matrices

**The Most Important Result in This Topic (board-exam favourite):**

> Every circulant matrix $C = \text{circ}(c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_{N-1})$ is **diagonalized** by the unitary DFT matrix $F$. That is:
>
> $$ C = F \Lambda F^H $$
>
> where $F^H$ is the conjugate transpose of $F$, and $\Lambda$ is a diagonal matrix whose entries are the **DFT of the first row** of $C$.

The DFT matrix $F$ has entries:

$$
F_{k,n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k,n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1
$$

The **eigenvalues** of $C$ are the DFT coefficients of the generating vector:

$$
\lambda_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} c_n \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequence:** All circulant matrices of the same size share the *same eigenvectors* — namely, the columns of $F$ (the DFT basis). This is why circulant matrices commute: $C_1 C_2 = C_2 C_1$.

### 2.3 Block Circulant Matrix with Circulant Blocks (BCCB)

A 2-D image $X$ of size $M \times N$ can be vectorized by stacking columns (lexicographic ordering) to form a vector $\mathbf{x}$ of length $MN$. The 2-D linear convolution of $X$ with a 2-D FIR filter $H$ (size $P \times Q$) under **periodic boundary conditions** is expressed as:

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C} \, \mathbf{x}
$$

where $\mathbf{C}$ is an $MN \times MN$ matrix that has a **block circulant structure with circulant blocks** (BCCB). Each block of $\mathbf{C}$ is itself a circulant matrix of size $N \times N$, and the blocks themselves are arranged in a circulant pattern over $M$ blocks.

### 2.4 BCCB Diagonalization by 2-D DFT

The BCCB matrix is diagonalized by the **2-D DFT** matrix, which is the Kronecker product of the row DFT and the column DFT:

$$
\mathbf{C} = (F_M \otimes F_N) \, \Lambda \, (F_M \otimes F_N)^H
$$

Here $\otimes$ is the **Kronecker product**, and $\Lambda$ is a diagonal matrix containing the 2-D DFT of the filter kernel $H$. This reduces the convolution to an element-wise Hadamard (entry-wise) product in the frequency domain.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Key Condition |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Circulant matrix | $C = \text{circ}(c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_{N-1})$; $C_{i,j} = c_{(j-i)\bmod N}$ | Square matrix, $N \times N$ |
| 2 | Diagonalization | $C = F \Lambda F^H$ | $F$ is the unitary DFT matrix |
| 3 | Eigenvalues | $\lambda_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} c_n \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | DFT of generating vector |
| 4 | Convolution link | $C \mathbf{x} \iff \mathbf{c} \circledast \mathbf{x}$ (circular convolution) | $\circledast$ = circular conv |
| 5 | Product formula | $\text{circ}(a) \cdot \text{circ}(b) = \text{circ}(a \circledast b)$ | Closure under product |
| 6 | Block matrix product | $(AB)_{ij} = \sum_k A_{ik} B_{kj}$ | Block dimensions conformable |
| 7 | Block transpose | $(B^T)_{ij} = (B_{ji})^T$ | Swap and transpose each block |
| 8 | BCCB form | $\mathbf{C} = (F_M \otimes F_N) \Lambda (F_M \otimes F_N)^H$ | 2-D periodic convolution |
| 9 | Kronecker product | $(A \otimes B)_{(i_1,i_2),(j_1,j_2)} = A_{i_1,j_1} B_{i_2,j_2}$ | Used in vectorization |
| 10 | Image filtering | $Y = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{H \cdot X\}$ | Element-wise product in DFT |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Whenever you see a question asking *"How is image filtering implemented efficiently?"* — the answer always traces back to the *BCCB diagonalization theorem* above (rows 8 and 10).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT Restoration):** Inverse filtering of a blurred medical image requires solving $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$. Because $\mathbf{C}$ is BCCB, the inverse is trivially $1/\lambda$ in the frequency domain.
- **Satellite Image Deblurring:** Wiener filtering uses $\hat{X}(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{|H(u,v)|^2 + S_\eta(u,v)/S_x(x,v)} Y(u,v)$ — derived from the BCCB structure of the blur operator.
- **Computational Photography:** Rolling-shutter correction and deconvolution both exploit circulant structure.
- **JPEG Compression:** The $8 \times 8$ DCT basis is essentially the real part of the DFT — circulant-matrix theory underpins block transform coding.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Diagonalization of a $3 \times 3$ Circulant Matrix

**Goal:** Show that $C = \text{circ}(c_0, c_1, c_2)$ satisfies $C = F \Lambda F^H$ for some unitary $F$.

### Step 1 — Write the circulant matrix explicitly

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} c_0 & c_1 & c_2 \\ c_2 & c_0 & c_1 \\ c_1 & c_2 & c_0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The first row is $[c_0, c_1, c_2]$. The second row is the first row cyclically shifted right by one: the last element $c_2$ moves to the front, giving $[c_2, c_0, c_1]$. The third row is a further cyclic shift: $[c_1, c_2, c_0]$.

### Step 2 — Construct the $3 \times 3$ unitary DFT matrix $F$

For $N = 3$, the primitive root of unity is $\omega = e^{-j 2\pi / 3} = \cos(120^\circ) - j \sin(120^\circ) = -\tfrac{1}{2} - j\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

The unitary DFT matrix is:

$$
F = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & \omega & \omega^2 \\ 1 & \omega^2 & \omega^4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Since $\omega^3 = 1$, we have $\omega^4 = \omega$. Thus:

$$
F = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & \omega & \omega^2 \\ 1 & \omega^2 & \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $F \mathbf{c}$, which gives the eigenvalues

The product $F \mathbf{c}$ has its $k$-th entry equal to $\lambda_k / \sqrt{3}$, where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_0 &= c_0 + c_1 + c_2 \\
\lambda_1 &= c_0 + c_1 \omega + c_2 \omega^2 \\
\lambda_2 &= c_0 + c_1 \omega^2 + c_2 \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each $\lambda_k$ is the inner product of the $k$-th row of $F$ (scaled by $\sqrt{3}$) with the generating vector $\mathbf{c}$. This is exactly the $N$-point DFT formula.

### Step 4 — Build $\Lambda$

$$
\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_0, \lambda_1, \lambda_2)
$$

### Step 5 — Verify $C = F \Lambda F^H$

By the eigen-decomposition theorem, since each column of $F$ is an eigenvector of $C$ and the corresponding eigenvalue is $\lambda_k$, the matrix product $F \Lambda F^H$ reconstructs $C$. We verify the (0,0) entry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(C)_{0,0} &= (F \Lambda F^H)_{0,0} \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{2} \lambda_k \, F_{0,k} \, \overline{F_{0,k}} \\
&= \frac{1}{3}\sum_{k=0}^{2} \lambda_k \\
&= \frac{1}{3}(\lambda_0 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now substitute:

$$
\lambda_0 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 3 c_0 + c_1(1 + \omega + \omega^2) + c_2(1 + \omega^2 + \omega)
$$

Using the identity $1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0$ (sum of cube roots of unity):

$$
\lambda_0 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 3 c_0
$$

Therefore $(C)_{0,0} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 3 c_0 = c_0$. ✓

A similar calculation verifies the off-diagonal entries. The complete verification confirms:

$$
\boxed{\,C = F \Lambda F^H\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Block Circulant Form of 2-D Convolution

**Setup:** Let $X$ be an $M \times N$ image and $H$ be a $P \times Q$ filter kernel, both extended periodically (period $M$ in rows, $N$ in columns). Let $h$ be the impulse response of $H$.

### Step 1 — Vectorize the image

Using lexicographic (column-stacking) ordering:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \text{vec}(X) \in \mathbb{C}^{MN}
$$

### Step 2 — Build the BCCB matrix

For a 1-D circular convolution $y = h \circledast x$, the matrix representation is circulant. For 2-D, the natural extension is a **block matrix where each block is circulant** and the blocks themselves are arranged in a circulant pattern. The $k$-th block column is obtained by cyclically shifting the 0-th block column downward.

The full BCCB operator is:

$$
\mathbf{C} = \text{circ}(\mathbf{C}_0, \mathbf{C}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{C}_{M-1})
$$

where each block $\mathbf{C}_i$ is itself an $N \times N$ circulant matrix built from the $i$-th row of the zero-padded filter $H$.

### Step 3 — Apply 2-D DFT diagonalization

Let $F_M$ be the $M$-point unitary DFT matrix and $F_N$ the $N$-point one. Then:

$$
\mathbf{C} = (F_M \otimes F_N) \, \Lambda \, (F_M \otimes F_N)^H
$$

where $\Lambda$ is diagonal with entries $\Lambda_{(k,\ell)} = H(k,\ell)$ (the 2-D DFT of the filter kernel). The proof uses the fact that the 2-D DFT basis factors as a Kronecker product of the two 1-D DFT bases, and the BCCB structure of $\mathbf{C}$ matches this factorization.

### Step 4 — Practical Filtering Equation

Multiplying $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C} \mathbf{x}$ by $(F_M \otimes F_N)$ on both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(F_M \otimes F_N) \mathbf{y} &= \Lambda (F_M \otimes F_N) \mathbf{x} \\
\mathcal{F}_{2D}\{Y\} &= \mathcal{F}_{2D}\{H\} \cdot \mathcal{F}_{2D}\{X\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the celebrated **Convolution Theorem in 2-D**, which states that circular convolution in the spatial domain becomes element-wise multiplication in the frequency domain.

### Step 5 — Restoration via inverse filtering

If $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{n}$ (blurred + noise), the Wiener filter estimate is:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{C}^{-1} \mathbf{y}, \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{C}^{-1} = (F_M \otimes F_N) \Lambda^{-1} (F_M \otimes F_N)^H
$$

In the frequency domain, this becomes an element-wise operation:

$$
\hat{X}(u,v) = \frac{Y(u,v)}{H(u,v)}
$$

which is implementable in $O(MN \log(MN))$ time using the 2-D FFT.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Circulant-Matrix-Based Image Filtering

The block diagram below illustrates the *functional architecture* of how a circulant / block-circulant matrix underpins the entire DIP filtering pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image X of size M by N] --> B[Lexicographic Vectorization]
    B --> C[Vector x of length MN]
    C --> D[Form BCCB Matrix C from Filter Kernel H]
    D --> E[Diagonalize C using 2D DFT Matrix F_M tensor F_N]
    E --> F[Lambda Matrix with H u v on Diagonal]
    F --> G[Apply Convolution Theorem: Y equals F inverse times Lambda times F times X]
    G --> H[Output Filtered Image Y]
    H --> I{Application Layer}
    I --> J[Smoothing LPF]
    I --> K[Sharpening HPF]
    I --> L[Deblurring Inverse Filter]
    I --> M[Wiener Restoration]
    I --> N[Edge Detection Laplacian]
```

## 4.2 Structural Comparison: Circulant vs. Block Circulant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CIRC["CIRCULANT MATRIX - 1D Case"]
        C1[Row 0: c0 c1 c2 c3] --> C2[Row 1: c3 c0 c1 c2]
        C2 --> C3[Row 2: c2 c3 c0 c1]
        C3 --> C4[Row 3: c1 c2 c3 c0]
    end
    subgraph BLOCK["BLOCK CIRCULANT - 2D Case"]
        B1[Block Row 0: B0 B1 B2] --> B2[Block Row 1: B2 B0 B1]
        B2 --> B3[Block Row 2: B1 B2 B0]
        B1 --> B1note[Each Bi is itself a circulant matrix]
        B2 --> B2note[Each Bi is itself a circulant matrix]
        B3 --> B3note[Each Bi is itself a circulant matrix]
    end
    CIRC -. "Extension to 2D using Kronecker Product" .-> BLOCK
```

## 4.3 Algorithm-Level Architecture of BCCB Diagonalization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Image X and Filter H] --> S1[Zero pad H to size M by N]
    S1 --> S2[Compute 2D FFT of H to get H u v]
    S2 --> S3[Compute 2D FFT of X to get X u v]
    S3 --> S4[Element-wise multiply: Y u v equals H u v times X u v]
    S4 --> S5[Compute inverse 2D FFT to get Y]
    S5 --> S6[Output: Filtered Image Y]
    S6 --> S7[End]
    S4 -. uses circulant property .-> S4note[Diagonal form of BCCB enables Hadamard product]
    S4note -. avoids .-> S4avoid[Full matrix multiplication which is O to the 4th power]
    S4avoid -. replaced by .-> S4replaced[FFT based multiplication O to the power N log N]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**
*Define a circulant matrix. Show the structure of a $3 \times 3$ circulant matrix generated by the vector $\mathbf{c} = [1, 2, 3]^T$.*

**Model Answer:**

A **circulant matrix** is a square matrix in which each row is obtained by cyclically shifting the previous row one position to the right. Formally, for an $N \times N$ matrix $C$ with first row $[c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_{N-1}]$, the element satisfies $C_{i,j} = c_{(j-i) \bmod N}$.

For $\mathbf{c} = [1, 2, 3]^T$:

$$
C = \text{circ}(1, 2, 3) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Mark Distribution:** Definition — 1 mark, Matrix display — 1 mark, Explanation of cyclic shift — 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**
*State the diagonalization theorem for circulant matrices and mention its significance in image filtering.*

**Model Answer:**

**Theorem:** Every $N \times N$ circulant matrix $C$ can be diagonalized as $C = F \Lambda F^H$, where $F$ is the $N$-point unitary DFT matrix and $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_0, \lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_{N-1})$ contains the DFT of the generating vector as its entries.

**Significance in Image Filtering:**
- The product $C \mathbf{x}$ (which represents a circular convolution) can be computed as $F \Lambda F^H \mathbf{x} = F \cdot \Lambda \cdot (F^H \mathbf{x})$.
- This corresponds to: (i) take the DFT of $\mathbf{x}$, (ii) multiply each frequency component by the corresponding eigenvalue $\lambda_k$, (iii) take the inverse DFT.
- The result is **image filtering in the frequency domain**, which is $O(N^2 \log N)$ instead of $O(N^4)$ direct matrix multiplication.

**Mark Distribution:** Theorem statement — 1 mark, Formula — 1 mark, Significance — 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q.A (a)** (7 Marks) *Define a circulant matrix. For the generating vector $\mathbf{c} = [c_0, c_1, c_2, c_3]^T$, write the $4 \times 4$ circulant matrix and find its eigenvalues using the DFT approach.*

**Q.A (b)** (7 Marks) *Explain the concept of a Block Circulant Matrix with Circulant Blocks (BCCB) and show how it is diagonalized by the 2-D DFT. State the convolution theorem that follows from this diagonalization.*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
A circulant matrix is a square matrix in which each row is a cyclic right-shift of the previous row. Element-wise: $C_{i,j} = c_{(j-i) \bmod N}$.

**Step 2 — Construct the $4 \times 4$ circulant matrix (2 marks):**

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} c_0 & c_1 & c_2 & c_3 \\ c_3 & c_0 & c_1 & c_2 \\ c_2 & c_3 & c_0 & c_1 \\ c_1 & c_2 & c_3 & c_0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute eigenvalues using DFT (3 marks):**
Using the diagonalization theorem, eigenvalues are $\lambda_k = \text{DFT}_k(\mathbf{c})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_0 &= c_0 + c_1 + c_2 + c_3 \\
\lambda_1 &= c_0 + c_1 \, e^{-j\pi/2} + c_2 \, e^{-j\pi} + c_3 \, e^{-j3\pi/2} \\
&= c_0 - j c_1 - c_2 + j c_3 \\
\lambda_2 &= c_0 + c_1 \, e^{-j\pi} + c_2 \, e^{-j2\pi} + c_3 \, e^{-j3\pi} \\
&= c_0 - c_1 + c_2 - c_3 \\
\lambda_3 &= c_0 + c_1 \, e^{-j3\pi/2} + c_2 \, e^{-j3\pi} + c_3 \, e^{-j9\pi/2} \\
&= c_0 + j c_1 - c_2 - j c_3
\end{aligned}
$$

(Explanations: Apply the DFT formula $\lambda_k = \sum_{n=0}^{3} c_n e^{-j 2\pi k n / 4}$ term by term, simplifying the complex exponentials using Euler's formula.)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Step 1 — Define BCCB (2 marks):**
A Block Circulant Matrix with Circulant Blocks (BCCB) is an $MN \times MN$ block matrix where:
- The matrix is partitioned into $M \times M$ blocks, each of size $N \times N$.
- The blocks themselves are arranged in a circulant pattern.
- Each individual block is also a circulant matrix.

This is the matrix representation of **2-D circular convolution** of an image with an FIR filter.

**Step 2 — Diagonalization by 2-D DFT (3 marks):**
Let $F_M$ and $F_N$ be the $M$-point and $N$-point unitary DFT matrices. Then:

$$
\mathbf{C} = (F_M \otimes F_N) \, \Lambda \, (F_M \otimes F_N)^H
$$

where $\Lambda$ is a diagonal matrix whose entries are the 2-D DFT coefficients $H(u,v)$ of the filter kernel.

**Step 3 — Convolution theorem (2 marks):**
The 2-D circular convolution of an image $X$ with filter $H$ can be implemented as:

$$
Y(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot X(u,v)
$$

followed by the inverse 2-D FFT. Equivalently: $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C} \mathbf{x}$ becomes an element-wise Hadamard product in the frequency domain, which is the cornerstone of efficient image filtering.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q.B (a)** (7 Marks) *Derive the diagonalization formula $C = F \Lambda F^H$ for a $3 \times 3$ circulant matrix generated by $\mathbf{c} = [c_0, c_1, c_2]^T$. Show each step clearly.*

**Q.B (b)** (7 Marks) *Explain with suitable diagram how a block circulant matrix arises in 2-D image filtering. Discuss the role of the Kronecker product in its diagonalization.*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Step 1 — Construct the $3 \times 3$ circulant matrix (1 mark):**

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} c_0 & c_1 & c_2 \\ c_2 & c_0 & c_1 \\ c_1 & c_2 & c_0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Construct the unitary DFT matrix (2 marks):**
With $\omega = e^{-j 2\pi/3}$ and $\omega^3 = 1$:

$$
F = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & \omega & \omega^2 \\ 1 & \omega^2 & \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute eigenvalues (2 marks):**
Apply the DFT formula $\lambda_k = \sum_{n=0}^{2} c_n \omega^{kn}$:

$$
\lambda_0 = c_0 + c_1 + c_2
$$
$$
\lambda_1 = c_0 + c_1 \omega + c_2 \omega^2
$$
$$
\lambda_2 = c_0 + c_1 \omega^2 + c_2 \omega
$$

**Step 4 — Verify $C = F \Lambda F^H$ (2 marks):**
Compute the (0,0) entry:

$$
(F\Lambda F^H)_{0,0} = \frac{1}{3}(\lambda_0 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2) = \frac{1}{3}(3 c_0) = c_0 \;\checkmark
$$

since $1 + \omega + \omega^2 = 0$. A similar verification holds for all other entries. Hence:

$$
\boxed{C = F \Lambda F^H}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Step 1 — How BCCB arises (2 marks):**
When an image $X$ (size $M \times N$) is convolved with a 2-D FIR filter $H$ under *periodic boundary conditions* (zero-padded so that $H$ has the same size as $X$), the linear operation $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}$ (where $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}$ are vectorized forms of $X$ and the filtered image) has a matrix $\mathbf{C}$ that is **block circulant with circulant blocks**. This is because the convolution index $(m - k) \bmod M$ in the row direction and $(n - \ell) \bmod N$ in the column direction gives a doubly cyclic shift structure.

**Step 2 — Block structure (2 marks):**
The matrix is organized as $M$ block rows, each containing $M$ blocks of size $N \times N$. Within each block, the rows are cyclic shifts of one another (circulant within block). Across the blocks, the block rows are cyclic shifts of one another (circulant across blocks).

**Step 3 — Role of Kronecker product (2 marks):**
The 2-D DFT basis vectors factor into the Kronecker product of 1-D row-DFT and column-DFT vectors. Hence the diagonalizing matrix for the BCCB operator is $F_M \otimes F_N$, and:

$$
\mathbf{C} = (F_M \otimes F_N) \Lambda (F_M \otimes F_N)^H
$$

The Kronecker product structure allows the 2-D problem to be decomposed into two sequential 1-D DFTs, which is exactly what the *row-column FFT algorithm* does in practice.

**Step 4 — Concluding remark (1 mark):**
This Kronecker-based diagonalization is the *theoretical foundation* for why 2-D image filtering can be performed efficiently in the frequency domain with computational cost $O(MN \log(MN))$ instead of $O(M^2 N^2)$ in the spatial domain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cyclic (modular) indexing:** Students often write $C_{i,j} = c_{j-i}$ without the "$\bmod N$". This costs **0.5–1 mark** per occurrence in the KTU valuation key.
> 2. **Confusing $F$ with the *unnormalized* DFT matrix:** The unitary form $F$ has a $1/\sqrt{N}$ factor, while the signal-processing form $W$ does not. Board answers should clarify which form is used.
> 3. **Missing the conjugate transpose:** Many students write $C = F \Lambda F^{-1}$, which is correct only when $F$ is real. For complex DFT, it must be $F^H$ (conjugate transpose). Losing 1 mark is common.
> 4. **Drawing the BCCB diagram incorrectly:** Block rows and block columns must both be cyclic shifts. A common mistake is making the block pattern non-cyclic, which contradicts the definition.
> 5. **Skipping the Kronecker product in part (b):** Examiners specifically look for the explicit use of $\otimes$ when explaining 2-D diagonalization. Forgetting it loses up to **2 marks**.
> 6. **Not mentioning the convolution theorem explicitly:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the link between matrix theory and the *practical filtering equation*. Always end your answer with $Y(u,v) = H(u,v) X(u,v)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Board Exam:**

- ✅ A **circulant matrix** is fully determined by its first row; every other row is a cyclic right-shift.
- ✅ Element rule: $C_{i,j} = c_{(j-i) \bmod N}$.
- ✅ **Diagonalization theorem:** $C = F \Lambda F^H$, with $F$ = unitary DFT matrix and $\Lambda$ = diag(DFT of $\mathbf{c}$).
- ✅ **Eigenvalues of $C$** are the DFT coefficients of the generating vector.
- ✅ All circulant matrices of the same size share the **same eigenvectors** (columns of $F$).
- ✅ Circulant matrices **commute**: $C_1 C_2 = C_2 C_1$.
- ✅ **Product closure:** $\text{circ}(a) \cdot \text{circ}(b) = \text{circ}(a \circledast b)$, where $\circledast$ is circular convolution.
- ✅ A **block matrix** is a matrix partitioned into smaller sub-matrices (blocks); block arithmetic requires conformable dimensions.
- ✅ A **Block Circulant Matrix with Circulant Blocks (BCCB)** represents 2-D circular convolution.
- ✅ **BCCB diagonalization:** $\mathbf{C} = (F_M \otimes F_N) \Lambda (F_M \otimes F_N)^H$, where $\Lambda$ contains the 2-D DFT of the filter.
- ✅ **2-D Convolution Theorem:** $Y(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot X(u,v)$ — element-wise product in the frequency domain.
- ✅ **Kronecker product** $\otimes$ factorizes the 2-D DFT into two 1-D DFTs (row then column).
- ✅ **Computational benefit:** Filtering via FFT is $O(MN \log MN)$; direct spatial convolution is $O(M^2 N^2)$.
- ✅ **Image restoration** (Wiener, inverse, constrained deconvolution) all rely on the BCCB structure of the degradation matrix.
- ✅ **Boundary condition:** BCCB theory applies to *periodic* (wrap-around) convolution; for *zero-padding*, the matrix is **block Toeplitz with Toeplitz blocks** (BTTB).
- ✅ **Key board-exam keywords** to use: *circulant*, *generating vector*, *cyclic shift*, *DFT diagonalization*, *Kronecker product*, *block circulant*, *BCCB*, *convolution theorem*, *Hadamard product*.
- ✅ **Real-world link:** JPEG DCT, MRI deblurring, Wiener filtering, satellite image restoration all use this theory.

---

topics.title: 2D Image Transforms - DFT and Its Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** 2D Image Transforms - DFT and Its Properties # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **2D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** is a mathematical transform that converts a finite discrete image $f(x, y)$ of size $M \times N$ from the **spatial domain** into its corresponding representation $F(u, v)$ in the **frequency domain**. It is the discrete analogue of the continuous Fourier Transform and is the cornerstone tool for frequency-domain image analysis in KTU 2024 Scheme DIP coursework. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 2D DFT is defined over a finite, discrete image grid. The transform is **separable** and **symmetric**, allowing it to be computed using two successive 1D DFT operations along the rows and columns. ### Forward 2D DFT (Analysis Equation) $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \left( \frac{u x}{M} + \frac{v y}{N} \right)}$$ ### Inverse 2D DFT (Synthesis Equation) $$f(x, y) = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{u=0}^{M-1} \sum_{v=0}^{N-1} F(u, v) \, e^{+j 2\pi \left( \frac{u x}{M} + \frac{v y}{N} \right)}$$ where the variables are defined as: - $f(x, y)$ = digital image with spatial coordinates $x \in [0, M-1]$, $y \in [0, N-1]$ - $F(u, v)$ = DFT coefficients (complex-valued) with frequency coordinates $u \in [0, M-1]$, $v \in [0, N-1]$ - $j = \sqrt{-1}$ (imaginary unit) - $M, N$ = number of rows and columns of the image (both must be powers of **2** for the Fast Fourier Transform) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Music Decomposer** > > Imagine listening to a symphony orchestra playing a complex musical piece. Your ear instantly hears **multiple instruments playing different notes simultaneously** — a violin, a flute, a drum. But mathematically, that single complex sound wave reaching your ear is just the **sum of simple sine waves** at different frequencies. > > The **2D DFT does the same thing for an image**: > - The **image** $f(x, y)$ is like the mixed orchestra sound. > - The **DFT** $F(u, v)$ tells you "how much of each spatial frequency" (i.e., how much of each "note") is present in the image at horizontal frequency $u$ and vertical frequency $v$. > - **Low frequencies** $(u, v \approx 0)$ = smooth, slowly varying regions (sky, walls). > - **High frequencies** $(u, v \approx M/2, N/2)$ = edges, noise, fine texture, sharp details. > > Once we know $F(u, v)$, we can **boost, suppress, or filter** specific frequencies — the foundation of image filtering, compression (JPEG uses a related transform), and enhancement. --- ## 1.3 Spatial Domain vs Frequency Domain > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Distinction (Mandatory for KTU):** > > | Domain | Variable | Unit | Meaning | > | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | > | **Spatial** | $(x, y)$ | pixels | Position of intensity values | > | **Frequency** | $(u, v)$ | **cycles/image** | Rate of intensity change | > > The DC component $F(0, 0)$ equals $MN$ times the **average pixel intensity** of the image. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown The 2D DFT is best understood as a **two-stage separable process**: **Step 1 — Row-wise 1D DFT:** Apply 1D DFT along each row of the image. **Step 2 — Column-wise 1D DFT:** Apply 1D DFT along each column of the intermediate result. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Separability Matters in KTU Examinations:** > Because the 2D DFT is separable, we never need to write a 2D summation in code or in proofs. We always reduce it to two 1D operations. This reduces computational complexity from $O(M^2 N^2)$ to $O(MN \log_2 M \log_2 N)$ when using the FFT. ### 2.1.1 Proof of Separability (Outline) The exponential kernel of the 2D DFT can be factored: $$e^{-j 2\pi \left( \frac{u x}{M} + \frac{v y}{N} \right)} = e^{-j 2\pi \frac{u x}{M}} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi \frac{v y}{N}}$$ Substituting into the forward equation, we obtain: $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} e^{-j 2\pi \frac{u x}{M}} \left[ \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \frac{v y}{N}} \right]$$ This means: **first take the 1D DFT of each row**, then take the 1D DFT of each column of that result. --- ## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Constraint:** Vertical pipe `|` symbols are written as `\vert` to keep markdown tables intact. Memorize this entire table — it covers roughly **70% of Module 2 marks**. | # | Property / Concept | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Utility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Forward 2D DFT** | $F(u, v) = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} f(x, y) \, e^{-j2\pi(ux/M + vy/N)}$ | Convert image → frequency domain | | 2 | **Inverse 2D DFT** | $f(x, y) = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{u}\sum_{v} F(u, v) \, e^{+j2\pi(ux/M + vy/N)}$ | Reconstruct image from spectrum | | 3 | **DC Component** | $F(0, 0) = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} f(x, y)$ | Sum of all pixel intensities | | 4 | **Average Intensity** | $\bar{f}(x,y) = \frac{1}{MN} F(0,0)$ | Mean brightness of image | | 5 | **Periodicity** | $F(u, v) = F(u + M, v) = F(u, v + N)$ | Spectrum tiles infinitely | | 6 | **Conjugate Symmetry** | $F(u, v) = F^{*}(-u, -v)$ | Spectrum is Hermitian-symmetric | | 7 | **Separability** | $F(u, v) = F_{\text{rows}}(u, y) \rightarrow F_{\text{cols}}(u, v)$ | Reduces 2D to 2 × 1D transforms | | 8 | **Translation** | $f(x, y) \, e^{j2\pi(u_0 x/M + v_0 y/N)} \leftrightarrow F(u - u_0, v - v_0)$ | Shifts spectrum | | 9 | **Rotation** | $f(r, \theta, \phi) \leftrightarrow F(\rho, \phi + \theta, \rho)$ | Rotating image rotates spectrum by same angle | | 10 | **Convolution Theorem** | $f(x, y) * h(x, y) \leftrightarrow F(u, v) \cdot H(u, v)$ | Spatial convolution = frequency multiplication | | 11 | **Correlation Theorem** | $f(x, y) \circ g(x, y) \leftrightarrow F^{*}(u, v) \cdot G(u, v)$ | Used in template matching | | 12 | **Parseval's Energy Theorem** | $\sum_{x}\sum_{y} \vert f(x, y) \vert^{2} = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{u}\sum_{v}\vert F(u, v) \vert^{2}$ | Energy conservation between domains | | 13 | **Scaling Property** | $f(ax, by) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{\vert ab \vert} F(u/a, v/b)$ | Compress image → expands spectrum | | 14 | **Differentiation** | $\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right) \leftrightarrow (j 2\pi u/M, \, j 2\pi v/N) F(u,v)$ | Edge detection in frequency domain | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where 2D DFT is used in industry (high-yield KTU content):** > > 1. **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT Reconstruction):** The raw data from MRI scanners is in the frequency domain (called *k-space*). The DFT reconstructs the diagnostic image. > 2. **JPEG / MPEG Compression:** Block-based DCT (a close cousin of the DFT) compresses images by discarding high-frequency components. > 3. **Astronomy:** Removing atmospheric noise (high-frequency) and isolating star signals (low-frequency). > 4. **Texture Analysis & Pattern Recognition:** Identifying repetitive textures by inspecting peak frequencies. > 5. **Watermarking & Steganography:** Embedding signals in the mid-frequency band where they survive compression but remain invisible. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: 2D DFT from First Principles (Forward Transform) Starting from the continuous 2D Fourier Transform, the discrete version is obtained by **sampling** the spatial domain at integer points and the frequency domain at equally spaced frequencies. ### Step 1: Continuous 2D Fourier Transform The continuous 2D Fourier Transform of a continuous image $f_c(x, y)$ is defined as: $$F_c(u, v) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f_c(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi (u x + v y)} \, dx \, dy$$ ### Step 2: Discretize the Spatial Domain Replace the continuous integral with a discrete double summation over an $M \times N$ grid: $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi (u x \Delta_x + v y \Delta_y)}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute the Sampling Intervals Setting $\Delta_x = \Delta_y = 1$ (unit pixel spacing) yields: $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \left( \frac{u x}{M} + \frac{v y}{N} \right)}$$ ### Step 4: Separate the Exponentials Apply the algebraic identity $e^{a + b} = e^{a} \cdot e^{b}$: $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi u x / M} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi v y / N}$$ ### Step 5: Rearrange as Two 1D DFTs $$\begin{aligned} F(u, v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \left[ e^{-j 2\pi u x / M} \left( \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi v y / N} \right) \right] \\ &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} F_{\text{row}}(x, v) \, e^{-j 2\pi u x / M} \end{aligned}$$ The inner summation $F_{\text{row}}(x, v)$ is the **1D DFT of the $x$-th row**, and the outer summation is the **1D DFT of the $v$-th column** of the intermediate result. > [!NOTE] > **Computational Interpretation:** We have shown that the 2D DFT is mathematically equivalent to applying a 1D DFT to every row, then applying a 1D DFT to every column of that result. This is what the FFT algorithm exploits. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Periodicity Property **Statement:** $F(u, v) = F(u + M, v)$ for all integers $u, v$. ### Step 1: Substitute $u + M$ into the DFT equation $$F(u + M, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \frac{(u+M) x}{M}} \, e^{-j 2\pi \frac{v y}{N}}$$ ### Step 2: Expand the exponential $$e^{-j 2\pi \frac{(u+M) x}{M}} = e^{-j 2\pi \frac{u x}{M}} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi \frac{M x}{M}} = e^{-j 2\pi \frac{u x}{M}} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi x}$$ ### Step 3: Apply Euler's identity Since $x$ is an integer, $e^{-j 2\pi x} = \cos(2\pi x) - j \sin(2\pi x) = 1$. ### Step 4: Conclude $$\begin{aligned} F(u + M, v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \frac{u x}{M}} \cdot (1) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi \frac{v y}{N}} \\ &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \left( \frac{u x}{M} + \frac{v y}{N} \right)} \\ &= F(u, v) \end{aligned}$$ Hence **proved**. The same argument shows $F(u, v) = F(u, v + N)$, so the spectrum is periodic with periods $M$ and $N$ along the two axes. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Conjugate Symmetry Property **Statement:** $F(u, v) = F^{*}(-u, -v)$ for real-valued images $f(x, y)$. ### Step 1: Take the complex conjugate of $F(-u, -v)$ $$F^{*}(-u, -v) = \left[ \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \left( \frac{-u x}{M} + \frac{-v y}{N} \right)} \right]^{*}$$ ### Step 2: Apply the property $(A \cdot B)^{*} = A^{*} \cdot B^{*}$ For real $f(x, y)$, we have $f(x, y)^{*} = f(x, y)$. Therefore: $$F^{*}(-u, -v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{+j 2\pi \left( \frac{u x}{M} + \frac{v y}{N} \right)}$$ ### Step 3: Compare with $F(u, v)$ Recall $F(u, v)$ contains $e^{-j 2\pi(...)}$. Since $(u, v)$ are dummy variables, replacing them with $(-u, -v)$ and conjugating produces the original expression back. Hence: $$F(u, v) = F^{*}(-u, -v)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Consequence (often asked in KTU exams):** For real-valued images, the magnitude spectrum $\vert F(u, v) \vert$ is **symmetric about the origin**. This means only **half the spectrum** carries unique information, allowing storage savings of nearly **50%**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: 2D Convolution Theorem **Statement:** If $f(x, y)$ and $h(x, y)$ have DFTs $F(u, v)$ and $H(u, v)$, then: $$f(x, y) * h(x, y) \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; F(u, v) \cdot H(u, v)$$ ### Step 1: Write the spatial convolution $$g(x, y) = f(x, y) * h(x, y) = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m, n) \, h(x - m, y - n)$$ ### Step 2: Compute the DFT of $g(x, y)$ $$G(u, v) = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} \left[ \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m, n) \, h(x - m, y - n) \right] e^{-j 2\pi (ux/M + vy/N)}$$ ### Step 3: Swap the order of summation $$G(u, v) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m, n) \left[ \sum_{x}\sum_{y} h(x - m, y - n) \, e^{-j 2\pi (ux/M + vy/N)} \right]$$ ### Step 4: Apply the shift theorem to the inner sum Using $h(x - m, y - n) \leftrightarrow H(u, v) \, e^{-j 2\pi (um/M + vn/N)}$: $$G(u, v) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m, n) \, H(u, v) \, e^{-j 2\pi (um/M + vn/N)}$$ ### Step 5: Factor out $H(u, v)$ $$G(u, v) = H(u, v) \cdot \underbrace{\left[ \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m, n) \, e^{-j 2\pi (um/M + vn/N)} \right]}_{= F(u, v)}$$ ### Step 6: Conclusion $$\boxed{G(u, v) = F(u, v) \cdot H(u, v)}$$ The convolution theorem is the **single most important property** for KTU examinations because it is the foundation of frequency-domain filtering. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Frequency Domain Coordinate System (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Input Image f x y

That is: > > $$F(u, v) = F(u + M, v) = F(u, v + N)$$ **Proof (2 marks):** $$F(u + M, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi \frac{(u+M)x}{M}} \, e^{-j 2\pi \frac{vy}{N}}$$ $$= \sum_{x}\sum_{y} f(x,y) \, e^{-j 2\pi ux/M} \cdot \underbrace{e^{-j 2\pi x}}_{=1} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi vy/N} = F(u, v)$$ since $x$ is an integer, $e^{-j 2\pi x} = 1$. Similarly for $F(u, v + N) = F(u, v)$. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Explain the separability property of the 2D DFT. Why is it important computationally?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** A 2D function $f(x, y)$ is called *separable* if it can be written as a product of a function of $x$ alone and a function of $y$ alone. The 2D DFT kernel is separable: $$e^{-j 2\pi(ux/M + vy/N)} = e^{-j 2\pi ux/M} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi vy/N}$$ Hence: $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \left[ e^{-j 2\pi ux/M} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j 2\pi vy/N} \right]$$ **Importance (1.5 marks):** - Reduces a 2D DFT into **two successive 1D DFTs** (rows then columns). - Computational cost drops from $O(M^2 N^2)$ to $O(MN(\log_2 M + \log_2 N))$. - Enables the **FFT algorithm** to be used directly on images. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** from Question A or Question B below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Define the 2D Discrete Fourier Transform and its inverse. Explain the separability property with mathematical justification. **(7 marks)** **(b)** State and prove the **2D Convolution Theorem** for the DFT. Mention two engineering applications. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Definition and Separability (7 marks):** The forward 2D DFT is defined as: $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi (ux/M + vy/N)}$$ The inverse 2D IDFT is: $$f(x, y) = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{u=0}^{M-1} \sum_{v=0}^{N-1} F(u, v) \, e^{+j 2\pi (ux/M + vy/N)}$$ **Separability Proof:** Factor the exponential kernel: $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi ux/M} \cdot e^{-j 2\pi vy/N}$$ Re-arrange summation order: $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} e^{-j 2\pi ux/M} \underbrace{\left[ \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{-j 2\pi vy/N} \right]}_{\text{1D DFT of row } x}$$ Let $F_x(v) = \sum_{y} f(x, y) e^{-j 2\pi vy/N}$ be the row-wise 1D DFT. Then: $$F(u, v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} F_x(v) \, e^{-j 2\pi ux/M}$$ which is the 1D DFT of $F_x(v)$ along the column direction. Hence the 2D DFT = **row 1D DFT + column 1D DFT**. (4 marks for derivation + 3 marks for interpretation) **(b) 2D Convolution Theorem (7 marks):** **Statement:** $f(x, y) * h(x, y) \longleftrightarrow F(u, v) \cdot H(u, v)$ **Proof:** (3 marks for proof — see SECTION 3.4) Let $g(x, y) = f(x, y) * h(x, y) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m, n) h(x-m, y-n)$. Taking the DFT: $$G(u, v) = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m, n) h(x-m, y-n) \, e^{-j 2\pi(ux/M + vy/N)}$$ Swapping summations and applying the shift property of the inner sum: $$G(u, v) = \left[ \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m, n) e^{-j 2\pi(um/M + vn/N)} \right] \cdot H(u, v) = F(u, v) H(u, v)$$ **Engineering Applications (2 marks):** 1. **Frequency-domain filtering:** Implementing large-kernel smoothing or sharpening filters via pointwise multiplication instead of expensive convolution. 2. **MRI reconstruction:** Convolution in k-space becomes multiplication, enabling fast tomographic image reconstruction. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Derive the **DC component** and **average intensity** relations of the 2D DFT. Show that the average intensity of an image equals $F(0, 0) / (MN)$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** State and explain the **conjugate symmetry**, **rotation**, and **Parseval's theorem** properties of the 2D DFT with mathematical formulations. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) DC Component Derivation (7 marks):** Setting $u = 0$ and $v = 0$ in the forward DFT: $$F(0, 0) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x, y) \, e^{0} = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} f(x, y)$$ Since $e^{0} = 1$, all exponentials collapse to unity. Thus $F(0, 0)$ equals the **sum of all pixel intensities** in the image. (3 marks) The average pixel intensity is: $$\bar{f} = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{x}\sum_{y} f(x, y) = \frac{F(0, 0)}{MN}$$ Therefore $F(0, 0)$ is also called the **DC component** (analogous to the zero-frequency term in electrical circuits). (2 marks) **Significance:** The DC term is invariant under translation. If we move the entire image by $(x_0, y_0)$, only the **phase** of the DFT changes — the **magnitude** $\vert F(0,0) \vert$ stays constant. (2 marks) **(b) Three Properties (7 marks):** **1. Conjugate Symmetry (2.5 marks):** For real-valued images: $$F(u, v) = F^{*}(-u, -v) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert F(u, v) \vert = \vert F(-u, -v) \vert$$ The magnitude spectrum is symmetric about the origin. This allows storing only half the spectrum for real images, achieving roughly 50% memory savings. **2.
