## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
List any **four** desirable properties of features detected by the SIFT algorithm.

**Model Answer (4 marks mapped to rubric):**
1. **Scale invariance** — features are detected consistently regardless of image zoom.
2. **Rotation invariance** — orientation normalization ensures robustness under image rotation.
3. **Illumination invariance** — unit-length descriptor normalization handles brightness changes.
4. **Robustness to noise and small viewpoint changes** — Taylor-series refinement rejects unstable candidates.

(Any 4 of the above — 3 marks for content, 1 mark for proper presentation.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
What is the role of **support vectors** in an SVM? Why are they called "support" vectors?

**Model Answer:**
Support vectors are the training data points that lie exactly on the **margin boundaries** (i.e., those for which the Lagrange multiplier $\alpha_i > 0$). They are called "support" vectors because they are the only points that *support* (define) the position and orientation of the optimal separating hyperplane. All other training points (with $\alpha_i = 0$) are irrelevant to the final decision boundary. (3 marks)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the four major stages of the **SIFT algorithm** in detail, with the mathematical expressions for scale-space construction and difference of Gaussian. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Scale-Space Extrema Detection (2 marks):**  
The scale space of an image is defined by convolving the input image $I(x,y)$ with a variable-scale Gaussian $G(x,y,\sigma)$:

$$L(x,y,\sigma) = G(x,y,\sigma) \ast I(x,y)$$

A **scale pyramid** is built by progressively smoothing the image. Adjacent Gaussian-blurred images are subtracted to form the **Difference of Gaussian**:

$$D(x,y,\sigma) = L(x,y,k\sigma) - L(x,y,\sigma)$$

Local extrema of $D(x,y,\sigma)$ are detected by comparing each point to its **26 neighbors** (8 in current scale, 9 in scale above, 9 in scale below).

**Stage 2 — Keypoint Localization (2 marks):**  
A second-order **Taylor expansion** of $D(\mathbf{x})$ is used to interpolate the true location of each candidate. Low-contrast keypoints (with $|D(\hat{\mathbf{x}})| < 0.03$) are discarded. Edge responses are eliminated by evaluating the **ratio of principal curvatures** from the $2 \times 2$ Hessian matrix $\mathbf{H}$:

$$\frac{\text{Tr}(\mathbf{H})^2}{\text{Det}(\mathbf{H})} < \frac{(r+1)^2}{r}$$

where $r = 10$ is typically used.

**Stage 3 — Orientation Assignment (1 mark):**  
For each keypoint, an orientation histogram with 36 bins (covering $360^\circ$) is built from gradient magnitudes and orientations within a neighborhood. The peak becomes the **dominant orientation** $\theta(x,y)$:

$$m(x,y) = \sqrt{(L_{x+1,y} - L_{x-1,y})^2 + (L_{x,y+1} - L_{x,y-1})^2}$$

$$\theta(x,y) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{L_{x,y+1} - L_{x,y-1}}{L_{x+1,y} - L_{x-1,y}}\right)$$

**Stage 4 — Descriptor Generation (2 marks):**  
A $16 \times 16$ window centered on the keypoint is rotated by $-\theta$ and divided into a $4 \times 4$ grid of sub-blocks. An **8-bin orientation histogram** is built per sub-block, yielding a $4 \times 4 \times 8 = \mathbf{128}$-dimensional descriptor. The descriptor is then **normalized to unit length** to achieve illumination invariance.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe how **support vectors** define the **optimal hyperplane** in a linear SVM. Derive the margin expression. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Class +1   ●  ●  ●             Class -1   ●  ●  ●
              \                       /
               \                     /
                \   ╔═══════════╗   /
                 \  ║  w·x + b ║  /
                  \ ╚═══════════╝ /
            ←——γ——→ ←——margin—— → ←——γ——→
                  Support         Support
                  Vector 1        Vector 2
```

The two **dashed lines** represent the canonical margin boundaries $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$. The **solid line** is the optimal hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = 0$, equidistant from both.

**Margin Derivation (3 marks):**

For a point $\mathbf{x}_i$, the signed distance to the hyperplane is:

$$d_i = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$$

The geometric margin $\gamma$ is the minimum such signed distance across all correctly classified points. By the canonical constraint $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$, the **minimum** signed distance is exactly $1/\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$. Hence the **total margin** between the two classes is:

$$\gamma_{\text{total}} = \frac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$$

**Optimal Hyperplane (2 marks):**  
SVM selects $\mathbf{w}, b$ that maximize $2/\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$, equivalently minimizing $\tfrac{1}{2}\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$. The solution:

$$\mathbf{w}^\ast = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i^\ast y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

Only points with $\alpha_i^\ast > 0$ — the **support vectors** — contribute. The hyperplane is therefore completely determined by these few critical training points.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **kernel trick** in SVM. Compare **linear, polynomial, and RBF kernels** with suitable mathematical expressions. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Concept of Kernel Trick (3 marks):**  
Some datasets are not linearly separable in their original feature space. Mapping them to a higher-dimensional space via a non-linear transformation $\phi(\cdot)$ can make them linearly separable — but explicit computation of $\phi$ is computationally infeasible. The **kernel trick** exploits the fact that the SVM dual problem depends only on inner products $\langle \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j \rangle$. A **kernel function** $K$ computes this inner product in the high-dimensional space directly, without ever computing $\phi$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle$$

A valid kernel must satisfy **Mercer's condition**: the Gram matrix $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ must be positive semi-definite.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Kernel | Formula | Use Case | Hyperparameters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linear | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ | Linearly separable data, high-D sparse features | None |
| Polynomial | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + c)^d$ | Moderate non-linearity, image classification | Degree $d$, constant $c$ |
| RBF (Gaussian) | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2\right)$ | Strong non-linearity, most general-purpose | $\gamma = 1/(2\sigma^2)$ |

---

**(b)** Discuss how **SIFT features combined with SVM classifiers** form a complete **image classification system**. Mention any two real-world applications. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Pipeline Description (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Feature Extraction with SIFT:**  
SIFT detects keypoints invariant to scale, rotation, and illumination. Each keypoint is described by a 128-D vector. For $N$ keypoints detected in an image, we obtain $N \times 128$ feature vectors.

**Step 2 — Visual Vocabulary Construction:**  
All SIFT descriptors from training images are clustered using **k-means clustering** into $k$ visual words. The cluster centers form a **codebook** (visual vocabulary).

**Step 3 — Bag-of-Visual-Words Histogram:**  
Each image is represented as a **$k$-dimensional histogram** counting how many of its SIFT descriptors are closest to each visual word. This gives a fixed-length representation suitable for SVM input.

**Step 4 — Training the SVM:**  
The histograms of training images, paired with their class labels, are used to train a multi-class SVM (typically using **one-vs-one** or **one-vs-all** strategies with RBF kernel).

**Step 5 — Classification:**  
For a new image, the same pipeline (Steps 1–3) is applied to obtain its histogram, which is fed into the trained SVM to predict the class label.

**Real-World Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR):** Search engines use SIFT + SVM to retrieve visually similar images from large databases.
2. **Medical Image Classification:** Classifying skin lesions, X-rays, or histopathology images as malignant/benign using SIFT features and SVM.
3. (Alternative) **Satellite Image Analysis** — land-cover classification; **Industrial Defect Detection** — automated quality inspection.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting normalization:** Students frequently forget to normalize the SIFT descriptor to unit length, losing illumination invariance — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing margin width:** The geometric margin is $2/\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$, NOT $1/\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$. This is a common slip.
> 3. **Mis-stating the kernel formula:** RBF kernel uses $\exp(-\gamma \lVert\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2)$, NOT $\exp(-\gamma \lVert\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert)$. The square is on the entire norm, not on $\gamma$.
> 4. **Not stating Mercer's condition:** A valid kernel must satisfy positive semi-definiteness of the Gram matrix.
> 5. **Sketching SIFT without writing equations:** Always pair the stage name with the formula ($D(x,y,\sigma)$, $\theta(x,y)$, $m(x,y)$).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for SIFT & SVM (KTU 2024 — Module 4)**

- **SIFT Core:** Scale Invariant Feature Transform; produces 128-D descriptors invariant to scale, rotation, illumination.
- **SIFT Stages:** Scale-Space Construction → DoG Extrema Detection → Keypoint Localization → Orientation Assignment → Descriptor Generation.
- **Difference of Gaussian Formula:** $D(x,y,\sigma) = L(x,y,k\sigma) - L(x,y,\sigma)$ — an efficient approximation of Laplacian of Gaussian.
- **Descriptor Layout:** $4 \times 4$ sub-blocks, each with 8 orientation bins → 128 dimensions, **normalized to unit length**.
- **SVM Definition:** A maximum-margin classifier that finds the optimal hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = 0$.
- **SVM Objective (Hard Margin):** $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$.
- **SVM Objective (Soft Margin):** $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ — parameter $C$ balances margin width and misclassification.
- **Geometric Margin:** $\gamma = 2/\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$ — maximize this → minimize $\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert$.
- **Support Vectors:** Training points with $\alpha_i > 0$ that lie on the margin; they alone define the hyperplane.
- **Kernel Trick:** Replace inner products with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ to enable non-linear classification without explicit high-D mapping.
- **Common Kernels:** Linear, Polynomial $K = (\mathbf{x}_i^T\mathbf{x}_j + c)^d$, RBF $K = \exp(-\gamma \lVert\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2)$.
- **SIFT + SVM Pipeline:** SIFT extracts features → Bag-of-Visual-Words histograms → SVM classifies.
- **Real-World Applications:** CBIR, medical imaging, biometric authentication, robotic navigation, defect detection.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Decision Trees # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning classifier that models decisions and their possible consequences as a hierarchical tree-like structure of nodes and branches. In the context of **Digital Image Processing (DIP)**, decision trees are employed primarily for **image classification**, **pattern recognition**, and **pixel-wise segmentation**, where features extracted from an image (such as texture, intensity, edge magnitude, or spectral response) are recursively partitioned into homogeneous subsets based on a learned splitting rule. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** Decision Trees in DIP are categorized under *Pattern Classification & Image Recognition*. They are tree-structured classifiers where each internal node represents a test on a feature, each branch represents the outcome of that test, and each leaf node represents a class label (or regression value). Formally, a decision tree can be viewed as a function $f : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that maps an input feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a discrete class label $y \in \{1, 2, \dots, C\}$ by traversing the tree from the root to a leaf according to a sequence of binary or multi-way decisions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Diagnosis" Intuition Imagine a doctor diagnosing whether a skin lesion in a medical image is **benign (Class B)** or **malignant (Class M)**. The doctor follows a flowchart of questions: 1. *"Is the pixel intensity of the lesion greater than 150?"* → If **No**, go left; if **Yes**, go right. 2. *"Is the texture variance high?"* → If **No**, classify as benign; if **Yes**, proceed. 3. *"Is the lesion roughly symmetric?"* → Final decision. This flowchart IS a decision tree. The **questions are internal nodes**, the **yes/no arrows are branches**, and the **final diagnosis at the end of each path is a leaf node**. The "best question to ask first" is what training the tree optimizes — analogous to how a doctor's training prioritizes the most discriminating symptom first. In DIP, the "questions" are thresholds applied to features like **Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) contrast**, **LBP histograms**, **Hue values in HSV space**, or **edge orientation histograms**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Decision Trees for Image Processing?** > - **Interpretability**: The decision path is human-readable (unlike a neural network black box). > - **No feature scaling required**: Works directly on raw or normalized features. > - **Handles mixed data types**: Useful for multi-modal images (RGB + depth + IR). > - **Computationally efficient at inference**: $O(\text{depth of tree})$ lookup time. ## 1.3 Structural Anatomy of a Decision Tree A decision tree consists of three types of nodes: | Component | Symbol | Function | DIP Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Root Node** | $r$ | Topmost node; represents the entire training dataset | All pixels in a satellite image | | **Internal Node** | $n_i$ | Represents a test on a single feature attribute | "Mean intensity > 128?" | | **Leaf Node** | $l_j$ | Terminal node; represents the final class prediction | "Class = Vegetation" | | **Branch** | $b_k$ | Outcome of a test; connects parent to child | "Yes" branch, "No" branch | The **depth** of a tree is the length of the longest path from the root to any leaf. A tree with depth $d$ can classify into at most $2^d$ distinct regions in the feature space. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Algorithmic Philosophy — The Recursive Partitioning Principle The construction of a decision tree follows a **divide-and-conquer, greedy top-down induction** strategy (the same family includes the classical algorithms **ID3**, **C4.5**, and **CART**). The fundamental operational loop is: 1. **Start** at the root with the entire training dataset $S$ of $N$ samples. 2. **Evaluate every candidate feature** $A \in \{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n\}$ and every candidate threshold $t$ on that feature, computing a "purity score" of the resulting split. 3. **Select the feature-threshold pair** $(A^*, t^*)$ that yields the **highest purity gain** (or equivalently, the **greatest reduction in impurity**). 4. **Split** the dataset $S$ into $S_{\text{left}}$ and $S_{\text{right}}$ subsets. 5. **Recursively repeat** steps 2–4 on each non-pure child subset. 6. **Stop** when: - All samples in a node belong to the same class (pure node), OR - A maximum depth $d_{\max}$ is reached, OR - The number of samples $n < n_{\min}$ (minimum samples per leaf), OR - No split yields a positive information gain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Greedy Caveat:** The tree makes the locally optimal split at each node. This does NOT guarantee a globally optimal tree (which is NP-complete to find). This is a frequent KTU exam pitfall. ## 2.2 Three Purity Metrics — The Engine of Splits The "best question to ask" is quantified using **impurity measures**. Three are mandatory for KTU 2024: ### Metric 1 — Entropy (used in ID3 / C4.5) Entropy measures the disorder or randomness in a dataset. For a node with class probability distribution $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_C$: $$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$ - $H(S) = 0$ → perfectly pure node (all samples same class). - $H(S) = \log_2 C$ → maximum disorder (uniform distribution). ### Metric 2 — Information Gain (IG) Information Gain is the **reduction in entropy** achieved by partitioning $S$ on feature $A$: $$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{|S_v|}{|S|} H(S_v)$$ where $S_v$ is the subset of $S$ where feature $A = v$. The chosen split maximizes $IG(S, A)$. ### Metric 3 — Gini Impurity (used in CART) $$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$ - $Gini(S) = 0$ → pure. - $Gini(S)$ is maximized when classes are equally distributed. The **Gini Gain** (analogous to IG) is: $$\Delta Gini(S, A) = Gini(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{|S_v|}{|S|} Gini(S_v)$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Markdown Note:** All absolute value and division bars use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the raw pipe `|` character to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Units | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $0 \le H \le \log_2 C$ (bits) | ID3, C4.5 | | 2 | **Information Gain** | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $IG \ge 0$ | Feature selection | | 3 | **Gini Index** | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^{2}$ | $0 \le Gini \le 1 - \frac{1}{C}$ | CART | | 4 | **Gini Gain** | $\Delta Gini = Gini(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_v)$ | $\Delta Gini \ge 0$ | CART split | | 5 | **Classification Error** | $E(S) = 1 - \max_{i} p_i$ | $0 \le E \le 1 - \frac{1}{C}$ | Pruning evaluation | | 6 | **Gain Ratio (C4.5)** | $GR(S,A) = \frac{IG(S,A)}{SplitInfo(S,A)}$; $\quad SplitInfo = -\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Normalized IG | C4.5 only | | 7 | **Tree Depth** | $d_{\max}$ (hyperparameter) | Integers $\ge 1$ | Stopping criterion | | 8 | **Pruning Loss** | $L_{\alpha}(T) = \sum_{t \in \text{leaves}} N_t \cdot H(t) + \alpha \cdot \vert \text{leaves}(T) \vert$ | Cost-complexity | Post-pruning | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Image Processing Pipelines In a real DIP pipeline, decision trees are deployed as: - **Final-stage classifier** after feature extraction (e.g., classify land cover from Sentinel-2 satellite imagery using NDVI, EVI, texture features). - **Pixel-level segmentation** in medical imaging (tumor vs. non-tumor in MRI). - **Ensemble core** — **Random Forests** are collections of decorrelated decision trees trained on random feature subsets, dramatically improving robustness on noisy images. - **Real-time embedded vision** on resource-constrained devices (drones, mobile phones) due to $O(\log N)$ inference after tree flattening. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style > **Problem Context:** A DIP system must classify 16 image patches (training samples) as either **"Edge" (E)** or **"Non-Edge" (N)**. Two candidate features are extracted from each patch: > - **Feature F1** (Mean Gradient Magnitude) > - **Feature F2** (Local Variance) > > Use **Information Gain (with Entropy)** to determine which feature the root node should split on. Full dataset distribution: **9 Edge patches, 7 Non-Edge patches**. ### Step 1 — Compute the Entropy of the Root Node $S$ The root node $S$ contains $N = 16$ patches, with class probabilities: $$p_E = \frac{9}{16}, \quad p_N = \frac{7}{16}$$ Substituting into the entropy formula: $$ \begin{aligned} H(S) &= -p_E \log_2 p_E \;-\; p_N \log_2 p_N \\ &= -\frac{9}{16} \log_2 \frac{9}{16} \;-\; \frac{7}{16} \log_2 \frac{7}{16} \\ &= -0.5625 \times \log_2 0.5625 \;-\; 0.4375 \times \log_2 0.4375 \end{aligned} $$ Computing each logarithm numerically: $$ \begin{aligned} \log_2 0.5625 &= \log_2 \left(\frac{9}{16}\right) = \log_2 9 - \log_2 16 \\ &= 3.1699 - 4.0000 = -0.8301 \\ \log_2 0.4375 &= \log_2 \left(\frac{7}{16}\right) = \log_2 7 - \log_2 16 \\ &= 2.8074 - 4.0000 = -1.1926 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} H(S) &= -0.5625 \times (-0.8301) - 0.4375 \times (-1.1926) \\ &= 0.4669 + 0.5218 \\ &= 0.9887 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The dataset is nearly maximally disordered (binary entropy at $p=0.5$ would be $1.0$ bit). A good split should reduce this significantly. ### Step 2 — Evaluate Feature F1 (Mean Gradient Magnitude) Suppose thresholding F1 at $t = 50$ produces: - **Branch F1 > 50** → $S_1$ has **6 patches**: 6 Edge, 0 Non-Edge. - **Branch F1 ≤ 50** → $S_2$ has **10 patches**: 3 Edge, 7 Non-Edge. **Entropy of $S_1$ (pure node):** $$ \begin{aligned} p_E &= 1, \quad p_N = 0 \\ H(S_1) &= -1 \cdot \log_2 1 - 0 \cdot \log_2 0 = 0 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ (by convention, $0 \log_2 0 = 0$). **Entropy of $S_2$:** $$ \begin{aligned} p_E &= \frac{3}{10} = 0.3, \quad p_N = \frac{7}{10} = 0.7 \\ \log_2 0.3 &= -1.7370 \\ \log_2 0.7 &= -0.5146 \\ H(S_2) &= -0.3 \times (-1.7370) - 0.7 \times (-0.5146) \\ &= 0.5211 + 0.3602 = 0.8813 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Weighted post-split entropy:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S \mid F1) &= \frac{6}{16} H(S_1) + \frac{10}{16} H(S_2) \\ &= \frac{6}{16} \times 0 + \frac{10}{16} \times 0.8813 \\ &= 0 + 0.5508 = 0.5508 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Information Gain for F1:** $$ \begin{aligned} IG(S, F1) &= H(S) - H(S \mid F1) \\ &= 0.9887 - 0.5508 \\ &= 0.4379 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate Feature F2 (Local Variance) Suppose thresholding F2 at $t = 30$ produces: - **Branch F2 > 30** → $S_3$ has **8 patches**: 7 Edge, 1 Non-Edge. - **Branch F2 ≤ 30** → $S_4$ has **8 patches**: 2 Edge, 6 Non-Edge. **Entropy of $S_3$:** $$ \begin{aligned} p_E &= 0.875, \quad p_N = 0.125 \\ \log_2 0.875 &= -0.1926 \\ \log_2 0.125 &= -3.0000 \\ H(S_3) &= -0.875 \times (-0.1926) - 0.125 \times (-3.0000) \\ &= 0.1685 + 0.3750 = 0.5436 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Entropy of $S_4$** (by symmetry, $p_E = 0.25, p_N = 0.75$): $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_4) &= -0.25 \log_2 0.25 - 0.75 \log_2 0.75 \\ &= -0.25 \times (-2) - 0.75 \times (-0.4150) \\ &= 0.5 + 0.3113 = 0.8113 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Weighted post-split entropy:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S \mid F2) &= \frac{8}{16} \times 0.5436 + \frac{8}{16} \times 0.8113 \\ &= 0.5 \times 0.5436 + 0.5 \times 0.8113 \\ &= 0.2718 + 0.4057 = 0.6775 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Information Gain for F2:** $$ \begin{aligned} IG(S, F2) &= 0.9887 - 0.6775 = 0.3112 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Decision: Choose the Best Feature $$ \begin{aligned} IG(S, F1) &= 0.4379 \text{ bits} \\ IG(S, F2) &= 0.3112 \text{ bits} \\ \therefore \quad F1 \succ F2 &\implies \text{Split root on F1 (Mean Gradient Magnitude)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Analytical Decision Matrix | Feature | Branch Composition | Branch Entropy | Weighted $H(S \mid F)$ | $IG(S, F)$ | Winner | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **F1** (Gradient > 50) | (6E, 0N) and (3E, 7N) | 0 ; 0.8813 | 0.5508 | **0.4379** | ✅ **ROOT** | | **F2** (Variance > 30) | (7E, 1N) and (2E, 6N) | 0.5436 ; 0.8113 | 0.6775 | 0.3112 | ❌ | | **Random split baseline** | (5E, 4N) and (4E, 3N) | 0.9911 ; 0.9852 | 0.9883 | 0.0004 | ❌❌ | ## 3.2 Gini-Based Cross-Check (Optional but KTU-Favored) For F1 split: $$ \begin{aligned} Gini(S_1) &= 1 - (1)^2 - (0)^2 = 0 \\ Gini(S_2) &= 1 - (0.3)^2 - (0.7)^2 = 1 - 0.09 - 0.49 = 0.42 \\ \Delta Gini(S, F1) &= Gini(S) - \left[\frac{6}{16}(0) + \frac{10}{16}(0.42)\right] \\ &= 0.4922 - 0.2625 = 0.2297 \end{aligned} $$ For F2 split: $$ \begin{aligned} Gini(S_3) &= 1 - (0.875)^2 - (0.125)^2 = 1 - 0.7656 - 0.0156 = 0.2188 \\ Gini(S_4) &= 1 - (0.25)^2 - (0.75)^2 = 1 - 0.0625 - 0.5625 = 0.3750 \\ \Delta Gini(S, F2) &= 0.4922 - \left[\frac{8}{16}(0.2188) + \frac{8}{16}(0.3750)\right] \\ &= 0.4922 - 0.2969 = 0.1953 \end{aligned} $$ Both impurity metrics agree: **F1 is the superior root split**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Decision Tree Induced on the Image-Patch Example ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Root Node S

[KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define a Decision Tree. List any two advantages of using decision trees in image classification.** **Model Answer:** A Decision Tree is a supervised, non-parametric classifier that recursively partitions the feature space using axis-aligned splits, producing a hierarchical tree of decision nodes and terminal leaf nodes that output class labels. **Two advantages:** 1. **Interpretability**: The decision path is transparent and can be visualized as a flowchart — crucial in medical/legal imaging applications where decisions must be justified. 2. **No feature scaling required**: Unlike SVMs or Neural Networks, decision trees are invariant to monotonic transformations of features, so raw pixel intensities or normalized features can both be used directly. 3. *(Optional third)* Handles both numerical (pixel intensity) and categorical (region label) features simultaneously. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is Information Gain? Why is it preferred over raw entropy for selecting the root split?** **Model Answer:** **Information Gain (IG)** is the reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning a dataset $S$ using a feature $A$: $$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$ **Why IG over raw entropy:** Raw entropy $H(S)$ is a property of the dataset alone and does not depend on any feature — it cannot tell us which feature is best for splitting. IG measures the *delta* in purity attributable to a particular split, enabling direct comparison between features. The feature with the **highest IG** produces the **largest reduction in disorder** and is therefore the most discriminative at that node. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1** #### Q.A(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Construct a Decision Tree using the ID3 algorithm (Information Gain criterion) for the following image classification dataset. Each tuple represents one image patch with features (Mean Intensity, Edge Density, Texture Variance) and Class label (Tumor / Non-Tumor):** | Patch | Mean Intensity | Edge Density | Texture Variance | Class | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | High | High | High | Tumor | | 2 | High | Low | Low | Tumor | | 3 | Low | High | High | Tumor | | 4 | Low | Low | Low | Non-Tumor | | 5 | High | High | Low | Tumor | | 6 | Low | High | Low | Non-Tumor | | 7 | Low | Low | High | Non-Tumor | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Root Entropy $H(S)$:** Class distribution: 4 Tumor, 3 Non-Tumor. $$ \begin{aligned} p_T &= 4/7, \quad p_{NT} = 3/7 \\ H(S) &= -\frac{4}{7}\log_2 \frac{4}{7} - \frac{3}{7}\log_2 \frac{3}{7} \\ &= 0.4614 + 0.5239 = 0.9853 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — IG for "Mean Intensity":** Split produces: - **High**: Patches {1, 2, 5} → 3 Tumor, 0 Non-Tumor → $H = 0$ - **Low**: Patches {3, 4, 6, 7} → 1 Tumor, 3 Non-Tumor For "Low" subset: $p_T = 1/4 = 0.25, p_{NT} = 3/4 = 0.75$ $$ \begin{aligned} H(\text{Low}) &= -0.25 \log_2 0.25 - 0.75 \log_2 0.75 \\ &= 0.5 + 0.3113 = 0.8113 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} IG(S, \text{Intensity}) &= 0.9853 - \left[\frac{3}{7}(0) + \frac{4}{7}(0.8113)\right] \\ &= 0.9853 - 0.4636 = 0.5217 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — IG for "Edge Density":** Split produces: - **High**: {1, 3, 5, 6} → 3 Tumor, 1 Non-Tumor → $p_T = 0.75, p_{NT} = 0.25$ → $H = 0.8113$ bits - **Low**: {2, 4, 7} → 1 Tumor, 2 Non-Tumor → $p_T = 1/3, p_{NT} = 2/3$ → $H = 0.9183$ bits $$ \begin{aligned} IG(S, \text{Edge}) &= 0.9853 - \left[\frac{4}{7}(0.8113) + \frac{3}{7}(0.9183)\right] \\ &= 0.9853 - (0.4636 + 0.3935) \\ &= 0.9853 - 0.8571 = 0.1282 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — IG for "Texture Variance":** Split produces: - **High**: {1, 3, 7} → 2 Tumor, 1 Non-Tumor → $p_T = 2/3$ → $H = 0.9183$ bits - **Low**: {2, 4, 5, 6} → 2 Tumor, 2 Non-Tumor → $H = 1.0$ bits $$ \begin{aligned} IG(S, \text{Texture}) &= 0.9853 - \left[\frac{3}{7}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{7}(1.0)\right] \\ &= 0.9853 - (0.3935 + 0.5714) \\ &= 0.9853 - 0.9649 = 0.0204 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Choose Root:** $$ IG(\text{Intensity}) = 0.5217 \succ IG(\text{Edge}) = 0.1282 \succ IG(\text{Texture}) = 0.0204 $$ **Root split: Mean Intensity.** **Step 6 — Recurse on "High" subset {1,2,5}:** All 3 are Tumor → **Pure leaf node → Class = Tumor**. **Step 7 — Recurse on "Low" subset {3,4,6,7}:** Compute new entropy: $$ H(S_{Low}) = 0.8113 \text{ bits} $$ Test remaining features on this subset: - Edge Density split: High→{3,6} (1T,1NT) Low→{4,7} (0T,2NT) - $IG_{Low}(\text{Edge}) = 0.8113 - [0.5 \times 1.0 + 0.5 \times 0] = 0.8113 - 0.5 = 0.3113$ bits - Texture Variance split: High→{3,7} (1T,1NT) Low→{4,6} (0T,2NT) - $IG_{Low}(\text{Texture}) = 0.8113 - [0.5 \times 1.0 + 0.5 \times 0] = 0.3113$ bits Both tie — choose Edge Density (lexicographic priority). **Final Tree:** ``` Root: Mean Intensity? ├── High → [Leaf: TUMOR] (3 patches, pure) └── Low → Edge Density? ├── High → [Leaf: ?] (2 patches: 1T, 1NT → Tie → default Tumor) └── Low → [Leaf: NON-TUMOR] (2 patches, pure) ``` --- #### Q.A(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Explain the Gini Index impurity measure used in the CART algorithm. For a binary classification node with $p_1 = 0.7$ and $p_2 = 0.3$, compute the Gini impurity and compare it with the entropy value.** **Model Solution:** **Gini Index Definition:** The Gini impurity at a node measures the probability of misclassifying a randomly chosen sample if it were labeled according to the class distribution at that node: $$ Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^{2} $$ For binary classification ($C = 2$): $$ Gini(S) = 1 - p_1^2 - p_2^2 = 2 p_1 (1 - p_1) $$ **Properties:** - $Gini(S) = 0$ when node is pure. - $Gini(S)$ is maximized at $p_1 = p_2 = 0.5$ giving $Gini_{\max} = 0.5$. - Cheaper to compute than entropy (no logarithm). - Used by default in scikit-learn's `DecisionTreeClassifier`. **Numerical Computation:** Given $p_1 = 0.7$ (Class 1), $p_2 = 0.3$ (Class 2): $$ \begin{aligned} Gini &= 1 - (0.7)^2 - (0.3)^2 \\ &= 1 - 0.49 - 0.09 \\ &= 0.42 \end{aligned} $$ **Entropy Comparison:** $$ \begin{aligned} H &= -0.7 \log_2 0.7 - 0.3 \log_2 0.3 \\ &= -0.7 \times (-0.5146) - 0.3 \times (-1.7370) \\ &= 0.3602 + 0.5211 = 0.8813 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison Table:** | Measure | Value | Range | Computational Cost | |---|---|---|---| | **Entropy $H(S)$** | 0.8813 bits | $[0, 1]$ bit (binary) | Higher (logarithm) | | **Gini $Gini(S)$** | 0.4200 (unitless) | $[0, 0.5]$ (binary) | Lower (polynomial) | **Conclusion:** For binary classification, Gini = $\frac{H}{2}$ approximately when $H$ is in bits (Taylor approximation). Both yield identical split decisions in ~98% of cases. CART prefers Gini for computational efficiency; ID3/C4.5 use entropy for theoretical purity guarantees. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2** #### Q.B(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Discuss the problem of overfitting in decision trees. Explain pre-pruning and post-pruning techniques with suitable diagrams.** **Model Solution:** **Overfitting in Decision Trees:** An overfitted tree memorizes training data — including noise — leading to: - Perfect accuracy on training data. - Poor generalization on unseen test data. - Excessively deep tree with leaves containing very few samples. Mathematically, a tree $T$ is overfitted when its training error $E_{train}(T) \to 0$ but its generalization gap $E_{test}(T) - E_{train}(T)$ is large. **Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping):** Stop tree growth *before* it perfectly fits the training set. Common criteria: - **Maximum depth** $d_{\max}$: Halt recursion when depth reaches $d_{\max}$. - **Minimum samples per leaf** $n_{\min}$: Halt if $|S_v| < n_{\min}$. - **Minimum impurity decrease**: Halt if best $IG < \theta$ (threshold). - **Maximum number of leaf nodes**. $$ \text{Pre-prune rule:} \quad \text{Split only if } IG(S, A^*) \geq \theta_{\min} $$ **Advantages:** Fast, simple. **Disadvantages:** Risks underfitting due to "horizon effect" (a seemingly useless early split may enable a highly informative split deeper down). **Post-Pruning (Cost-Complexity Pruning):** Grow the full tree first, then *collapse* subtrees back into leaf nodes if doing so reduces a regularized cost: $$ L_{\alpha}(T) = \sum_{t \in \text{leaves}(T)} N_t \cdot H(t) + \alpha \cdot \vert \text{leaves}(T) \vert $$ where $N_t$ = number of samples at leaf $t$, and $\alpha \geq 0$ is the regularization parameter controlling the bias-variance trade-off. **Algorithm (Reduced Error Pruning):** 1. Grow full tree $T_{\max}$ on training data. 2. For each internal node $n$, evaluate $T_n$ (subtree at $n$) vs. replacing it with a leaf. 3. If replacing reduces $L_{\alpha}$, prune. 4. Repeat until no further improvement. **Advantages:** More accurate than pre-pruning because decisions are made with full information. **Disadvantages:** Computationally expensive. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Full Grown Tree T_max"] -->|Evaluate each internal node| B{"Cost_α with subtree
vs
Cost_α with leaf"} B -->|Subtree cheaper| C["Keep subtree"] B -->|Leaf cheaper| D["Prune to leaf"] C --> E["Pruned Tree T_final"] D --> E ``` --- #### Q.B(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **For a binary classification dataset with $S = 10$ samples, class distribution: 6 Positive, 4 Negative, evaluate the Information Gain for splitting on feature $A$ if the split produces (Positive=5, Negative=1) in branch A=Yes and (Positive=1, Negative=3) in branch A=No. Justify whether the split is useful.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Root Entropy:** $$ \begin{aligned} p_{+} &= 6/10 = 0.6, \quad p_{-} = 4/10 = 0.4 \\ H(S) &= -0.6 \log_2 0.6 - 0.4 \log_2 0.4 \\ &= -0.6 \times (-0.7370) - 0.4 \times (-1.3219) \\ &= 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Branch Entropies:** Branch A=Yes (6 samples: 5+, 1−): $$ \begin{aligned} p_{+} &= 5/6 \approx 0.8333, \quad p_{-} = 1/6 \approx 0.1667 \\ H(\text{Yes}) &= -0.8333 \log_2 0.8333 - 0.1667 \log_2 0.1667 \\ &= 0.2198 + 0.4308 = 0.6500 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ Branch A=No (4 samples: 1+, 3−): $$ \begin{aligned} p_{+} &= 1/4 = 0.25, \quad p_{-} = 3/4 = 0.75 \\ H(\text{No}) &= -0.25 \log_2 0.25 - 0.75 \log_2 0.75 \\ &= 0.5 + 0.3113 = 0.8113 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Weighted Post-Split Entropy:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S \mid A) &= \frac{6}{10} \times 0.6500 + \frac{4}{10} \times 0.8113 \\ &= 0.3900 + 0.3245 = 0.7145 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Information Gain:** $$ \begin{aligned} IG(S, A) &= H(S) - H(S \mid A) \\ &= 0.9710 - 0.7145 \\ &= 0.2565 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Justification:** | Criterion | Value | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | $H(S)$ | 0.9710 bits | High disorder at root | | $H(S \mid A)$ | 0.7145 bits | Reduced disorder post-split | | $IG(S, A)$ | 0.2565 bits | Moderate gain | | Relative reduction | $\frac{0.2565}{0.9710} = 26.4\%$ | Substantial reduction | **Verdict:** The split is **useful**. It reduces the disorder by ~26%, producing one highly pure branch (5+/1−) and a moderately pure branch (1+/3−). Neither branch is fully pure, so the tree should recursively continue splitting both children. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the $0 \log_2 0 = 0$ convention** → Marks lost when computing entropy of pure nodes. > 2. **Using natural log ($\ln$) instead of $\log_2$** → Entropy must be in BITS, so always $\log_2$. > 3. **Mixing up IG and Entropy** → IG is a *reduction*, not an absolute value. The "weight" $|S_v|/|S|$ in the weighted sum is the most-skipped step. > 4. **Not normalizing probabilities** → Always ensure $p_1 + p_2 + \dots + p_C = 1$ before plugging into formulas. > 5. **In tree construction, forgetting to recurse** → After choosing the root, students often forget to recursively split the children. Show the recursion explicitly. > 6. **Confusing Gini with Gini Gain** → $Gini(S)$ measures impurity; $\Delta Gini$ measures the *reduction* (used for split selection). > 7. **Writing $|\cdot|$ with vertical pipes inside markdown tables** → Always use $\vert$ or `\mid` in LaTeX-wrapped table content to avoid breaking markdown rendering. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ A **Decision Tree** is a hierarchical, flowchart-like supervised classifier with **root**, **internal**, and **leaf** nodes connected by **branches**. - ✅ **Tree construction** follows a **greedy top-down recursive partitioning** strategy. - ✅ The **best split** maximizes **Information Gain** (ID3/C4.5) or **Gini Gain** (CART). - ✅ **Entropy** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ ranges in $[0, \log_2 C]$ bits. - ✅ **Information Gain** $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{|S_v|}{|S|} H(S_v)$. - ✅ **Gini Index** $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ ranges in $[0, 1 - 1/C]$. - ✅ **Gain Ratio** (C4.5) = $\frac{IG}{\text{SplitInfo}}$ — normalizes IG against feature cardinality. - ✅ **Stopping criteria**: pure node, max depth, min samples per leaf, min impurity decrease. - ✅ **Overfitting** is mitigated by **pre-pruning** (early stop) and **post-pruning** (cost-complexity $L_\alpha$). - ✅ In DIP, decision trees are used for **pixel classification**, **segmentation**, and **object recognition** using features like GLCM, LBP, gradient, and color histograms. - ✅ **Random Forest** = ensemble of decorrelated decision trees → dramatic improvement in noisy image classification. - ✅ **Greedy ≠ Optimal**: Local splits don't guarantee global optimum (NP-complete problem).

topics.title: Object Detection using HOG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET635 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Object Detection using HOG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG)** is a **feature descriptor** used in computer vision and image processing for the purpose of **object detection**. The technique counts occurrences of **gradient orientation** in localized portions (cells) of an image. The HOG descriptor was introduced by **Navneet Dalal and Bill Triggs** (INRIA, 2005), originally for the task of pedestrian detection.

The HOG descriptor captures the **shape and structural information** of objects by describing the distribution of intensity gradients within small spatial regions (cells) and normalizing them across larger overlapping blocks. The final concatenated vector is then fed to a classifier (typically a **linear Support Vector Machine (SVM)**) to detect object presence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Parameters (Dalal–Triggs Defaults):**
> - Cell size: **8 × 8** pixels
> - Block size: **2 × 2** cells (16 × 16 pixels)
> - Number of orientation bins: **9** (unsigned, 0°–180°)
> - Block stride: **8** pixels (50% overlap between blocks)
> - Detector window: **64 × 128** pixels (for pedestrian detection)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Silhouette of a Shadow":**
> Imagine you are trying to identify a person standing in a dimly lit room by looking at the **outline of their shadow** on a wall. You don't care about their facial color, the texture of their clothes, or the lighting. You only care about **which directions the edges of the shadow are pointing** (up, down, left, right, diagonal) and **how strong** each direction is. A vertical edge of the shadow tells you it's a torso, a horizontal edge tells you a limb is bent, and so on. The **HOG descriptor works the same way** — it forgets color and fine texture and instead records *the direction and strength of brightness changes* (gradients) in small image patches. By collecting this information across the whole image and comparing it to a learned template (via SVM), it can robustly detect objects like pedestrians, cars, or animals.

### Why HOG? — The Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike raw pixel intensity values which are sensitive to illumination changes, **gradient orientations are invariant (to a large degree) to lighting variations**. A pedestrian looks the same in bright sunlight or in shadow — what changes is the absolute brightness, but the *edges* (transitions from dark to light) remain similar. HOG exploits this invariance.

| Property | Raw Pixels | HOG Descriptor |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| Sensitivity to illumination | High | Low (due to normalization) |
| Captures shape information | Poorly | Strongly |
| Translation invariance | None | Partial (block-level) |
| Computational cost | Low | Moderate |
| Discriminative power for objects | Low | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The HOG Algorithm — Step-by-Step Pipeline

The HOG feature extraction is performed in **five sequential stages**:

#### Step 1: Global Image Normalization (Optional)
- **Gamma correction** is sometimes applied to reduce the effect of local intensity variations.
- Dalal & Triggs found that **square-root gamma compression** ($\gamma = 0.5$) gave slightly better results than $\log$ compression.
- However, in modern implementations, this step is often skipped because block normalization achieves similar effects.

#### Step 2: Gradient Computation
- Compute the **first-order image gradients** in the x and y directions using **1-D centered, point discrete derivative masks**:
  - Horizontal: $[-1, 0, +1]$
  - Vertical:  $[-1, 0, +1]^T$
- The gradient at pixel $(x, y)$ is:
  - $G_x(x, y) = I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)$
  - $G_y(x, y) = I(x, y+1) - I(x, y-1)$
- Then compute **magnitude** and **orientation (angle)** at each pixel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why centered masks?**
> A centered mask aligns the gradient with the pixel of interest, providing more accurate spatial localization than a forward-difference mask $[-1, +1]$. This is critical because HOG cells are small (8×8), and misalignments would degrade the histogram.

#### Step 3: Spatial Binning — Cell Histograms
- Divide the image (or detection window) into **small connected regions called cells**, typically **8 × 8 pixels**.
- For each cell, construct a **1-D histogram of gradient orientations** over all pixels in the cell.
- The histogram has **N bins** (commonly **9 bins** for unsigned gradients in 0°–180°).
- Each pixel's gradient magnitude is **bilinearly interpolated** into the histogram bins based on its orientation — this distributes the magnitude between two adjacent bin centers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signed vs. Unsigned Gradients:**
> - **Unsigned (0°–180°):** Dalal & Triggs found this works significantly better for human detection. A vertical edge pointing "down" is treated the same as one pointing "up".
> - **Signed (0°–360°):** Doubles the number of bins (18 bins), but performance is generally worse for pedestrians.

#### Step 4: Block Normalization
- Group cells into **larger spatial blocks** (typically **2 × 2 cells**, i.e., 16 × 16 pixels).
- Concatenate the histograms of all cells in the block to form a **long feature vector**.
- **Normalize** this vector to compensate for local illumination and contrast variations.
- Blocks **overlap** (stride = 8 pixels, i.e., 50% overlap), so each cell contributes to multiple block descriptors.

#### Step 5: Feature Vector Assembly & Classification
- Concatenate the normalized histograms from **all blocks** in the detection window to form the **final HOG feature vector**.
- For a 64 × 128 window with 8 × 8 cells, 2 × 2 blocks, and 50% overlap, this yields a vector of dimension:
  - Number of blocks = $7 \times 15 = 105$
  - Features per block = $4 \text{ cells} \times 9 \text{ bins} = 36$
  - **Total dimension = $105 \times 36 = 3780$**
- This vector is classified by a **linear SVM** trained on positive (object) and negative (background) samples.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units | Purpose |
|--------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| $I(x, y)$ | Pixel intensity at position $(x, y)$ | $[0, 255]$ | Input image |
| $G_x(x, y)$ | $I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)$ | Intensity / pixel | Horizontal gradient |
| $G_y(x, y)$ | $I(x, y+1) - I(x, y-1)$ | Intensity / pixel | Vertical gradient |
| $\vert G(x, y) \vert$ | $\sqrt{G_x(x, y)^2 + G_y(x, y)^2}$ | Intensity / pixel | Gradient magnitude |
| $\theta(x, y)$ | $\arctan\left(\dfrac{G_y(x, y)}{G_x(x, y)}\right)$ | Degrees or radians | Gradient orientation |
| $h_k$ | Weighted vote of pixel into bin $k$ | Normalized magnitude | Cell histogram value |
| $\mathbf{v}$ | Concatenated cell histograms in a block | Vector | Block feature vector |
| L2-norm | $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_i v_i^2}$ | Scalar | Normalization factor |
| L1-norm | $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_1 = \sum_i \vert v_i \vert$ | Scalar | Normalization factor |
| L2-norm: $\mathbf{v}'$ | $\dfrac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2}}$ | Normalized vector | Final block descriptor |
| L1-norm: $\mathbf{v}'$ | $\dfrac{\mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_1 + \varepsilon}$ | Normalized vector | Final block descriptor |
| L2-hys: $\mathbf{v}'$ | L2-normalize, then clip to $\max(v_i, 0.2)$, then re-normalize | Normalized vector | Final block descriptor |
| $\varepsilon$ | Small constant (e.g., $10^{-5}$ or $0.01$) | Scalar | Prevents division by zero |
| $B_w, B_h$ | Block width, height in pixels (e.g., 16, 16) | pixels | Block size |
| $C_w, C_h$ | Cell width, height in pixels (e.g., 8, 8) | pixels | Cell size |
| $N_b$ | Number of orientation bins (commonly 9) | Integer | Histogram bins per cell |
| Bin width | $\dfrac{180°}{N_b}$ (unsigned) or $\dfrac{360°}{N_b}$ (signed) | Degrees | Histogram resolution |

---

### 2.3 Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world deployment of HOG-based detectors:**
> 1. **Pedestrian detection** in autonomous vehicles (e.g., early versions of Mobileye, Tesla Autopilot vision modules)
> 2. **Surveillance and security systems** for crowd monitoring
> 3. **Traffic sign recognition** in advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS)
> 4. **Animal detection in wildlife monitoring** (deer, cattle crossing roads)
> 5. **Assistive technologies** for visually impaired individuals (obstacle avoidance)
> 6. **Sports analytics** for player tracking and pose estimation
> 7. **Robotics** for object recognition in warehouse automation

The HOG+SVM pipeline was the **state-of-the-art pedestrian detector** from 2005 until the rise of deep convolutional neural networks (CNN-based detectors like YOLO, Faster R-CNN, and SSD) around 2014–2016. Even today, HOG is widely used in **embedded systems and low-power devices** where deep learning inference is too expensive.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Gradient Magnitude and Orientation

Given an image $I(x, y)$, the gradient is a 2-D vector:

$$
\nabla I(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} G_x(x, y) \\ G_y(x, y) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial x} \\ \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Using the 1-D centered difference approximation:

$$
G_x(x, y) = I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)
$$

$$
G_y(x, y) = I(x, y+1) - I(x, y-1)
$$

The **magnitude** of this gradient vector is its Euclidean norm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert G(x, y) \vert &= \sqrt{G_x(x, y)^2 + G_y(x, y)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\big[I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)\big]^2 + \big[I(x, y+1) - I(x, y-1)\big]^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **orientation** is the angle the gradient vector makes with the horizontal axis:

$$
\theta(x, y) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{G_y(x, y)}{G_x(x, y)}\right)
$$

For robustness, the 4-quadrant inverse tangent (`atan2`) is typically used:

$$
\theta(x, y) = \text{atan2}\big(G_y(x, y),\; G_x(x, y)\big) \in [-180°, +180°]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For HOG, this is often **wrapped** to $[0°, 180°]$ (unsigned) by taking $\theta \bmod 180°$, because for human silhouettes, the orientation of an edge "downward" is as informative as "upward".

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Block Normalization (L2-Norm)

Let $\mathbf{v} = [v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n]$ be the concatenated histogram of a block. The L2-norm is:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i^2}
$$

The L2-normalized descriptor is:

$$
\mathbf{v}' = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2} = \left[ \frac{v_1}{\sqrt{\sum v_i^2}},\; \frac{v_2}{\sqrt{\sum v_i^2}},\; \ldots \right]
$$

To prevent division by zero when the block has uniform intensity (no gradient), a small constant $\varepsilon$ is added:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L2-norm}} &= \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2}} \\
&= \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i^2 + \varepsilon^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $\varepsilon^2$ (not $\varepsilon$) is added inside the square root — this is a subtle but important detail. Dalal & Triggs used $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$ in their experiments. The use of $\varepsilon^2$ ensures that when $\mathbf{v}$ is small, the denominator does not blow up, but it does not artificially inflate the norm.

Similarly, the **L1-norm** and **L1-sqrt** normalizations are:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L1-norm}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_1 + \varepsilon} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert v_i \vert + \varepsilon}
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L1-sqrt}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_1 + \varepsilon}}
$$

The **L2-hys** (L2 with hysteresis clipping) variant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L2}} &= \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2}} \\
\mathbf{v}_{\text{clip}}[i] &= \min\big(\mathbf{v}_{\text{L2}}[i],\, 0.2\big) \\
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L2-hys}} &= \frac{\mathbf{v}_{\text{clip}}}{\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{clip}} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dalal & Triggs found that **L2-hys** performed best, followed closely by L2-norm, with L1-norm slightly worse. The clipping at 0.2 limits the influence of any single dominant bin, making the descriptor more robust to noise and outlier gradients.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Single Cell Histogram + Block Normalization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Setup:**
> Consider a tiny 4 × 4 image patch representing a single HOG cell (we use a larger cell to compute meaningful interior gradients):

$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 20 & 20 & 20 \\ 20 & 20 & 20 & 20 \\ 200 & 200 & 200 & 200 \\ 200 & 200 & 200 & 200 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This represents a **horizontal edge** at row 2 (dark above, bright below). Let's compute the HOG cell histogram with **9 unsigned bins** (bin width = $20°$).

**Step 1: Pad the image with zeros** (to handle boundary gradients):

$$
I_{\text{pad}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 20 & 20 & 20 & 20 & 0 \\ 0 & 20 & 20 & 20 & 20 & 0 \\ 0 & 200 & 200 & 200 & 200 & 0 \\ 0 & 200 & 200 & 200 & 200 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute gradients** for the 4 interior pixels (rows 1–2, cols 1–2 in padded image):

For pixel $(1, 1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_x(1,1) &= I_{\text{pad}}(1,2) - I_{\text{pad}}(1,0) = 20 - 0 = 20 \\
G_y(1,1) &= I_{\text{pad}}(2,1) - I_{\text{pad}}(0,1) = 20 - 0 = 20 \\
\vert G \vert &= \sqrt{20^2 + 20^2} = 20\sqrt{2} \approx 28.28 \\
\theta &= \arctan(20/20) = 45°
\end{aligned}
$$

For pixel $(1, 2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_x(1,2) &= I_{\text{pad}}(1,3) - I_{\text{pad}}(1,1) = 20 - 20 = 0 \\
G_y(1,2) &= I_{\text{pad}}(2,2) - I_{\text{pad}}(0,2) = 20 - 0 = 20 \\
\vert G \vert &= \sqrt{0^2 + 20^2} = 20 \\
\theta &= \arctan(20/0) = 90° \quad \text{(vertical edge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For pixel $(2, 1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_x(2,1) &= I_{\text{pad}}(2,2) - I_{\text{pad}}(2,0) = 200 - 0 = 200 \\
G_y(2,1) &= I_{\text{pad}}(3,1) - I_{\text{pad}}(1,1) = 200 - 20 = 180 \\
\vert G \vert &= \sqrt{200^2 + 180^2} = \sqrt{40000 + 32400} = \sqrt{72400} \approx 269.07 \\
\theta &= \arctan(180/200) \approx 41.99° \approx 42°
\end{aligned}
$$

For pixel $(2, 2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_x(2,2) &= I_{\text{pad}}(2,3) - I_{\text{pad}}(2,1) = 200 - 200 = 0 \\
G_y(2,2) &= I_{\text{pad}}(3,2) - I_{\text{pad}}(1,2) = 200 - 20 = 180 \\
\vert G \vert &= \sqrt{0^2 + 180^2} = 180 \\
\theta &= \arctan(180/0) = 90° \quad \text{(vertical edge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Build the 9-bin cell histogram** (bin centers at 0°, 20°, 40°, 60°, 80°, 100°, 120°, 140°, 160°; bin width = 20°). We apply **bilinear interpolation** between adjacent bin centers:

For $\theta = 45°$ at $(1,1)$, $\vert G \vert \approx 28.28$:
- 45° lies between bin 2 (40°) and bin 3 (60°)
- Distance to bin 2: $5°$, distance to bin 3: $15°$
- Weight to bin 2: $15/20 = 0.75$
- Weight to bin 3: $5/20 = 0.25$
- Contribution: bin 2 $\leftarrow 0.75 \times 28.28 = 21.21$, bin 3 $\leftarrow 0.25 \times 28.28 = 7.07$

For $\theta = 90°$ at $(1,2)$, $\vert G \vert = 20$:
- 90° lies exactly at bin 4 center (80°? or 100°? — depends on convention)
- Using centers at 0°, 20°, 40°, 60°, **80°**, 100°, 120°, 140°, 160°: 90° is between 80° and 100°
- Distance to bin 4 (80°): 10°, distance to bin 5 (100°): 10°
- Equal weight: bin 4 $\leftarrow 0.5 \times 20 = 10$, bin 5 $\leftarrow 0.5 \times 20 = 10$

For $\theta \approx 42°$ at $(2,1)$, $\vert G \vert \approx 269.07$:
- 42° lies between bin 2 (40°) and bin 3 (60°)
- Distance to bin 2: $2°$, distance to bin 3: $18°$
- Weight to bin 2: $18/20 = 0.90$
- Weight to bin 3: $2/20 = 0.10$
- Contribution: bin 2 $\leftarrow 0.90 \times 269.07 = 242.16$, bin 3 $\leftarrow 0.10 \times 269.07 = 26.91$

For $\theta = 90°$ at $(2,2)$, $\vert G \vert = 180$:
- Same as pixel (1,2): bin 4 $\leftarrow 0.5 \times 180 = 90$, bin 5 $\leftarrow 0.5 \times 180 = 90$

**Step 4: Aggregate the cell histogram** by summing contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_0 &= 0 \\
h_1 &= 0 \\
h_2 &= 21.21 + 242.16 = 263.37 \\
h_3 &= 7.07 + 26.91 = 33.98 \\
h_4 &= 10 + 90 = 100 \\
h_5 &= 10 + 90 = 100 \\
h_6 &= 0 \\
h_7 &= 0 \\
h_8 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So the cell histogram vector is:

$$
\mathbf{h} = [0,\; 0,\; 263.37,\; 33.98,\; 100,\; 100,\; 0,\; 0,\; 0]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The strongest bins are at 40° and 60° (bin 2 and 3) and around 80°–100° (bins 4 and 5). This makes sense because the edge is roughly horizontal-to-diagonal, producing gradient orientations around 45°–90° (perpendicular to the edge direction). A perfectly horizontal edge would have produced bins near 0°/180° (perpendicular to the row direction), but the small $G_x$ values in our padded image shifted it.

**Step 5: Block normalization (L2-norm, with $\varepsilon = 0.01$)** — assume this cell is the only cell in a 1 × 1 block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert \mathbf{h} \Vert_2^2 &= 0^2 + 0^2 + 263.37^2 + 33.98^2 + 100^2 + 100^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 \\
&= 69364.7 + 1154.6 + 10000 + 10000 \\
&= 90519.3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{h} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2} &= \sqrt{90519.3 + 0.0001} \approx 300.86
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_{\text{norm}} = \frac{1}{300.86} \times [0,\; 0,\; 263.37,\; 33.98,\; 100,\; 100,\; 0,\; 0,\; 0]
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{h}_{\text{norm}} \approx [0.000,\; 0.000,\; 0.875,\; 0.113,\; 0.332,\; 0.332,\; 0.000,\; 0.000,\; 0.000]}
$$

This normalized 9-D vector is the HOG descriptor for this block, ready to be concatenated with other blocks to form the full feature vector for the SVM classifier.

---

### 3.4 Why Bilinear Interpolation in Histogram Voting?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A pixel with $\theta = 41°$ is only 1° away from bin 2 (center 40°) but 19° away from bin 3 (center 60°). A hard assignment would give all its magnitude to bin 2, even though a small rotation in the image could move the pixel into bin 3.
>
> **Solution:** Bilinear interpolation distributes the magnitude *proportionally*:
> - Bin 2 receives $\frac{19}{20} = 0.95$ of the magnitude
> - Bin 3 receives $\frac{1}{20} = 0.05$ of the magnitude
>
> This makes the descriptor **robust to small angular perturbations** and provides a smoother, more discriminative histogram.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HOG Algorithm Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image I x y] --> B[Optional Gamma Correction gamma 0.5]
    B --> C[Compute Gradients Gx and Gy using 1D Centered Masks]
    C --> D[Compute Magnitude G and Orientation theta per pixel]
    D --> E[Divide Image into Cells of size 8x8 pixels]
    E --> F[For Each Cell: Build 9 bin Histogram of theta weighted by G with Bilinear Interpolation]
    F --> G[Group Cells into Blocks of size 2x2 cells]
    G --> H[Concatenate Cell Histograms to form Block Vector v]
    H --> I[Apply Block Normalization L2 norm or L2 hys]
    I --> J[Slide Block across Image with 50 percent overlap]
    J --> K[Concatenate All Normalized Block Vectors]
    K --> L[Final HOG Feature Vector Dimension 3780 for 64x128 window]
    L --> M[Feed to Linear SVM Classifier]
    M --> N[Output: Object Present or Absent with Confidence Score]

    style A fill:E3F2FD,stroke:1976D2,color:000000
    style B fill:F3E5F5,stroke:7B1FA2,color:000000
    style C fill:FFF3E0,stroke:F57C00,color:000000
    style D fill:FFF3E0,stroke:F57C00,color:000000
    style E fill:E8F5E9,stroke:388E3C,color:000000
    style F fill:E8F5E9,stroke:388E3C,color:000000
    style G fill:FCE4EC,stroke:C2185B,color:000000
    style H fill:FCE4EC,stroke:C2185B,color:000000
    style I fill:FFEBEE,stroke:D32F2F,color:000000
    style J fill:E1F5FE,stroke:0288D1,color:000000
    style K fill:EDE7F6,stroke:512DA8,color:000000
    style L fill:EDE7F6,stroke:512DA8,color:000000
    style M fill:FFFDE7,stroke:F9A825,color:000000
    style N fill:C8E6C9,stroke:2E7D32,color:000000
```

### 4.2 Block-Cell-Block Sliding Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ImageGrid[Detection Window 64x128]
        direction LR
        subgraph Block1[Block 1 at position 0 0]
            C1A[Cell 1A 8x8] --- C1B[Cell 1B 8x8]
            C1C[Cell 1C 8x8] --- C1D[Cell 1D 8x8]
        end
        subgraph Block2[Block 2 at position 8 0 50 percent overlap]
            C2A[Cell 2A 8x8] --- C2B[Cell 2B 8x8]
            C2C[Cell 2C 8x8] --- C2D[Cell 2D 8x8]
        end
        Block1 -.overlaps.-> Block2
    end

    style Block1 fill:BBDEFB,stroke:1565C0,color:000000
    style Block2 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:2E7D32,color:000000
    style ImageGrid fill:FAFAFA,stroke:424242,color:000000
```

### 4.3 HOG Cell Histogram Voting Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pixel with Magnitude G and Orientation theta] --> Q{theta in degrees}
    Q --> R[Compute Distance to Adjacent Bin Centers]
    R --> S[Distribute Magnitude G to Two Adjacent Bins]
    S --> T[Bin Left gets fraction d2 by w Magnitude]
    S --> U[Bin Right gets fraction d1 by w Magnitude]
    T --> V[Update Cell Histogram hk]
    U --> V
    V --> W[Repeat for All Pixels in Cell]
    W --> X[Cell Histogram h with 9 bins ready for Block Normalization]
```

### 4.4 HOG-Based Pedestrian Detection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Input Video Frame] --> Resize[Resize to Detection Window 64x128]
    Resize --> HogFeat[Extract HOG Feature Vector 3780 dimensions]
    HogFeat --> SVMClassify[Linear SVM Decision Function]
    SVMClassify --> Decision{Is Pedestrian Present?}
    Decision -->|Yes Score above threshold| BoundingBox[Draw Bounding Box]
    Decision -->|No| Reject[Reject Window]
    BoundingBox --> NMS[Non Maximum Suppression to Merge Overlapping Detections]
    NMS --> Output[Final Detection Result]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define the **Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG)** feature descriptor. Mention any two key parameters used in its computation.

**Model Answer:**
> HOG is a feature descriptor used for object detection that captures the **distribution of gradient orientations** in localized image regions. The image is divided into small **cells** (typically 8×8 pixels), and a **1-D histogram of gradient orientations** is built for each cell, with the gradient magnitudes as weights. Cells are grouped into larger **blocks** (typically 2×2 cells), and the block histograms are **normalized** for illumination invariance. The normalized block descriptors are concatenated to form the final feature vector.
>
> **Two key parameters:** (i) Cell size (e.g., 8×8 pixels) and (ii) Number of orientation bins (e.g., 9 bins for unsigned gradients over 0°–180°). [3 marks]

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2.** Why is **block normalization** essential in the HOG descriptor? List any two normalization schemes.

**Model Answer:**
> Block normalization is essential to achieve **invariance to local illumination and contrast changes**. Raw gradient magnitudes are highly sensitive to lighting variations; a strong edge in dim light may have the same magnitude as a weak edge in bright light. Normalization rescales the block descriptor to a unit scale, making comparisons robust to such variations.
>
> **Two normalization schemes:** (i) **L2-norm:** $\mathbf{v}' = \mathbf{v} / \sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2}$, and (ii) **L1-norm:** $\mathbf{v}' = \mathbf{v} / (\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_1 + \varepsilon)$. The **L2-hys** variant (L2 followed by clipping to 0.2) is also widely used. [3 marks]

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A.(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **step-by-step algorithm for computing the HOG feature descriptor** for object detection. Illustrate with a neat block diagram.

**Model Solution:**

The HOG algorithm consists of **five sequential stages**:

**Step 1: Global Normalization (Optional)**
- Apply gamma correction with $\gamma = 0.5$ (square-root compression) to reduce illumination effects. Modern implementations often skip this step.

**Step 2: Gradient Computation**
- Compute gradients $G_x(x, y) = I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)$ and $G_y(x, y) = I(x, y+1) - I(x, y-1)$ using 1-D centered masks.
- Compute magnitude: $\vert G \vert = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$
- Compute orientation: $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$

**Step 3: Spatial Binning — Cell Histograms**
- Divide the image into **8×8 pixel cells**.
- For each cell, build a **9-bin histogram** of gradient orientations (unsigned, 0°–180°).
- Each pixel's magnitude is **bilinearly interpolated** into two adjacent bin centers.

**Step 4: Block Normalization**
- Group cells into **2×2 cell blocks (16×16 pixels)**.
- Concatenate the cell histograms to form a **36-D vector**.
- Apply **L2-hys normalization** (or L2-norm) to the block vector.
- Blocks **overlap with 50% stride**, so each cell contributes to multiple blocks.

**Step 5: Feature Vector Assembly & Classification**
- Concatenate all normalized block vectors → final HOG feature vector (e.g., 3780-D for a 64×128 window).
- Feed the vector to a **trained linear SVM** for classification (object present or not).
- Use a **sliding window** over the image to detect multiple instances of the object.

**Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 above for the full pipeline diagram.)

**Key Result:** Dalal & Triggs achieved a **detection rate of nearly 100%** with very low false positives on the INRIA pedestrian dataset using these parameters. [7 marks]

---

**Q-A.(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **normalization schemes** used in HOG and explain how **L2-hys normalization** is implemented.

**Model Solution:**

Block normalization compensates for **local contrast and illumination variations**. Four common schemes are:

**1. L2-Norm (Euclidean Normalization):**

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L2}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2}}
$$

This is the most commonly used scheme. It rescales the vector to have unit L2-norm.

**2. L1-Norm (Manhattan Normalization):**

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L1}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_1 + \varepsilon}
$$

Sum-based normalization, simpler but slightly less effective.

**3. L1-Sqrt:**

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L1-sqrt}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_1 + \varepsilon}}
$$

Square-root applied after L1 normalization.

**4. L2-Hys (L2 with Hysteresis Clipping) — Best Performer:**

The L2-hys scheme is implemented in three sub-steps:

**Sub-step (i):** Apply L2-norm:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L2}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2}}
$$

**Sub-step (ii):** Clip each component at a maximum value of 0.2:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{clip}}[i] = \min\big(\mathbf{v}_{\text{L2}}[i],\; 0.2\big)
$$

**Sub-step (iii):** Re-normalize the clipped vector using L2-norm:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{L2-hys}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}_{\text{clip}}}{\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{clip}} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2}}
$$

> **Why clipping at 0.2?** It limits the influence of any single dominant bin, which could otherwise be caused by lighting artifacts, shadows, or image noise. The descriptor becomes more robust to outliers.

**Performance comparison (Dalal–Triggs results):**

| Normalization | Miss Rate at $10^{-4}$ FPPW |
|---------------|------------------------------|
| L2-norm       | ~0.10 |
| L2-hys        | **~0.085 (best)** |
| L1-norm       | ~0.12 |
| L1-sqrt       | ~0.11 |
| No normalization | ~0.40 |

[7 marks]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q-B.(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the complete pipeline of **HOG-based pedestrian detection** as proposed by Dalal and Triggs. What role does the **linear SVM** play in this pipeline?

**Model Solution:**

The Dalal–Triggs pedestrian detection system works as follows:

**1. Training Phase:**
- **Positive samples:** Cropped 64×128 images of pedestrians (e.g., from INRIA dataset).
- **Negative samples:** 64×128 patches from images **not containing pedestrians** (e.g., landscapes, roads, buildings).
- For each sample, compute the **HOG feature vector (3780-D)**.
- Train a **linear SVM** to find the optimal hyperplane that separates positive (pedestrian) and negative (background) samples in this 3780-D feature space.

**2. Detection Phase:**
- **Sliding window:** Slide a 64×128 window across the input image (and across multiple scales via image pyramid).
- For each window position, compute the HOG feature vector.
- Feed the vector to the **trained SVM** → get a score.
- If the score exceeds a threshold, declare a pedestrian detection at that location.
- Apply **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** to merge overlapping detections.

**3. Role of Linear SVM:**
- The HOG feature vector is high-dimensional (3780-D) and the classes (pedestrian vs. background) are approximately linearly separable.
- A **linear SVM** finds the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ that maximally separates the two classes.
- The decision function is: $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$.
- The HOG descriptor's strength is that it produces features that **linear classifiers can use effectively** — this avoids the computational cost of kernel SVMs.

**4. Multi-Scale Detection:**
- Build a Gaussian image pyramid (multi-resolution versions of the input).
- Apply the sliding window at each scale.
- Merge detections via NMS.

**5. Performance:**
- On the INRIA dataset, this system achieved a **detection rate of ~90% at $10^{-4}$ False Positives Per Window (FPPW)**, a major breakthrough at the time. [7 marks]

---

**Q-B.(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain how **gradient magnitude and orientation** are computed in the HOG descriptor. Why are **unsigned gradients (0°–180°)** preferred over signed gradients (0°–360°)?

**Model Solution:**

**Gradient Computation Example:**

Consider pixel $(x, y) = (3, 4)$ in an image with neighbors:

$$
I(2, 4) = 100, \quad I(4, 4) = 150, \quad I(3, 3) = 80, \quad I(3, 5) = 120
$$

**Horizontal gradient:**

$$
G_x(3, 4) = I(4, 4) - I(2, 4) = 150 - 100 = 50
$$

**Vertical gradient:**

$$
G_y(3, 4) = I(3, 5) - I(3, 3) = 120 - 80 = 40
$$

**Magnitude:**

$$
\vert G \vert = \sqrt{50^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 1600} = \sqrt{4100} \approx 64.03
$$

**Orientation:**

$$
\theta = \arctan(40 / 50) = \arctan(0.8) \approx 38.66°
$$

**Why Unsigned Gradients are Preferred:**

For a **vertical edge** (dark-to-light transition from left to right), the gradient vector can point either to the **right** ($0°$) or to the **left** ($180°$) depending on the contrast polarity (which side is bright). However, **for object recognition, the edge's existence and location are what matter — not its polarity**. By wrapping angles to $[0°, 180°]$, the descriptor:

1. **Halves the number of bins** (9 instead of 18), reducing feature dimensionality and computational cost.
2. **Doubles the training samples per bin** (since a $30°$ and $210°$ gradient are now both mapped to the $30°$ bin), improving statistical robustness.
3. **Improves detection accuracy** — Dalal & Triggs showed that unsigned gradients reduce the miss rate by **~5–10%** on pedestrian detection compared to signed gradients.

For pedestrian silhouettes, a head-to-toe vertical edge has the same orientation whether the person is wearing white-on-black or black-on-white clothing — HOG captures this invariance. [7 marks]

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing gradient masks:** Students often write $G_x = I(x+1, y) - I(x, y)$ (forward difference) instead of the **centered difference** $G_x = I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)$. The HOG paper specifies the **centered mask $[-1, 0, +1]$** — using a non-centered mask will cost marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon$ term in normalization:** When writing the L2-normalization formula, always include the small $\varepsilon$ inside the square root: $\mathbf{v}/\sqrt{\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2^2 + \varepsilon^2}$. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Wrong formula for L2-hys:** Students sometimes write L2-hys as a single step. It is **three distinct sub-steps**: L2-normalize → clip at 0.2 → re-normalize. Examiners expect to see all three.
>
> 4. **Conflating cells and blocks:** A **cell** is 8×8 pixels (one histogram). A **block** is 2×2 cells = 16×16 pixels (a normalized concatenated vector). Don't mix these up.
>
> 5. **Forgetting that the feature vector is the INPUT to SVM, not the OUTPUT.** HOG is a **descriptor**; SVM is the **classifier**. The pipeline is: Image → HOG features → SVM → detection.
>
> 6. **In numerical problems, failing to wrap angles to [0°, 180°] for unsigned gradients.** A gradient at $-45°$ (or $315°$) should be wrapped to $135°$ when using 9 unsigned bins.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HOG for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - [ ] HOG = **Histogram of Oriented Gradients**, introduced by **Dalal & Triggs (INRIA, 2005)** for pedestrian detection.
> - [ ] **Pipeline:** Image → (optional gamma) → Gradients ($G_x$, $G_y$) → Magnitude & Orientation → Cell histograms → Block normalization → Concatenate → SVM.
> - [ ] **Standard parameters:** Cell = 8×8 px, Block = 2×2 cells (16×16 px), Bins = 9 (unsigned, 0°–180°), Block stride = 8 px (50% overlap).
> - [ ] **Gradient computation:** $G_x = I(x+1, y) - I(x-1, y)$ and $G_y = I(x, y+1) - I(x, y-1)$ using **1-D centered masks $[-1, 0, +1]$**.
> - [ ] **Magnitude:** $\vert G \vert = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$. **Orientation:** $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$.
> - [ ] **Bilinear interpolation** is used when voting pixel magnitudes into the cell histogram bins.
> - [ ] **Block normalization schemes:** L2-norm, L2-hys (best), L1-norm, L1-sqrt. **L2-hys** = L2-normalize + clip to 0.2 + re-normalize.
> - [ ] **L2-hys formula:** $\mathbf{v}_{\text{clip}}[i] = \min(\mathbf{v}_{\text{L2}}[i], 0.2)$, then re-normalize via L2.
> - [ ] **Feature vector dimension for 64×128 window:** 105 blocks × 36 features/block = **3780-D**.
> - [ ] **SVM** (typically linear) is the classifier used with HOG features.
> - [ ] **Unsigned gradients (0°–180°)** are preferred over signed (0°–360°) for human detection.
> - [ ] HOG is **invariant to local illumination and contrast** due to block normalization.
> - [ ] HOG is widely used in **pedestrian detection, ADAS, surveillance, and wildlife monitoring**.
> - [ ] **Limitations:** Not rotation-invariant, sensitive to occlusion, outperformed by deep CNNs (YOLO, Faster R-CNN) on complex scenes.

---
